original_problem:
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?
claim_verified:
After the routine patches above, the proof establishes the claimed partial theorem: for a finite simple modular matroid \(M\) of rank \(r\) with an order-reversing involution on its lattice of flats, every rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient satisfying the stated tropical Plücker and circuit-incidence relations is \(\infty\) on \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-sets and is constant on bases of each rank-\(k\) flat. The construction
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
\]
for \(M\)-independent \((r-k)\)-sets \(J\), and \(\infty\) otherwise, gives a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\), descends to projective valuated matroids, is involutive, and sends the flat quotient \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) to \(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}\). It does not prove order-reversal for arbitrary inclusions in the relative Dressian.
solution_verified:
### Theorem proved

Let \(M\) be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(r\) on ground set \(E\). Assume that its lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Fix \(0\le k\le r\). Let
\[
p:\binom{E}{k}\to \mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}
\]
be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\), meaning that \(p\) is not identically \(\infty\), satisfies the tropical Plücker relations, and satisfies the circuit-incidence relations
\[
\min_{c\in C} p_{A\cup\{c\}}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\). We use the convention \(p_T=\infty\) whenever \(|T|\ne k\). For \(k=0\), there are no circuit-incidence relations.

Then:

1. If \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). If \(I,I'\in\binom{E}{k}\) are \(M\)-independent and \(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I')\), then \(p_I=p_{I'}\). Thus \(p\) has a well-defined value \(\bar p(F)\) on every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), namely the common value on the bases of \(F\).

2. Define \(p^\dagger\) on \((r-k)\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

3. The operation \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) descends to projective valuated matroids, is involutive, and satisfies
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
for every representative \(p\).

4. For every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), let \(p^F\) be the \(0/\infty\)-valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
on \(E\). Then \(p^F\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\), and
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

No assertion is made here that \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) reverses arbitrary inclusions of tropical linear spaces.

---

### Proof

Write \(\overline{\mathbb R}=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). A finite family in \(\overline{\mathbb R}\) has no unique finite minimizer if either all entries are \(\infty\), or the smallest finite value occurs at least twice.

Because \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an order-reversing lattice involution,
\[
(F\vee G)^\perp=F^\perp\wedge G^\perp,\qquad
(F\wedge G)^\perp=F^\perp\vee G^\perp.
\]
Also
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]
Indeed, the anti-isomorphism sends maximal chains in \([\widehat 0,F]\) to maximal chains in \([F^\perp,\widehat 1]\).

---

#### 1. Two elementary facts about modular matroids

We identify elements of \(E\) with atoms of \(\mathcal L(M)\), since \(M\) is simple.

**Lemma 1.1.** Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular matroid. Suppose \(S\) is a set of atoms such that whenever \(a,b\in S\), every atom on the rank-two flat \(a\vee b\) also belongs to \(S\). Then \(S\) is the set of atoms of a flat of \(N\).

**Proof.** Let \(F=\bigvee_{a\in S} a\). It suffices to show that every atom \(x\le F\) lies in \(S\). Choose atoms \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in S\) minimally such that
\[
x\le a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_m.
\]
By minimality, no \(a_i\) lies in the join of the others; hence \(a_1,\dots,a_m\) are independent atoms, so
\[
\operatorname{rk}(a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_m)=m.
\]

If \(m=1\), then \(x=a_1\in S\). Assume \(m\ge 2\). If \(x=a_m\), then \(x\in S\), so assume \(x\ne a_m\). Put
\[
P=a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_{m-1},\qquad L=a_m\vee x.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}P=m-1\), \(\operatorname{rk}L=2\), and
\[
P\vee L=P\vee a_m.
\]
By modularity,
\[
\operatorname{rk}(P\wedge L)
=
\operatorname{rk}P+\operatorname{rk}L-\operatorname{rk}(P\vee L)
=
(m-1)+2-m
=
1.
\]
Thus \(b:=P\wedge L\) is an atom. Since \(b\le P\), induction on \(m\) gives \(b\in S\). Also \(a_m\in S\). Because \(x\) lies on the line \(b\vee a_m=L\), the line-closedness assumption gives \(x\in S\). Hence \(S\) is closed. ∎

**Lemma 1.2.** Let \(N\) be a finite simple matroid with atom set \(A(N)\). Let
\[
w:A(N)\to \overline{\mathbb R}
\]
be not identically \(\infty\). Then \(w\) satisfies the tropical circuit condition
\[
\min_{x\in C} w_x
\]
with no unique finite minimizer for every circuit \(C\) of \(N\) if and only if, for every real \(\tau\), the upper level set
\[
A_\tau=\{x\in A(N): w_x\ge \tau\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

**Proof.** Suppose some \(A_\tau\) is not a flat. Then there exists \(e\notin A_\tau\) with \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(A_\tau)\). Hence some circuit \(C\subseteq A_\tau\cup\{e\}\) contains \(e\). Since \(w_e<\tau\le w_x\) for all \(x\in C\setminus\{e\}\), the circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimizer.

Conversely, suppose a circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimizer \(e\). Choose a real number \(\tau\) with
\[
w_e<\tau\le w_x
\]
for every \(x\in C\setminus\{e\}\); this is possible even if all other values are \(\infty\). Then \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq A_\tau\), but \(e\notin A_\tau\) and \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(C\setminus\{e\})\). Thus \(A_\tau\) is not a flat. ∎

---

#### 2. Flat-constancy of valuated quotients

Let \(p:\binom{E}{k}\to\overline{\mathbb R}\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

If \(k=0\), then the only \(0\)-subset is \(\varnothing\), which is independent, so the claims below are immediate. Assume \(k\ge 1\).

Let \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) be \(M\)-dependent. Choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and an element \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). For every \(x\in C\setminus\{c\}\), the set \(A\cup\{x\}=A\) has size \(k-1\), so by convention
\[
p_{A\cup\{x\}}=\infty.
\]
The only possibly finite term in the circuit-incidence relation for \(C\) and \(A\) is \(p_I=p_{A\cup\{c\}}\). Therefore the relation forces \(p_I=\infty\).

Now suppose
\[
I=A\cup\{a\},\qquad I'=A\cup\{b\}
\]
are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I').
\]
Then \(a\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{b\})\) but \(a\notin\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\). Hence there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing \(a\). This circuit must also contain \(b\), because otherwise it would be contained in the independent set \(A\cup\{a\}\). Applying the circuit-incidence relation to \(C\) and \(A\), all terms are \(\infty\) except possibly
\[
p_{A\cup\{a\}},\qquad p_{A\cup\{b\}}.
\]
Thus those two values are equal.

Finally, any two bases of a rank-\(k\) flat \(F\) are connected by a sequence of single-element basis exchanges inside \(M|F\). The preceding adjacent-basis argument shows that \(p\) has the same value on all bases of \(F\).

Therefore \(\bar p(F)\) is well-defined for every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). For flats of rank different from \(k\), set
\[
\bar p(F):=\infty.
\]

---

#### 3. Definition and nontriviality of \(p^\dagger\)

Let
\[
\ell=r-k.
\]
Define \(p^\dagger\) on \(\ell\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

This function is not identically \(\infty\). Since \(p\) is not identically \(\infty\), there exists \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) with \(p_I<\infty\). By the previous section, \(I\) is \(M\)-independent. Let \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Then \(F^\perp\) has rank \(\ell\). Choose a basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p\bigl((F^\perp)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I
<
\infty.
\]

It remains to prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker and circuit-incidence relations.

---

#### 4. A rank-three matching lemma

**Lemma 4.1.** Let \(P\) be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(3\). Let
\[
w:A(P)\to\overline{\mathbb R}
\]
be a tropical point of \(P\), i.e. \(w\) is not identically \(\infty\) and satisfies the tropical circuit condition on every circuit of \(P\). Extend \(w\) to all flats of \(P\) by setting \(w(F)=\infty\) whenever \(F\) is not an atom.

For any four hyperplanes \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) of \(P\), the minimum of
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),
\]
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),
\]
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(L_1=L_2\), then \(L_1\wedge L_2=L_1\) is not an atom, so
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_2)=\infty.
\]
The other two sums are equal:
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4)
=
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3).
\]
Thus the claim holds. Hence assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

Normalize \(w\) by subtracting its finite minimum, so \(\min w=0\). By Lemma 1.2, every positive upper level set
\[
A_\tau=\{x:w(x)\ge \tau\},\qquad \tau>0,
\]
is a proper flat of \(P\). These flats are nested. In a rank-three simple matroid, every nonempty proper flat is either an atom or a hyperplane. Therefore the positive upper level sets form a chain consisting of at most one hyperplane \(B\) and at most one atom \(a\le B\). Allowing degenerate cases, we may choose a flag \(a\le B\) and numbers
\[
0\le \beta\le \alpha\le \infty
\]
such that
\[
w(x)=
\begin{cases}
0,& x\notin B,\\
\beta,& x\in B\setminus\{a\},\\
\alpha,& x=a.
\end{cases}
\]

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). For each \(i\), set
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
This is an atom. For \(i\ne j\), the atom \(L_i\wedge L_j\) lies in \(B\) if and only if \(y_i=y_j\), in which case
\[
L_i\wedge L_j=y_i.
\]
Thus the cost of pairing \(i\) and \(j\) is \(0\) if \(y_i\ne y_j\), and is \(w(y_i)\) if \(y_i=y_j\).

The three displayed sums are therefore the three perfect-matching costs on four colored objects \(y_1,y_2,y_3,y_4\), where a matched pair contributes only when the two colors agree. If all four colors are distinct, all three costs are \(0\). If exactly one pair agrees, the two matchings avoiding that pair tie for the minimum. If the multiplicities are \(2+2\), the two cross-matchings tie for the minimum. If the multiplicities are \(3+1\), all three matchings have the same cost. If all four colors are equal, all three matchings again have the same cost. In all cases there is no unique finite minimum.

Now suppose one hyperplane equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). Since the \(L_i\) are distinct, \(L_2,L_3,L_4\ne B\). Put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B,\qquad j=2,3,4,
\]
and write \(c(y)=w(y)\) for atoms \(y\le B\). For atoms \(y,z\le B\), define
\[
g(y,z)=
\begin{cases}
c(y),& y=z,\\
0,& y\ne z.
\end{cases}
\]
The three sums are
\[
c(y_2)+g(y_3,y_4),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+g(y_2,y_4),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+g(y_2,y_3).
\]

If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, then at least two of them are different from \(a\), so at least two of the three sums are equal to \(\beta\), and the third is at least \(\beta\). If exactly two of the \(y_j\) are equal, then the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those two equal atoms have the same cost and are minimal. If all three are equal, all three sums are equal. Again, no unique finite minimum occurs. ∎

---

#### 5. A local dual Plücker relation

**Lemma 5.1.** Let \(Y\) be a flat of \(M\) of rank \(k+2\). Let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Put
\[
K_{ij}=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
With the convention \(\bar p(F)=\infty\) unless \(\operatorname{rk}(F)=k\), the minimum of
\[
\bar p(K_{12})+\bar p(K_{34}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{13})+\bar p(K_{24}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{14})+\bar p(K_{23})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \(K_{12}=H_1\) has rank \(k+1\), so \(\bar p(K_{12})=\infty\). The other two sums are equal. Hence the result follows.

Assume now that \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct. Since \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular, two distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\). Thus every \(K_{ij}\) has rank \(k\).

Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting with a hyperplane lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\operatorname{rk}Z\ge k-2.
\]
Since \(H_1\wedge H_2\) has rank \(k\), we also have \(\operatorname{rk}Z\le k\). There are three cases.

**Case 1: \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k\).**  
Then every \(K_{ij}\) contains \(Z\) and has rank \(k\), so every \(K_{ij}=Z\). All three sums are equal.

**Case 2: \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\).**  
For each \(i\), define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i} H_j.
\]
We claim \(\operatorname{rk}T_i=k-1\). Indeed, \(T_i\) is obtained by intersecting two distinct hyperplanes, giving rank \(k\), and then intersecting with a third hyperplane, which lowers rank by at most one. Thus \(\operatorname{rk}T_i\ge k-1\). On the other hand \(T_i\le H_j\wedge H_m\) for distinct \(j,m\ne i\), so \(\operatorname{rk}T_i\le k\). If \(\operatorname{rk}T_i=k\), then intersecting \(T_i\) with \(H_i\) would give rank at least \(k-1\), contradicting \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\). Hence \(\operatorname{rk}T_i=k-1\).

Let \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\). Then \(T_m,T_n\subseteq K_{ij}\). They are distinct: if \(T_m=T_n\), then this common flat would be contained in all four \(H_i\), hence in \(Z\), impossible because \(\operatorname{rk}T_m=k-1>\operatorname{rk}Z\). Thus \(T_m\) and \(T_n\) are distinct rank-\((k-1)\) flats containing \(Z\) inside the rank-\(k\) flat \(K_{ij}\), so
\[
T_m\vee T_n=K_{ij}.
\]

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\). For each \(i\), choose
\[
t_i\in T_i\setminus Z
\]
so that \(B\cup\{t_i\}\) spans \(T_i\). Since \(T_i\wedge T_j=Z\) for \(i\ne j\), the elements \(t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\) are distinct. Moreover, for \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), the set
\[
B\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is a basis of \(K_{ij}\). Therefore
\[
p_{B\cup\{t_m,t_n\}}=\bar p(K_{ij}).
\]

Apply the tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) to the \((k-2)\)-set \(B\) and the four elements \(t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\). The three resulting sums are precisely
\[
\bar p(K_{12})+\bar p(K_{34}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{13})+\bar p(K_{24}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{14})+\bar p(K_{23}).
\]
Thus the desired relation holds.

**Case 3: \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-1\).**  
The interval \([Z,Y]\) is the lattice of flats of a finite simple modular rank-three matroid \(P\), namely the simplification of \((M|Y)/Z\). Its atoms are the rank-\(k\) flats of \(M\) between \(Z\) and \(Y\), and its hyperplanes are the rank-\((k+1)\) flats between \(Z\) and \(Y\).

Define
\[
w(X)=\bar p(X)
\]
for every atom \(X\) of \(P\). If all values \(w(X)\) are \(\infty\), then all six \(\bar p(K_{ij})\) are \(\infty\), and the desired relation is trivial.

Assume \(w\) is not identically \(\infty\). We show that \(w\) is a tropical point of \(P\). Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(P\). Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\). For each atom \(X\in D\), choose an element
\[
e_X\in X\setminus Z.
\]
Because \(D\) is dependent in the simplification of \((M|Y)/Z\), the set
\[
B\cup\{e_X:X\in D\}
\]
is dependent in \(M\). Since every proper subset of \(D\) is independent in \(P\), every proper subset of the representatives is independent over \(Z\). Therefore any \(M\)-circuit contained in \(B\cup\{e_X:X\in D\}\) must contain all representatives \(e_X\).

Choose such an \(M\)-circuit \(C\). Applying the circuit-incidence relation for \(p\) with the \((k-1)\)-set \(B\), the terms coming from elements of \(C\cap B\) are \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{B\cup\{e_X\}}=\bar p(X)=w(X).
\]
Hence
\[
\min_{X\in D} w(X)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(w\) is a tropical point of \(P\).

Now apply Lemma 4.1 to the four hyperplanes \(H_1,\dots,H_4\) of \(P\). Their pairwise meets are precisely the atoms \(K_{ij}\), so Lemma 4.1 gives exactly the claimed relation. ∎

---

#### 6. Tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the rank-\(\ell\) tropical Plücker relations, where \(\ell=r-k\).

If \(\ell<2\), there are no Plücker relations. Assume \(\ell\ge 2\). Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(\ell-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must show that the minimum of
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,b\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{c,d\}},
\]
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,c\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{b,d\}},
\]
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,d\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{b,c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every set \(S\cup\{x,y\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all six terms are \(\infty\). Thus assume \(S\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\), say \(a\), lies in \(X\), then every set \(S\cup\{a,x\}\) is \(M\)-dependent. Each Plücker sum contains exactly one such term, so all three sums are \(\infty\). Hence we may assume
\[
a,b,c,d\notin X.
\]

Set
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{rk}X=\ell-2=r-k-2\), we have \(\operatorname{rk}Y=k+2\). For \(x\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_x=(X\vee x)^\perp.
\]
Because \(x\notin X\), the flat \(X\vee x\) has rank \(\ell-1\), so \(H_x\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\).

For any pair \(x,y\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), if \(S\cup\{x,y\}\) is \(M\)-independent, then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(S\cup\{x,y\})=X\vee x\vee y,
\]
and therefore
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(S\cup\{x,y\})^\perp
=
(X\vee x\vee y)^\perp
=
H_x\wedge H_y.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}=\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y).
\]

If \(S\cup\{x,y\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, then since \(x,y\notin X\), we have
\[
X\vee x=X\vee y.
\]
Therefore \(H_x=H_y\), so \(H_x\wedge H_y=H_x\) has rank \(k+1\), and hence
\[
\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y)=\infty=p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}.
\]
Consequently, in all cases,
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}=\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y).
\]

The desired Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is therefore precisely Lemma 5.1 applied to the four hyperplanes \(H_a,H_b,H_c,H_d\) of \(Y\). Hence \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations.

---

#### 7. A codimension-one polarity lemma

**Lemma 7.1.** Let \(N\) and \(N'\) be finite simple modular matroids of the same rank \(n\ge 1\). Suppose
\[
\delta:\mathcal L(N')\to\mathcal L(N)
\]
is an order-reversing lattice isomorphism. Let \(q\) be a rank-\((n-1)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(N\). For every hyperplane \(H\) of \(N\), let \(\bar q(H)\) be the common value of \(q\) on bases of \(H\). Define
\[
u_a=\bar q(\delta(a))
\]
for every atom \(a\) of \(N'\). Then \(u\) is a tropical point of \(N'\).

**Proof.** Since \(q\) is not identically \(\infty\), there is an \((n-1)\)-subset \(I\) with \(q_I<\infty\). By flat-constancy, \(I\) is independent in \(N\), and \(H=\operatorname{cl}_N(I)\) is a hyperplane with \(\bar q(H)<\infty\). Since \(\delta\) maps atoms of \(N'\) bijectively to hyperplanes of \(N\), \(u\) is not identically \(\infty\).

By Lemma 1.2, it suffices to prove that every upper level set
\[
A_\tau=\{a\in A(N'):u_a\ge \tau\}
\]
is a flat of \(N'\). By Lemma 1.1, it is enough to prove that \(A_\tau\) is line-closed.

Let \(a,b\in A_\tau\), and let \(c\) be an atom on the line \(a\vee b\). If \(c=a\) or \(c=b\), there is nothing to prove, so assume \(a,b,c\) are distinct. Then
\[
Z=\delta(a\vee b)
\]
has rank \(n-2\), and the three hyperplanes \(\delta(a),\delta(b),\delta(c)\) of \(N\) all contain \(Z\).

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\). For \(s\in\{a,b,c\}\), choose
\[
x_s\in \delta(s)\setminus Z
\]
so that \(B\cup\{x_s\}\) is a basis of \(\delta(s)\). In the rank-two contraction \(N/Z\), the elements \(x_a,x_b,x_c\) lie in three distinct rank-one flats, hence form a circuit. Equivalently, \(B\cup\{x_a,x_b,x_c\}\) is dependent in \(N\), while \(B\) together with any two of the \(x_s\) is independent. Thus there is an \(N\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq B\cup\{x_a,x_b,x_c\}
\]
containing all three elements \(x_a,x_b,x_c\).

Apply the circuit-incidence relation for \(q\) with the \((n-2)\)-set \(B\). The terms coming from elements of \(C\cap B\) are \(\infty\), and
\[
q_{B\cup\{x_s\}}=\bar q(\delta(s))=u_s
\]
for \(s=a,b,c\). Therefore
\[
\min(u_a,u_b,u_c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Since \(u_a,u_b\ge \tau\), if \(u_c<\tau\), then \(u_c\) would be the unique finite minimum among \(u_a,u_b,u_c\). This is impossible. Hence \(u_c\ge \tau\), so \(c\in A_\tau\). Thus \(A_\tau\) is line-closed, hence a flat. Therefore \(u\) is a tropical point of \(N'\). ∎

---

#### 8. Circuit-incidence relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the circuit-incidence relations for a rank-\(\ell\) quotient, where \(\ell=r-k\).

If \(\ell=0\), there are no circuit-incidence relations. Assume \(\ell\ge 1\). Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit and let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(\ell-1\). We must show that
\[
\min_{c\in C} p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup\{c\}\) is either dependent or has the wrong size, so all terms are \(\infty\). Thus assume \(A\) is \(M\)-independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}X=\ell-1\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}Y=k+1\).

For \(c\in X\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\) is dependent or has size \(\ell-1\), so
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}=\infty.
\]
For \(c\notin X\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\) is independent, and
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp).
\]

Let \(q\) be the restriction of \(p\) to \(k\)-subsets of \(Y\):
\[
q_I=p_I,\qquad I\subseteq Y,\ |I|=k.
\]
If \(q\) is identically \(\infty\), then \(\bar p(H)=\infty\) for every rank-\(k\) flat \(H\subseteq Y\). Hence all terms \(p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}\) are \(\infty\), and the desired relation holds.

Assume \(q\) is not identically \(\infty\). Then \(q\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M|Y\), because the Plücker and circuit-incidence relations are inherited from \(p\).

Let \(N'\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its lattice of flats is the interval \([X,E]\). The lattice of flats of \(M|Y\) is the interval \([\widehat 0,Y]\). The map
\[
Z\longmapsto Z^\perp
\]
is an order-reversing isomorphism
\[
[X,E]\longrightarrow [\widehat 0,Y].
\]
By Lemma 7.1, the function \(u\) on atoms of \(N'\) defined by
\[
u_{X\vee c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
\]
is a tropical point of \(N'\). For \(c\notin X\), let \(\alpha(c)\) denote the atom of \(N'\) represented by \(c\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}=u_{\alpha(c)}.
\]

Suppose, toward a contradiction, that the values
\[
\{p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}:c\in C\}
\]
have a unique finite minimum at \(c_0\in C\). Then \(c_0\notin X\). Since \(C\) is a circuit of \(M\),
\[
c_0\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus\{c_0\})
\subseteq
\operatorname{cl}_M\bigl(X\cup(C\setminus X\setminus\{c_0\})\bigr).
\]
Thus, in the contraction \(M/X\), the element \(c_0\) lies in the closure of the images of
\[
R=C\setminus X\setminus\{c_0\}.
\]

If some element \(d\in R\) is parallel to \(c_0\) in \(M/X\), then \(\alpha(d)=\alpha(c_0)\), so
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{d\}}=u_{\alpha(d)}=u_{\alpha(c_0)}=p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c_0\}},
\]
contradicting uniqueness. Hence no element of \(R\) represents \(\alpha(c_0)\).

Passing to the simplification \(N'\), we then have
\[
\alpha(c_0)\in \operatorname{cl}_{N'}(\{\alpha(d):d\in R\}).
\]
Therefore there is a circuit \(D\) of \(N'\) such that
\[
\alpha(c_0)\in D\subseteq \{\alpha(c_0)\}\cup\{\alpha(d):d\in R\}.
\]
For every \(\beta\in D\setminus\{\alpha(c_0)\}\), choose \(d\in R\) with \(\beta=\alpha(d)\). By uniqueness of the finite minimum at \(c_0\),
\[
u_\beta=p^\dagger_{A\cup\{d\}}>p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c_0\}}=u_{\alpha(c_0)}
\]
or \(u_\beta=\infty\). Thus \(u\) has a unique finite minimum on the circuit \(D\), contradicting that \(u\) is a tropical point of \(N'\).

Therefore the circuit-incidence relation holds for \(p^\dagger\). Combining this with the Plücker relations proved above, \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

---

#### 9. Involutivity

Let \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and by definition
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]

Assume \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, and let
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then \(F^\perp\) has rank \(r-k\). If \(J\) is any basis of \(F^\perp\), then
\[
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus the common value of \(p^\dagger\) on bases of \(F^\perp\) is \(p_I\). Hence
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p_I.
\]
Therefore
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
exactly.

If \(p\) is replaced by \(p+c\) for a global real constant \(c\), then \(\bar p\) and \(p^\dagger\) are also shifted by \(c\). Hence the construction descends to projective valuated matroids.

---

#### 10. Compatibility with flat quotients

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\). Put
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F},
\]
viewed as a matroid on \(E\), where the elements of \(F\) are loops. Its rank is
\[
n=r-\operatorname{rk}F.
\]
Define
\[
p^F_I=
\begin{cases}
0,& I\text{ is a basis of }N_F,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

The flats of \(N_F\), viewed as subsets of \(E\), are exactly the flats of \(M\) containing \(F\). Hence \(N_F\) is a matroid quotient of \(M\). The \(0/\infty\)-valuation of the bases of a matroid satisfies the tropical Plücker relations.

We verify the circuit-incidence relations. If \(n=0\), there are none. Suppose \(n>0\). Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit and let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(n-1\). Suppose there were a unique \(c_0\in C\) such that \(A\cup\{c_0\}\) is an \(N_F\)-basis. Then \(A\) is independent in \(N_F\). Let
\[
H=\operatorname{cl}_{N_F}(A).
\]
For every \(c\in C\setminus\{c_0\}\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\) is not an \(N_F\)-basis, so \(c\in H\). Since \(H\) is a flat of \(N_F\), it is also a flat of \(M\). But
\[
c_0\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus\{c_0\})\subseteq H,
\]
contradicting that \(A\cup\{c_0\}\) is independent in \(N_F\). Therefore \(p^F\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

Now compute its image under \(\dagger\). Let \(G\) be a rank-\(n\) flat of \(M\). For any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(G\),
\[
p^F_I=0
\]
if and only if \(I\) is a basis of \(N_F\), which is equivalent to
\[
F\vee G=\widehat 1.
\]
Since \(M\) is modular and \(\operatorname{rk}F+\operatorname{rk}G=r\), this is equivalent to
\[
F\wedge G=\widehat 0.
\]
Thus
\[
\overline{p^F}(G)=
\begin{cases}
0,& G\text{ is a complement of }F,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(\operatorname{rk}F\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \((p^F)^\dagger_J=\infty\), and \(J\) cannot be a basis of \(N_{F^\perp}\), so \(p^{F^\perp}_J=\infty\).

Assume \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}_M(J).
\]
Then
\[
(p^F)^\dagger_J
=
\overline{p^F}(G^\perp).
\]
This is finite precisely when \(G^\perp\) is a complement of \(F\), i.e. when
\[
G^\perp\vee F=\widehat 1,\qquad G^\perp\wedge F=\widehat 0.
\]
Applying \(\perp\), these conditions are equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F^\perp=\widehat 0,\qquad G\vee F^\perp=\widehat 1,
\]
namely that \(G\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\). By the preceding characterization of flat quotients, this is exactly the condition for
\[
p^{F^\perp}_J=0.
\]
Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

This completes the proof of the theorem.

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Basis exchange inside a flat.**  
   If \(B,B'\) are bases of a flat \(F\), then they are bases of \(M|F\). By the basis-exchange axiom, if \(B\ne B'\), choose \(e\in B\setminus B'\); there exists \(f\in B'\setminus B\) such that \(B-e+f\) is again a basis of \(M|F\). This increases \(|B\cap B'|\). Iterating connects \(B\) to \(B'\) by single-element exchanges inside \(F\).

2. **Intervals and minors.**  
   For a flat \(Y\), the flats of \(M|Y\) are precisely the flats \(G\le Y\), so \(\mathcal L(M|Y)=[\hat0,Y]\). For a flat \(X\), the flats of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) correspond to flats \(G\) of \(M\) with \(X\le G\), so \(\mathcal L(\operatorname{si}(M/X))=[X,E]\). Intervals in a modular geometric lattice are modular, so these minors/simplifications are finite simple modular matroids. If \(Y=X^\perp\), the polarity restricts to an order-reversing isomorphism
   \[
   [X,E]\longrightarrow[\hat0,Y],\qquad G\mapsto G^\perp .
   \]

3. **Restriction of \(p\) to \(Y\).**  
   In Section 8, if \(q_I=p_I\) for \(I\subseteq Y\), \(|I|=k\), and \(q\not\equiv\infty\), then \(q\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M|Y\): the tropical Plücker relations are inherited because all involved subsets lie in \(Y\), and circuits of \(M|Y\) are exactly circuits of \(M\) contained in \(Y\).

4. **Lifting circuits from contractions.**  
   Let \(Z\) be a flat with basis \(B\). If \(D\) is a circuit in \(\operatorname{si}((M|Y)/Z)\) and one chooses representatives \(e_X\in X\setminus Z\) for atoms \(X\in D\), then the representatives are dependent over \(Z\), while every proper subcollection is independent over \(Z\). Hence \(B\cup\{e_X:X\in D\}\) is dependent in \(M\), but \(B\) together with any proper subcollection is independent. Therefore any \(M\)-circuit contained in this set must contain every representative \(e_X\). This justifies the circuit-incidence applications in Lemma 5.1 and Lemma 7.1.

5. **Flat quotient computation.**  
   For \(N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\),
   \[
   r_{N_F}(A)=r_M(F\cup(A\setminus F))-r_M(F).
   \]
   Thus a set \(I\) of size \(n=r-r(F)\) is an \(N_F\)-basis iff \(r_M(F\cup I)=r\), equivalently \(F\vee \operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\hat1\). If \(G=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\) has rank \(n\), modularity gives
   \[
   F\vee G=\hat1
   \iff r(F\wedge G)=r(F)+r(G)-r=0
   \iff F\wedge G=\hat0.
   \]
   Hence \(\overline{p^F}(G)=0\) exactly when \(G\) is a complement of \(F\), and \(\infty\) otherwise. Applying the polarity converts “\(G^\perp\) complements \(F\)” into “\(G\) complements \(F^\perp\),” proving \((p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}\).

6. **\(0/\infty\) Plücker vector.**  
   For an ordinary matroid \(N\), its basis indicator valuation \(v_B=0\) for bases and \(\infty\) otherwise satisfies the tropical Plücker relations. Indeed, after contracting the common \((m-2)\)-set in a three-term Plücker relation, the claim reduces to a rank-two matroid on four elements: if one perfect matching consists of two bases, the parallel-class relation prevents both other matchings from failing simultaneously. Thus the minimum cannot have a unique finite term.

<PROBLEM_SOLVED>
After the routine patches above, the proof establishes the claimed partial theorem: for a finite simple modular matroid \(M\) with an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\), every rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient satisfying the stated tropical Plücker and circuit-incidence relations is \(\infty\) on \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-sets and is constant on bases of each rank-\(k\) flat. The construction \(p^\dagger_J=\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)\) on independent \((r-k)\)-sets gives a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\), is projectively well-defined, is involutive, and sends \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) to \(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}\). It does not prove order-reversal for arbitrary inclusions in the full relative Dressian.
</PROBLEM_SOLVED>

parsed.minor_gaps:
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The proof uses standard matroid interval/minor identifications without explicitly verifying them: \(\mathcal L(M|Y)=[\hat0,Y]\), \(\mathcal L(\operatorname{si}(M/X))=[X,E]\), preservation of modularity for intervals, and restriction of the polarity to these intervals.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The representative-circuit arguments in Lemma 5.1 Case 3 and Lemma 7.1 are essentially correct but should explicitly justify the lift from circuits in a simplification/contraction to circuits in \(M\).
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Section 10 needs a written rank-function verification for the flat quotient \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\), especially the equivalence between being a basis of the quotient and the complement condition in \(\mathcal L(M)\).
- EXPOSITION ONLY — The uses of basis-exchange connectivity of bases and the fact that the \(0/\infty\)-basis vector of an ordinary matroid satisfies tropical Plücker relations are textbook-standard, but adding one sentence or a citation would improve rigor.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Basis exchange inside a flat.**  
   If \(B,B'\) are bases of a flat \(F\), then they are bases of \(M|F\). By the basis-exchange axiom, if \(B\ne B'\), choose \(e\in B\setminus B'\); there exists \(f\in B'\setminus B\) such that \(B-e+f\) is again a basis of \(M|F\). This increases \(|B\cap B'|\). Iterating connects \(B\) to \(B'\) by single-element exchanges inside \(F\).

2. **Intervals and minors.**  
   For a flat \(Y\), the flats of \(M|Y\) are precisely the flats \(G\le Y\), so \(\mathcal L(M|Y)=[\hat0,Y]\). For a flat \(X\), the flats of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) correspond to flats \(G\) of \(M\) with \(X\le G\), so \(\mathcal L(\operatorname{si}(M/X))=[X,E]\). Intervals in a modular geometric lattice are modular, so these minors/simplifications are finite simple modular matroids. If \(Y=X^\perp\), the polarity restricts to an order-reversing isomorphism
   \[
   [X,E]\longrightarrow[\hat0,Y],\qquad G\mapsto G^\perp .
   \]

3. **Restriction of \(p\) to \(Y\).**  
   In Section 8, if \(q_I=p_I\) for \(I\subseteq Y\), \(|I|=k\), and \(q\not\equiv\infty\), then \(q\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M|Y\): the tropical Plücker relations are inherited because all involved subsets lie in \(Y\), and circuits of \(M|Y\) are exactly circuits of \(M\) contained in \(Y\).

4. **Lifting circuits from contractions.**  
   Let \(Z\) be a flat with basis \(B\). If \(D\) is a circuit in \(\operatorname{si}((M|Y)/Z)\) and one chooses representatives \(e_X\in X\setminus Z\) for atoms \(X\in D\), then the representatives are dependent over \(Z\), while every proper subcollection is independent over \(Z\). Hence \(B\cup\{e_X:X\in D\}\) is dependent in \(M\), but \(B\) together with any proper subcollection is independent. Therefore any \(M\)-circuit contained in this set must contain every representative \(e_X\). This justifies the circuit-incidence applications in Lemma 5.1 and Lemma 7.1.

5. **Flat quotient computation.**  
   For \(N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\),
   \[
   r_{N_F}(A)=r_M(F\cup(A\setminus F))-r_M(F).
   \]
   Thus a set \(I\) of size \(n=r-r(F)\) is an \(N_F\)-basis iff \(r_M(F\cup I)=r\), equivalently \(F\vee \operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\hat1\). If \(G=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\) has rank \(n\), modularity gives
   \[
   F\vee G=\hat1
   \iff r(F\wedge G)=r(F)+r(G)-r=0
   \iff F\wedge G=\hat0.
   \]
   Hence \(\overline{p^F}(G)=0\) exactly when \(G\) is a complement of \(F\), and \(\infty\) otherwise. Applying the polarity converts “\(G^\perp\) complements \(F\)” into “\(G\) complements \(F^\perp\),” proving \((p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}\).

6. **\(0/\infty\) Plücker vector.**  
   For an ordinary matroid \(N\), its basis indicator valuation \(v_B=0\) for bases and \(\infty\) otherwise satisfies the tropical Plücker relations. Indeed, after contracting the common \((m-2)\)-set in a three-term Plücker relation, the claim reduces to a rank-two matroid on four elements: if one perfect matching consists of two bases, the parallel-class relation prevents both other matchings from failing simultaneously. Thus the minimum cannot have a unique finite term.

The construction \(p^\dagger_J=\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)\) on independent \((r-k)\)-sets gives a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\), is projectively well-defined, is involutive, and sends \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) to \(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}\). original_problem:
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?
claim_verified: After the listed routine insertions, the proof establishes the stated special case: for every finite loopless simple modular matroid \(M\) of rank at most \(3\) whose flat lattice admits an order-reversing involution, the poset of projective tropical linear spaces in \(\Trop(M)\) arising from valuated quotients of \(\mu_M\) admits an inclusion-reversing involution \(\Phi\) extending the flat-lattice polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).
solution_verified:
## Claim

Let \(M\) be a finite loopless simple modular matroid of rank \(r\le 3\) on ground set \(E\). Assume that its lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Let \(\mu_M\) be the ordinary valuated matroid of \(M\), and let \(\Dr(M)\) be the poset of projective tropical linear spaces \(L(v)\subseteq \Trop(M)\) arising from valuated matroid quotients \(v\) of \(\mu_M\), ordered by inclusion.

For a flat \(F\), write
\[
X_F:=\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}),
\]
viewed as a tropical linear space on the original ground set \(E\).

Then \(\Dr(M)\) admits an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\to \Dr(M)
\]
such that
\[
\Phi(X_F)=X_{F^\perp}
\]
for every flat \(F\in \mathcal L(M)\).

---

We use the min-plus convention and write \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). For a valuated matroid \(v\) of rank \(k\) on \(E\), its projective tropical linear space \(L(v)\) is cut out by the valuated circuit equations
\[
\min_{i\in S}\bigl(x_i+v_{S\setminus\{i\}}\bigr)
\quad\text{is attained at least twice}
\]
for all \((k+1)\)-subsets \(S\subseteq E\). We also use the standard quotient-inclusion theorem: for valuated matroids \(v,w\) of ranks \(k\le \ell\),
\[
L(v)\subseteq L(w)
\]
if and only if \(v\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(w\). Equivalently, for all \(I\in \binom{E}{k-1}\) and \(J\in \binom{E}{\ell+1}\),
\[
\min_{j\in J\setminus I}
\bigl(v_{I\cup\{j\}}+w_{J\setminus\{j\}}\bigr)
\]
is attained at least twice.

We identify elements of \(E\) with atoms of \(\mathcal L(M)\). Since \(\mathcal L(M)\) is finite and graded, the anti-automorphism \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) satisfies
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F),
\qquad
(A\vee B)^\perp=A^\perp\wedge B^\perp .
\]

### 1. Same-rank inclusions

We first record a useful fact.

**Lemma 1.** Let \(v,w\) be valuated matroids of the same rank \(k\) on \(E\). If \(L(v)\subseteq L(w)\), then \(L(v)=L(w)\).

**Proof.** By the quotient-inclusion theorem, \(v\) is a valuated quotient of \(w\). Let \(V,W\) be their underlying ordinary matroids. Then \(V\) is an ordinary matroid quotient of \(W\). Thus there is a matroid \(N\) on \(E\sqcup X\) such that
\[
N\setminus X=W,
\qquad
N/X=V .
\]
Because \(V\) and \(W\) have the same rank \(k\),
\[
r_N(E\sqcup X)-r_N(X)=r_V(E)=k=r_W(E)=r_N(E).
\]
Hence
\[
r_N(E\sqcup X)=r_N(E)+r_N(X).
\]
For any \(A\subseteq E\), submodularity gives
\[
r_N(A\sqcup X)+r_N(E)
\ge r_N(E\sqcup X)+r_N(A)
= r_N(E)+r_N(X)+r_N(A).
\]
Thus
\[
r_N(A\sqcup X)\ge r_N(A)+r_N(X).
\]
The opposite inequality follows from subadditivity, so equality holds:
\[
r_N(A\sqcup X)=r_N(A)+r_N(X).
\]
Therefore
\[
r_V(A)
=r_{N/X}(A)
=r_N(A\sqcup X)-r_N(X)
=r_N(A)
=r_W(A).
\]
So \(V=W\).

Now let \(B=I\cup\{a\}\) and \(B'=I\cup\{b\}\) be adjacent bases of this common matroid, with \(|I|=k-1\). Applying the valuated quotient relation to \(I\) and \(J=I\cup\{a,b\}\), the two terms are
\[
v_B+w_{B'}
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
v_{B'}+w_B .
\]
The minimum of two terms is attained twice exactly when they are equal, so
\[
v_B+w_{B'}=v_{B'}+w_B.
\]
Thus
\[
v_B-w_B=v_{B'}-w_{B'}.
\]
The basis graph of a matroid is connected by repeated basis exchange, hence \(v_B-w_B\) is constant on all bases. Therefore \(v\) and \(w\) differ by a global tropical scalar and define the same projective tropical linear space. \(\square\)

### 2. Rank-one elements are points of \(\Trop(M)\)

Let \(\eta\) be a rank-one valuated matroid on \(E\). Its Plücker coordinates are \(\eta_e\in\mathbb T\), not all \(\infty\). The circuit equation for a pair \(\{i,j\}\) is
\[
\min(x_i+\eta_j,\ x_j+\eta_i)
\]
attained at least twice.

If \(\eta_i,\eta_j<\infty\), this forces
\[
x_i-\eta_i=x_j-\eta_j.
\]
If \(\eta_j=\infty\) and \(\eta_i<\infty\), the same equation forces \(x_j=\infty\). Hence
\[
L(\eta)=\{[\eta_e]_{e\in E}\}
\]
as a single projective point.

Consequently the rank-one part of \(\Dr(M)\) is canonically identified with \(\Trop(M)\): a rank-one valuated matroid \(\eta\) is a quotient of \(\mu_M\) exactly when its singleton tropical linear space lies inside \(\Trop(M)\).

### 3. Codimension-one quotients for ranks \(2\) and \(3\)

Assume now \(2\le r\le 3\).

For \(q=(q_e)_{e\in E}\in \Trop(M)\), define a rank \(r-1\) valuated matroid candidate \(\nu_q\) by
\[
(\nu_q)_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\operatorname{cl}(I)^\perp}, & |I|=r-1,\ I\text{ independent in }M,\\
\infty, & |I|=r-1,\ I\text{ dependent in }M.
\end{cases}
\]
Adding a scalar to \(q\) adds the same scalar to all finite coordinates of \(\nu_q\), so the projective tropical linear space \(L(\nu_q)\) depends only on the projective point \(q\).

#### Rank \(2\)

If \(r=2\), then \(M=U_{2,E}\) because \(M\) is simple and loopless. Hyperplanes are atoms. The polarity permutes the atoms, and
\[
(\nu_q)_e=q_{e^\perp}.
\]
Thus \(q\mapsto \nu_q\) is just a coordinate permutation. It preserves \(\Trop(M)\), and by the rank-one identification above gives a bijection
\[
\alpha:\Trop(M)\longrightarrow \Dr(M)_{1}.
\]
Because \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an involution, \(\alpha^{-1}=\alpha\).

#### Rank \(3\)

Assume \(r=3\). In a simple rank-three modular matroid, any two distinct lines meet in exactly one point. Indeed, if \(L_1,L_2\) are distinct rank-two flats, then \(L_1\vee L_2=E\), so modularity gives
\[
2+2=\operatorname{rk}(L_1\cap L_2)+3,
\]
hence \(\operatorname{rk}(L_1\cap L_2)=1\).

Also, in a simple rank-three matroid, the circuits are exactly:

1. three distinct points lying on a line;
2. four-point subsets with no three collinear.

We prove that \(q\mapsto L(\nu_q)\) gives a bijection from \(\Trop(M)\) to the rank-two part of \(\Dr(M)\).

First let \(\nu\) be a rank-two valuated quotient of \(\mu_M\). We show that its coordinates are constant on pairs spanning the same line. Let \(a,b,c\) be distinct points on a line \(H\), and choose \(d\notin H\). Apply the quotient incidence relation to
\[
I=\{a\},
\qquad
J=\{a,b,c,d\}.
\]
The term with \(j=d\) contains \((\mu_M)_{\{a,b,c\}}=\infty\), while the terms with \(j=b,c\) are
\[
\nu_{ab},\qquad \nu_{ac}.
\]
Hence \(\nu_{ab}=\nu_{ac}\). Varying the points shows that there is a well-defined line weight
\[
w_H:=\nu_{ab}
\qquad\text{whenever }H=\operatorname{cl}(a,b).
\]

Define
\[
q_e:=w_{e^\perp}.
\]
Since \(\nu\) has some finite basis coordinate, \(q\) is not identically \(\infty\).

We show \(q\in \Trop(M)\). Let \(\{a,b,c\}\subseteq H\) be a line circuit, and put \(h=H^\perp\). Choose a line \(D\) not containing \(h\): if \(h\notin H\), take \(D=H\); if \(h\in H\), choose \(e\in H\setminus\{h\}\) and \(d\notin H\), and take \(D=\operatorname{cl}(d,e)\). Then \(D\) cannot contain \(h\), because otherwise \(D=H\), contradicting \(d\notin H\).

For \(x\in\{a,b,c\}\), the line \(x^\perp\) contains \(h\). Since \(D\) does not contain \(h\), the two distinct lines \(D\) and \(x^\perp\) meet in a unique point; call it \(u_x\). We have \(u_x\neq h\). Moreover, if \(x\neq y\), then
\[
x^\perp\cap y^\perp=(x\vee y)^\perp=H^\perp=\{h\},
\]
so \(u_x\neq u_y\). Thus \(h,u_a,u_b,u_c\) are four distinct points.

For each \(x\), the set \(\{h,u_y,u_z\}\), where \(\{x,y,z\}=\{a,b,c\}\), is a basis: \(u_y,u_z\) are distinct points on \(D\), while \(h\notin D\). Applying the quotient incidence relation to
\[
I=\{h\},
\qquad
J=\{h,u_a,u_b,u_c\},
\]
we obtain that
\[
\min\bigl(\nu_{hu_a},\nu_{hu_b},\nu_{hu_c}\bigr)
\]
is attained at least twice. But
\[
\operatorname{cl}(h,u_x)=x^\perp,
\]
so
\[
\nu_{hu_x}=w_{x^\perp}=q_x.
\]
Therefore
\[
\min(q_a,q_b,q_c)
\]
is attained at least twice.

Now let \(\{a,b,c,d\}\) be a four-point circuit with no three collinear. Suppose, for contradiction, that \(q_a\) is the unique minimum among \(q_a,q_b,q_c,q_d\). Let
\[
x=\operatorname{cl}(b,c)\cap \operatorname{cl}(a,d).
\]
The two lines are distinct, so \(x\) is a well-defined point; moreover \(x\notin\{a,b,c,d\}\), since otherwise three of \(a,b,c,d\) would be collinear. The line circuit \(\{a,d,x\}\) forces
\[
q_x=q_a,
\]
because \(q_a<q_d\). But then the line circuit \(\{b,c,x\}\) has unique minimum \(q_x\), since \(q_b,q_c>q_a=q_x\), a contradiction. Thus \(q\in\Trop(M)\).

Conversely, let \(q\in\Trop(M)\). We show that \(\nu_q\) is a rank-two valuated quotient of \(\mu_M\).

First assume that all coordinates of \(q\) are finite. Let
\[
c_0<c_1<\cdots<c_m
\]
be the distinct coordinate values of \(q\), and set
\[
F_t=\{e\in E:q_e\ge c_t\}
\qquad (1\le t\le m).
\]
Each \(F_t\) is a flat: if \(e\in \operatorname{cl}(F_t)\setminus F_t\), then there is a circuit \(C\subseteq F_t\cup\{e\}\) containing \(e\), and \(e\) would be the unique minimum of \(q\) on \(C\). Hence, after subtracting the scalar \(c_0\),
\[
q=\sum_{t=1}^m (c_t-c_{t-1})\mathbf 1_{F_t}.
\]
In rank three, a chain of nonempty proper flats has length at most two, so projectively
\[
q=\lambda \mathbf 1_p+\mu \mathbf 1_L
\]
with \(\lambda,\mu\ge 0\), where \(p\) is a point, \(L\) is a line, and if both coefficients are nonzero then \(p\le L\).

We verify the rank-two tropical Plücker relations. Let \(\{a,b,c,d\}\subseteq E\). We must show that the minimum of
\[
\nu_{ab}+\nu_{cd},\qquad
\nu_{ac}+\nu_{bd},\qquad
\nu_{ad}+\nu_{bc}
\]
is attained at least twice.

If \(a,b,c,d\) lie on one line \(H\), then all six pairs span \(H\), so all three sums are equal.

If \(a,b,c\) lie on a line \(H\) and \(d\notin H\), then the three sums have a common summand \(q_{H^\perp}\), and the remaining terms are
\[
q_{\operatorname{cl}(a,d)^\perp},\quad
q_{\operatorname{cl}(b,d)^\perp},\quad
q_{\operatorname{cl}(c,d)^\perp}.
\]
These three poles are distinct points on the line \(d^\perp\), so the line-circuit condition for \(q\) implies that their minimum is attained at least twice.

It remains to treat the case where \(a,b,c,d\) have no three collinear. Define
\[
s_1=\operatorname{cl}(a,b)^\perp,\qquad
s_1'=\operatorname{cl}(c,d)^\perp,
\]
\[
s_2=\operatorname{cl}(a,c)^\perp,\qquad
s_2'=\operatorname{cl}(b,d)^\perp,
\]
\[
s_3=\operatorname{cl}(a,d)^\perp,\qquad
s_3'=\operatorname{cl}(b,c)^\perp.
\]
The six points \(s_i,s_i'\) are distinct, because the six side lines of a quadrangle with no three collinear are distinct.

For a flat \(F\), define
\[
m_F=\bigl(|F\cap\{s_1,s_1'\}|,\ |F\cap\{s_2,s_2'\}|,\ |F\cap\{s_3,s_3'\}|\bigr).
\]
The three Plücker sums are, up to adding the same scalar to all three coordinates,
\[
\lambda m_p+\mu m_L.
\]

For a point \(p\), the vector \(m_p\) is either \(0\) or a standard basis vector. For a line \(L\), let \(\ell=L^\perp\). The condition \(s_i\in L\) is equivalent to \(\ell\in s_i^\perp\), i.e. to \(\ell\) lying on the corresponding side line of the quadrangle. Therefore \(m_L\) can only be one of the following types:
\[
0,\qquad e_i,\qquad 2e_i,\qquad (1,1,1).
\]
Indeed, \(\ell\) lies on no side, on exactly one side, on two opposite sides, or at one of the four vertices, in which case it lies on one side from each opposite pair.

If \(p\le L\), then every nonzero coordinate of \(m_p\) is supported where \(m_L\) is already positive. In the cases \(m_L=0,e_i,2e_i\), at most one coordinate of \(\lambda m_p+\mu m_L\) is positive, so the minimum is attained on at least two coordinates. In the case \(m_L=(1,1,1)\), the vector is either constant or obtained from a constant vector by increasing one coordinate; again the minimum is attained at least twice. Thus the Plücker relation holds.

Now we verify the quotient incidence relations between \(\nu_q\) and \(\mu_M\). Let \(x\in E\) and let \(J\in\binom{E}{4}\). We must show that
\[
\min_{j\in J\setminus\{x\}}
\bigl(\nu_{xj}+(\mu_M)_{J\setminus\{j\}}\bigr)
\]
is attained at least twice.

If \(J\) lies on a line, then every \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is dependent in \(M\), so all terms are \(\infty\).

If \(J=\{a,b,c,d\}\), where \(a,b,c\) are collinear on a line \(H\) and \(d\notin H\), then the finite \(\mu_M\)-terms are exactly those with \(j\in\{a,b,c\}\). If \(x\in H\), all finite terms have the same value \(q_{H^\perp}\). If \(x\notin H\), then
\[
\operatorname{cl}(x,a)^\perp,\quad
\operatorname{cl}(x,b)^\perp,\quad
\operatorname{cl}(x,c)^\perp
\]
are three distinct points on the line \(x^\perp\), so the line-circuit condition for \(q\) gives the required repeated minimum.

Finally suppose \(J\) has no three collinear. Then every \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is a basis of \(M\), so all \(\mu_M\)-terms are finite. The poles
\[
\operatorname{cl}(x,j)^\perp,
\qquad
j\in J\setminus\{x\},
\]
all lie on the line \(x^\perp\). If a minimizing pole occurs at least twice, then the minimum is repeated. Otherwise the minimizing pole occurs only once. There are then at least two other distinct poles: if \(x\in J\), duplicates would force three points of \(J\) to be collinear; if \(x\notin J\), a pole of multiplicity three would force three elements of \(J\) to lie on a line through \(x\). Thus we can choose three distinct poles on \(x^\perp\), one of which is the unique minimizer, contradicting the line-circuit condition for \(q\). Hence the quotient incidence relation holds.

Therefore \(\nu_q\) is a rank-two valuated quotient of \(\mu_M\) whenever \(q\) is finite. For arbitrary \(q\in\Trop(M)\), choose finite \(q^{(n)}\in\Trop(M)\) converging projectively to \(q\), which is possible because \(\Trop(M)\) is the closure of the finite Bergman fan. The coordinates of \(\nu_{q^{(n)}}\) converge coordinatewise to those of \(\nu_q\). Tropical Plücker and quotient incidence conditions are closed conditions: their failures are finite unions of strict-inequality open conditions. Hence \(\nu_q\) is also a rank-two valuated quotient for arbitrary \(q\). Since \(q\) is not the all-\(\infty\) vector, some coordinate \(q_e\) is finite; choosing a basis of the line \(e^\perp\) gives a finite coordinate of \(\nu_q\), so the rank is indeed two.

The two constructions are inverse. Starting with a rank-two quotient \(\nu\), its line weights give \(q_e=w_{e^\perp}\), and then for any pair \(a,b\),
\[
(\nu_q)_{ab}
=
q_{\operatorname{cl}(a,b)^\perp}
=
w_{\operatorname{cl}(a,b)}
=
\nu_{ab}.
\]
Starting with \(q\), the line \(e^\perp\) recovers \(q_e\). Thus, in rank three, the map
\[
\alpha:\Trop(M)\longrightarrow \Dr(M)_2,
\qquad
q\longmapsto L(\nu_q),
\]
is a bijection.

### 4. Incidence symmetry

For \(2\le r\le 3\), we prove that for all \(p,q\in\Trop(M)\),
\[
p\in \alpha(q)
\qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad
q\in \alpha(p).
\]

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be a basis of \(M\). Define
\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp .
\]
We claim that
\[
B^*:=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again a basis and that
\[
\operatorname{cl}(B^*\setminus\{b_i^*\})=b_i^\perp .
\]

For \(r=2\), if \(B=\{a,b\}\), then
\[
a^*=b^\perp,\qquad b^*=a^\perp.
\]
These are distinct atoms, hence a basis, and the displayed closure identity is immediate.

For \(r=3\), write \(B=\{a,b,c\}\). Then
\[
a^*=\operatorname{cl}(b,c)^\perp,\quad
b^*=\operatorname{cl}(a,c)^\perp,\quad
c^*=\operatorname{cl}(a,b)^\perp.
\]
The points \(b^*,c^*\) both lie on the line \(a^\perp\), and they are distinct, so
\[
\operatorname{cl}(b^*,c^*)=a^\perp.
\]
Similarly,
\[
\operatorname{cl}(a^*,c^*)=b^\perp,
\qquad
\operatorname{cl}(a^*,b^*)=c^\perp.
\]
Moreover \(a^*\not\le a^\perp\), because otherwise applying \(\perp\) would give
\[
a\le \operatorname{cl}(b,c),
\]
contradicting that \(a,b,c\) is a basis. Hence \(a^*,b^*,c^*\) is a basis. The closure identities follow.

Thus, applying the same construction to the basis \(B^*\), one obtains
\[
(b_i^*)^*=b_i.
\]

Now \(p\in \alpha(q)=L(\nu_q)\) is equivalent to the circuit equations
\[
\min_{i\in S}\bigl(p_i+(\nu_q)_{S\setminus\{i\}}\bigr)
\]
being attained at least twice for every \(r\)-subset \(S\subseteq E\).

If \(S=B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) is a basis of \(M\), this equation is
\[
\min_i\bigl(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\quad\text{attained at least twice.}
\]
If \(S\) is not a basis, then necessarily \(r=3\), and \(S\) is a three-point line circuit. In that case all coefficients \((\nu_q)_{S\setminus\{i\}}\) are equal, possibly all \(\infty\). Thus the equation is either vacuous or exactly the ordinary circuit condition for \(p\in\Trop(M)\). Therefore the only nonautomatic equations are the basis equations
\[
\min_i\bigl(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\quad\text{attained at least twice}
\]
for all bases \(B\).

But the equation for \(B\) with \((p,q)\) is exactly the equation for \(B^*\) with \((q,p)\), because \((b_i^*)^*=b_i\). Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on the set of bases, we conclude
\[
p\in \alpha(q)
\qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad
q\in \alpha(p).
\]

### 5. Definition of \(\Phi\)

If \(r=0\), then \(E=\varnothing\), and \(\Dr(M)\) has one element. Define \(\Phi=\mathrm{id}\).

If \(r=1\), then simplicity and looplessness imply \(E\) has one element. The poset \(\Dr(M)\) has two elements: the rank-zero space \(X_E\) and the rank-one space \(X_\varnothing=\Trop(M)\). Define \(\Phi\) by swapping them.

Assume now \(2\le r\le 3\). Let
\[
\hat 0:=X_E,
\qquad
\hat 1:=X_\varnothing=\Trop(M).
\]
Define
\[
\Phi(\hat 0)=\hat 1,
\qquad
\Phi(\hat 1)=\hat 0.
\]
For a rank-one element \(p\in \Dr(M)\), identified with a point \(p\in\Trop(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(p):=\alpha(p).
\]
For a rank \(r-1\) element \(L\in\Dr(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(L):=\alpha^{-1}(L).
\]
In rank \(2\), the rank-one and rank-\((r-1)\) parts coincide and \(\alpha^{-1}=\alpha\), so this definition is consistent. In rank \(3\), it exchanges points with rank-two tropical linear spaces.

By construction, \(\Phi\) is an involution.

### 6. \(\Phi\) reverses inclusion

Let \(X\subseteq Y\) in \(\Dr(M)\). If \(X\) and \(Y\) have the same rank, then \(X=Y\) by Lemma 1, so there is nothing to prove.

The cases where \(X=\hat 0\) or \(Y=\hat 1\) are automatic because \(\Phi\) swaps \(\hat 0\) and \(\hat 1\). Therefore the only nontrivial strict inclusion occurs when \(r=3\), \(X\) is a point \(p\), and \(Y\) is a rank-two element. Write
\[
Y=\alpha(q)
\]
for the unique \(q\in\Trop(M)\). Then
\[
p\subseteq \alpha(q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
p\in \alpha(q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
q\in \alpha(p)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Phi(Y)\subseteq \Phi(p).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is inclusion-reversing.

### 7. Extension of the flat-lattice polarity

It remains to check that
\[
\Phi(X_F)=X_{F^\perp}
\]
for every flat \(F\).

The flats \(\varnothing\) and \(E\) are handled by construction:
\[
X_\varnothing=\Trop(M)=\hat 1,
\qquad
X_E=\hat 0,
\]
and \(\Phi\) swaps them, just as \(\perp\) swaps \(\varnothing\) and \(E\).

Now let \(H\) be a hyperplane. The matroid \(M/H\oplus U_{0,H}\) has rank one. Its nonloops are precisely the elements of \(E\setminus H\), while every element of \(H\) is a loop. Therefore \(X_H\) is the projective point \(q^H\) with coordinates
\[
q^H_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & e\in H,\\
0, & e\notin H.
\end{cases}
\]
For any hyperplane \(K\),
\[
\alpha(q^H)_K=q^H_{K^\perp}.
\]
Hence
\[
\alpha(q^H)_K=\infty
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
K^\perp\le H
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
H^\perp\le K.
\]
But \(X_{H^\perp}\) is \(M/H^\perp\oplus U_{0,H^\perp}\), whose rank-\((r-1)\) Plücker coordinate at a hyperplane \(K\) is infinite exactly when \(H^\perp\le K\). Thus
\[
\Phi(X_H)=X_{H^\perp}.
\]

Finally let \(a\) be an atom. The matroid \(M/a\oplus U_{0,a}\) has rank \(r-1\). For a hyperplane \(K\) and any basis \(I\) of \(K\), the coordinate indexed by \(I\) is finite exactly when \(I\cup\{a\}\) is a basis of \(M\), equivalently when \(a\nleq K\). Thus the hyperplane weights of \(X_a\) are
\[
w^a_K=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & a\le K,\\
0, & a\nleq K.
\end{cases}
\]
Applying \(\alpha^{-1}\), we obtain the point \(q\) with
\[
q_e=w^a_{e^\perp}.
\]
Therefore
\[
q_e=\infty
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a\le e^\perp
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
e\le a^\perp.
\]
This is exactly the point \(X_{a^\perp}\), since \(X_{a^\perp}\) has infinite coordinates precisely on the hyperplane \(a^\perp\). Hence
\[
\Phi(X_a)=X_{a^\perp}.
\]

For \(r\le 3\), every proper nonempty flat is either an atom or a hyperplane. Therefore
\[
\Phi(X_F)=X_{F^\perp}
\]
for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\). This completes the proof.

\[

\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad

\]
Also cite Corollary 4.3.2(4) for the statement that a valuated quotient induces an ordinary matroid quotient on underlying matroids.

2. **Well-definedness of \(\alpha^{-1}\).** Insert after the rank-three bijection proof:
   
   If \(L(\nu)=L(\nu')\) are two rank-two quotient representatives of the same element of \(\Dr(M)_2\), then applying Lemma 1 to both inclusions gives \(\nu'=\nu+c\) on all bases. Thus \(\alpha^{-1}\) is independent of the chosen representative. Conversely, if \(L(\nu_q)=L(\nu_{q'})\), Lemma 1 gives \(\nu_{q'}=\nu_q+c\). For each atom \(e\), choose a basis \(\{a,b\}\) of the line \(e^\perp\); then
\[
q'_e=(\nu_{q'})_{ab}=(\nu_q)_{ab}+c=q_e+c.
\]
So \(q'\) and \(q\) define the same projective point. Hence \(\alpha\) is genuinely a bijection on spaces.

3. **Rank-stratification insertion for Section 6.** Before the inclusion reversal argument, insert:
   
   By the full quotient-inclusion theorem, if \(L(v)\subseteq L(w)\), then \(\operatorname{rk}(v)\le \operatorname{rk}(w)\). The rank-zero projective tropical linear space is unique, giving \(\hat 0=X_E\). If \(L(v)\subseteq L(\mu_M)=\Trop(M)\) and \(\operatorname{rk}(v)=r\), then Lemma 1 gives \(L(v)=L(\mu_M)\), so the rank-\(r\) element is uniquely \(\hat 1=X_\varnothing\). Therefore, for \(r\le 3\), the only strict inclusions not involving \(\hat0\) or \(\hat1\) are point-in-rank-two inclusions in rank \(3\). This makes the case analysis exhaustive.
