original_problem:
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?
claim_verified: After the routine patches above, the proof establishes the claimed partial theorem: for a finite simple modular matroid \(M\) with an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\), every rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient satisfying the stated tropical Plücker and circuit-incidence relations is \(\infty\) on \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-sets and is constant on bases of each rank-\(k\) flat. The construction \(p^\dagger_J=\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)\) on independent \((r-k)\)-sets gives a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\), is projectively well-defined, is involutive, and sends \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) to \(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}\). It does not prove order-reversal for arbitrary inclusions in the full relative Dressian.
solution_verified:
## Exact claim proved

Let \(M\) be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(r\) on ground set \(E\). Suppose \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Fix \(0\le k\le r\). Write \(\overline{\mathbb R}=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), and extend any function \(p:\binom Ek\to\overline{\mathbb R}\) by \(p_T=\infty\) when \(|T|\ne k\).

Call \(p\) a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\) if \(p\not\equiv\infty\), \(p\) satisfies the three-term tropical Plücker relations, and for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\min_{c\in C} p_{A\cup\{c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. For \(k=0\), there are no circuit-incidence relations.

The claim proved is:

1. Every such \(p\) is \(\infty\) on \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-sets and is constant on bases of each rank-\(k\) flat of \(M\).
2. If \(\bar p(F)\) denotes the common value of \(p\) on bases of a rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), and \(\bar p(F)=\infty\) for flats of other ranks, then
   \[
   p^\dagger_J=
   \begin{cases}
   \bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
   \infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent},
   \end{cases}
   \qquad |J|=r-k,
   \]
   is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated quotient of \(M\).
3. The map \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) descends to tropical projective classes and is involutive.
4. For every flat \(F\), if \(p^F\) is the \(0/\infty\)-valuated matroid of
   \[
   M/F\oplus U_{0,F},
   \]
   then
   \[
   (p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
   \]

No assertion is made here that this map reverses arbitrary inclusions of tropical linear spaces in the full relative Dressian.

---

## Proof

A finite family in \(\overline{\mathbb R}\) has **no unique finite minimizer** if either all entries are \(\infty\), or the smallest finite value occurs at least twice.

Since \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an order-reversing lattice involution,
\[
(F\vee G)^\perp=F^\perp\wedge G^\perp,
\qquad
(F\wedge G)^\perp=F^\perp\vee G^\perp.
\]
Also
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F),
\]
because the anti-isomorphism identifies the interval \([\hat 0,F]\) with \([F^\perp,\hat 1]\).

### 1. Flat-constancy of quotient valuations

Let \(p:\binom Ek\to\overline{\mathbb R}\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

If \(k=0\), there is only the empty set, so the statement is immediate. Assume \(k\ge 1\).

First let \(I\in\binom Ek\) be \(M\)-dependent. Choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and an element \(c\in C\). Set \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). For every \(x\in C\setminus\{c\}\), one has \(x\in A\), so \(A\cup\{x\}=A\) has size \(k-1\), hence
\[
p_{A\cup\{x\}}=\infty.
\]
The only possibly finite term in the circuit-incidence relation for \(C\) and \(A\) is \(p_I=p_{A\cup\{c\}}\). Thus \(p_I=\infty\).

Now suppose
\[
I=A\cup\{a\},
\qquad
I'=A\cup\{b\},
\]
are independent \(k\)-sets with the same closure. Then \(a\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{b\})\) but \(a\notin\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), so there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing \(a\). This circuit must also contain \(b\); otherwise it would be contained in the independent set \(A\cup\{a\}\). Applying circuit-incidence to \(C\) and \(A\), all terms are \(\infty\) except possibly
\[
p_{A\cup\{a\}},
\qquad
p_{A\cup\{b\}}.
\]
Hence these two values are equal.

Finally, if \(B,B'\) are bases of the same rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), then they are bases of the restriction \(M|F\). By basis exchange in \(M|F\), if \(B\ne B'\) and \(e\in B\setminus B'\), there exists \(f\in B'\setminus B\) such that \(B-e+f\) is again a basis of \(M|F\). This increases \(|B\cap B'|\). Iterating connects \(B\) to \(B'\) by single-element exchanges inside \(F\). The previous paragraph shows that \(p\) is unchanged along each exchange. Thus \(p\) is constant on the bases of \(F\).

So for every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), \(\bar p(F)\) is well-defined.

### 2. Auxiliary lattice facts

We use the following standard interval identifications.

If \(Y\) is a flat of \(M\), then the flats of \(M|Y\) are exactly the flats \(G\) of \(M\) with \(G\le Y\). Thus
\[
\mathcal L(M|Y)=[\hat 0,Y].
\]
Indeed, since \(Y\) is flat, \(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\subseteq Y\) for every \(A\subseteq Y\).

If \(X\) is a flat of \(M\), then the flats of \(M/X\), and hence of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\), correspond to flats \(G\) of \(M\) with \(X\le G\). Thus
\[
\mathcal L(\operatorname{si}(M/X))\cong [X,\hat 1].
\]
Simplification does not change the lattice of flats, because flats are unions of parallel classes.

Intervals in a modular lattice are modular. Hence restrictions to flats and simplifications of contractions of \(M\) are again finite simple modular matroids.

If \(Y=X^\perp\), then the polarity restricts to an order-reversing isomorphism
\[
[X,\hat 1]\longrightarrow [\hat 0,Y],
\qquad
G\longmapsto G^\perp.
\]

### 3. Two elementary lemmas on modular matroids

Because \(M\) is simple, elements of \(E\) are atoms of \(\mathcal L(M)\).

#### Lemma 3.1: Line-closed atom sets are flats

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular matroid. Let \(S\) be a set of atoms such that whenever \(a,b\in S\), every atom on the line \(a\vee b\) also belongs to \(S\). Then \(S\) is the set of atoms of a flat of \(N\).

**Proof.** Let \(F=\bigvee_{a\in S}a\). We show that every atom \(x\le F\) lies in \(S\). Choose atoms \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in S\) minimally such that
\[
x\le a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_m.
\]
Minimality implies that \(a_1,\dots,a_m\) are independent, so their join has rank \(m\).

If \(m=1\), then \(x=a_1\in S\). Assume \(m\ge2\). If \(x=a_m\), we are done. Otherwise set
\[
P=a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_{m-1},
\qquad
L=a_m\vee x.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(P)=m-1\), \(\operatorname{rk}(L)=2\), and \(P\vee L=P\vee a_m\) has rank \(m\). By modularity,
\[
\operatorname{rk}(P\wedge L)
=
(m-1)+2-m
=
1.
\]
Thus \(P\wedge L\) is an atom \(b\le P\). By induction on \(m\), \(b\in S\). Since \(b,a_m\in S\) and \(x\) lies on the line \(b\vee a_m=L\), line-closedness gives \(x\in S\). Therefore \(S\) is exactly the set of atoms of the flat \(F\). ∎

#### Lemma 3.2: Tropical points and upper level flats

Let \(N\) be a finite simple matroid with atom set \(A(N)\), and let
\[
w:A(N)\to\overline{\mathbb R}
\]
be not identically \(\infty\). Then \(w\) satisfies the tropical circuit condition
\[
\min_{x\in C} w_x
\]
with no unique finite minimizer for every circuit \(C\) of \(N\) if and only if, for every real \(\tau\),
\[
A_\tau=\{x\in A(N): w_x\ge \tau\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

**Proof.** Suppose some \(A_\tau\) is not a flat. Then there exists \(e\notin A_\tau\) with \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(A_\tau)\). Hence some circuit \(C\subseteq A_\tau\cup\{e\}\) contains \(e\). For this circuit,
\[
w_e<\tau\le w_x
\qquad\text{for all }x\in C\setminus\{e\},
\]
so \(e\) is the unique finite minimizer.

Conversely, suppose a circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimizer \(e\). Choose a real number \(\tau\) such that
\[
w_e<\tau\le w_x
\qquad\text{for all }x\in C\setminus\{e\}.
\]
Then \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq A_\tau\), while \(e\notin A_\tau\), and \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(C\setminus\{e\})\). Thus \(A_\tau\) is not a flat. ∎

#### Lemma 3.3: Lifting circuits from simplifications of contractions

Let \(N\) be a matroid, let \(Z\) be a flat, and let \(B\) be a basis of \(Z\). Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(\operatorname{si}(N/Z)\). For each atom \(\alpha\in D\), choose an element \(e_\alpha\) of \(N\setminus Z\) representing \(\alpha\). Then
\[
B\cup\{e_\alpha:\alpha\in D\}
\]
is dependent in \(N\), while \(B\) together with any proper subcollection of the representatives is independent. Consequently, there exists an \(N\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq B\cup\{e_\alpha:\alpha\in D\}
\]
and every such circuit contains all representatives \(e_\alpha\).

**Proof.** Simplification preserves independence of subsets containing at most one element from each parallel class. Thus the representatives of \(D\) are dependent in \(N/Z\), and every proper subcollection is independent in \(N/Z\). Translating independence in \(N/Z\) back to \(N\), this says exactly that \(B\) together with all representatives is dependent, while \(B\) together with any proper subcollection is independent. Taking a minimal dependent subset gives an \(N\)-circuit, and it must contain every representative, because omitting any one representative leaves an independent set. ∎

### 4. A rank-three matching lemma

Let \(P\) be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(3\). Let
\[
w:A(P)\to\overline{\mathbb R}
\]
be a tropical point of \(P\), i.e. \(w\not\equiv\infty\) and \(w\) satisfies the tropical circuit condition. Extend \(w\) to all flats of \(P\) by setting \(w(F)=\infty\) whenever \(F\) is not an atom.

#### Lemma 4.1

For any four hyperplanes \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) of \(P\), the minimum of
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),
\]
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),
\]
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(L_1=L_2\), then \(L_1\wedge L_2=L_1\) is not an atom, so that term is \(\infty\), while the other two displayed sums are equal. Thus assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

Normalize \(w\) by subtracting its finite minimum, so \(\min w=0\). By Lemma 3.2, every positive upper level set \(\{x:w_x\ge\tau\}\), \(\tau>0\), is a proper flat. In a simple rank-three matroid, every nonempty proper flat is either an atom or a hyperplane. Since these upper level sets form a chain, there is a flag \(a\le B\), with \(a\) an atom and \(B\) a hyperplane, and numbers
\[
0\le \beta\le \alpha\le\infty
\]
such that
\[
w(x)=
\begin{cases}
0,& x\notin B,\\
\beta,& x\in B\setminus\{a\},\\
\alpha,& x=a.
\end{cases}
\]

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Set
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For \(i\ne j\), the atom \(L_i\wedge L_j\) lies in \(B\) if and only if \(y_i=y_j\), in which case \(L_i\wedge L_j=y_i\). Thus a matched pair \((i,j)\) contributes \(w(y_i)\) when \(y_i=y_j\), and contributes \(0\) otherwise. The three displayed sums are therefore the three perfect-matching costs of four colored objects \(y_1,y_2,y_3,y_4\), where an equal-colored pair contributes its color weight and a distinct-colored pair contributes \(0\). Checking the possible multiplicity types of the colors gives no unique minimum: all distinct gives three zero sums; one repeated pair gives two zero sums; type \(2+2\) gives two zero sums; type \(3+1\) gives three equal sums; and type \(4\) gives three equal sums.

Now suppose one hyperplane equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), set
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
The three sums are
\[
w(y_2)+g(y_3,y_4),
\qquad
w(y_3)+g(y_2,y_4),
\qquad
w(y_4)+g(y_2,y_3),
\]
where
\[
g(y,z)=
\begin{cases}
w(y),& y=z,\\
0,& y\ne z.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are distinct, then at least two of them have weight \(\beta\), and the third has weight \(\beta\) or \(\alpha\), so the minimum is tied. If exactly two are equal, the two sums pairing \(B\) with one of those two equal atoms are equal and are no larger than the third. If all three are equal, all three sums are equal. Thus again there is no unique finite minimizer. ∎

### 5. A local dual Plücker relation

Let \(Y\) be a flat of \(M\) of rank \(k+2\). Let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Put
\[
K_{ij}=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Recall that \(\bar p(F)=\infty\) unless \(\operatorname{rk}(F)=k\).

#### Lemma 5.1

The minimum of
\[
\bar p(K_{12})+\bar p(K_{34}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{13})+\bar p(K_{24}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{14})+\bar p(K_{23})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \(K_{12}=H_1\) has rank \(k+1\), so \(\bar p(K_{12})=\infty\), while the other two displayed sums are equal. Thus assume the \(H_i\) are distinct.

Since \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular, two distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\). Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting with each hyperplane lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
k-2\le \operatorname{rk}(Z)\le k.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then every \(K_{ij}\) contains \(Z\) and has rank \(k\), hence every \(K_{ij}=Z\). All three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i} H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\). Indeed, \(T_i\) is obtained by intersecting two distinct hyperplanes, giving rank \(k\), and then intersecting with a third hyperplane, lowering rank by at most one. If \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k\), then intersecting \(T_i\) with \(H_i\) would give rank at least \(k-1\), contradicting \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\).

For \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), the flats \(T_m,T_n\) are distinct rank-\((k-1)\) flats contained in \(K_{ij}\), and their join is \(K_{ij}\). Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\). For each \(i\), choose
\[
t_i\in T_i\setminus Z
\]
so that \(B\cup\{t_i\}\) is a basis of \(T_i\). Since \(T_i\wedge T_j=Z\) for \(i\ne j\), the \(t_i\) are distinct. Moreover, for \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
B\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is a basis of \(K_{ij}\). Applying the tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) to \(B\) and \(t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\) gives exactly the desired relation.

It remains to consider \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\) is the lattice of flats of the finite simple modular rank-three matroid
\[
P=\operatorname{si}((M|Y)/Z).
\]
Its atoms are the rank-\(k\) flats between \(Z\) and \(Y\), and its hyperplanes are the rank-\((k+1)\) flats between \(Z\) and \(Y\).

Define
\[
w(X)=\bar p(X)
\]
for every atom \(X\) of \(P\). If \(w\equiv\infty\), then all six \(\bar p(K_{ij})\) are \(\infty\), and the claim is trivial. Otherwise, we show that \(w\) is a tropical point of \(P\).

Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(P\). Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\). For each atom \(X\in D\), choose \(e_X\in X\setminus Z\). By Lemma 3.3, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq B\cup\{e_X:X\in D\}
\]
containing all representatives \(e_X\). Applying circuit-incidence for \(p\) with the \((k-1)\)-set \(B\), the terms from elements of \(C\cap B\) are \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{B\cup\{e_X\}}=\bar p(X)=w(X).
\]
Therefore
\[
\min_{X\in D} w(X)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(w\) is a tropical point of \(P\).

Now apply Lemma 4.1 to the four hyperplanes \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) of \(P\). Their pairwise meets in \(P\) are precisely the atoms \(K_{ij}\). This proves the desired relation. ∎

### 6. Tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

Set
\[
\ell=r-k.
\]
Define \(p^\dagger\) on \(\ell\)-subsets by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

First, \(p^\dagger\not\equiv\infty\). Since \(p\not\equiv\infty\), there is \(I\in\binom Ek\) with \(p_I<\infty\). By Section 1, \(I\) is independent. Let \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Then \(F^\perp\) has rank \(\ell\). If \(J\) is a basis of \(F^\perp\), then
\[
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I
<
\infty.
\]

Now prove the tropical Plücker relations. If \(\ell<2\), there are none. Assume \(\ell\ge2\). Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(\ell-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must show that the minimum of
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,b\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{c,d\}},
\]
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,c\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{b,d\}},
\]
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,d\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{b,c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is dependent, all six involved \(\ell\)-sets are dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(S\) is independent, and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\), say \(a\), lies in \(X\), then every set \(S\cup\{a,x\}\) is dependent. Each of the three Plücker sums contains one such term, so all three sums are \(\infty\). Thus assume
\[
a,b,c,d\notin X.
\]

Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=\ell-2\), we have \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(x\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_x=(X\vee x)^\perp.
\]
Because \(x\notin X\), each \(H_x\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\).

For a pair \(x,y\), if \(S\cup\{x,y\}\) is independent, then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(S\cup\{x,y\})=X\vee x\vee y,
\]
and therefore
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(S\cup\{x,y\})^\perp
=
(X\vee x\vee y)^\perp
=
H_x\wedge H_y.
\]
Hence
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}
=
\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y).
\]

If \(S\cup\{x,y\}\) is dependent, then, since both \(x,y\notin X\), one has
\[
X\vee x=X\vee y.
\]
Thus \(H_x=H_y\), so \(H_x\wedge H_y=H_x\) has rank \(k+1\), and therefore
\[
\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y)=\infty=p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}.
\]
Thus in all cases,
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}
=
\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y).
\]

The desired Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is now exactly Lemma 5.1 applied to the four hyperplanes \(H_a,H_b,H_c,H_d\) of \(Y\).

### 7. A codimension-one polarity lemma

#### Lemma 7.1

Let \(N\) and \(N'\) be finite simple modular matroids of the same rank \(n\ge1\), and let
\[
\delta:\mathcal L(N')\to\mathcal L(N)
\]
be an order-reversing lattice isomorphism. Let \(q\) be a rank-\((n-1)\) valuated quotient of \(N\). For every hyperplane \(H\) of \(N\), let \(\bar q(H)\) be the common value of \(q\) on bases of \(H\). Define
\[
u_a=\bar q(\delta(a))
\]
for every atom \(a\) of \(N'\). Then \(u\) is a tropical point of \(N'\).

**Proof.** If \(n=1\), then \(N'\) has a single atom and the claim is immediate. Assume \(n\ge2\).

Since \(q\not\equiv\infty\), there is an \((n-1)\)-set \(I\) with \(q_I<\infty\). By Section 1, \(I\) is independent in \(N\), and \(H=\operatorname{cl}_N(I)\) is a hyperplane with \(\bar q(H)<\infty\). Since \(\delta\) maps atoms of \(N'\) bijectively to hyperplanes of \(N\), \(u\not\equiv\infty\).

By Lemma 3.2, it is enough to show that for every real \(\tau\), the upper level set
\[
A_\tau=\{a:u_a\ge \tau\}
\]
is a flat of \(N'\). By Lemma 3.1, it suffices to show \(A_\tau\) is line-closed.

Let \(a,b\in A_\tau\), and let \(c\) be an atom on the line \(a\vee b\). If \(c=a\) or \(c=b\), there is nothing to prove. Assume \(a,b,c\) are distinct. Set
\[
Z=\delta(a\vee b).
\]
Then \(Z\) has rank \(n-2\), and the three hyperplanes
\[
\delta(a),\delta(b),\delta(c)
\]
of \(N\) all contain \(Z\).

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\). For \(s\in\{a,b,c\}\), choose
\[
x_s\in \delta(s)\setminus Z
\]
so that \(B\cup\{x_s\}\) is a basis of \(\delta(s)\). In the simplification of \(N/Z\), the three atoms represented by \(x_a,x_b,x_c\) form a circuit. By Lemma 3.3, there is an \(N\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq B\cup\{x_a,x_b,x_c\}
\]
containing \(x_a,x_b,x_c\).

Apply circuit-incidence for \(q\) with the \((n-2)\)-set \(B\). Terms coming from elements of \(C\cap B\) are \(\infty\), while
\[
q_{B\cup\{x_s\}}
=
\bar q(\delta(s))
=
u_s.
\]
Thus
\[
\min(u_a,u_b,u_c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Since \(u_a,u_b\ge\tau\), if \(u_c<\tau\), then \(u_c\) would be the unique finite minimizer among \(u_a,u_b,u_c\). This is impossible. Hence \(u_c\ge\tau\), so \(c\in A_\tau\). Therefore \(A_\tau\) is line-closed, hence a flat. By Lemma 3.2, \(u\) is a tropical point of \(N'\). ∎

### 8. Circuit-incidence relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the circuit-incidence relations for a rank-\(\ell\) quotient, where \(\ell=r-k\).

If \(\ell=0\), there are no circuit-incidence relations. Assume \(\ell\ge1\). Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(\ell-1\). We must show that
\[
\min_{c\in C} p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is dependent, then every \(A\cup\{c\}\) is either dependent or has size \(\ell-1\), so all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent, and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=\ell-1\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Thus \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

If \(c\in X\), then \(A\cup\{c\}\) is dependent or has the wrong size, so
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}=\infty.
\]
If \(c\notin X\), then \(A\cup\{c\}\) is independent and
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp).
\]

Let \(q\) be the restriction of \(p\) to \(k\)-subsets of \(Y\):
\[
q_I=p_I,
\qquad
I\subseteq Y,\ |I|=k.
\]
If \(q\equiv\infty\), then all rank-\(k\) flats contained in \(Y\) have \(\bar p\)-value \(\infty\), so all terms \(p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}\) are \(\infty\). The circuit-incidence relation is then trivial.

Assume \(q\not\equiv\infty\). Then \(q\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M|Y\). The tropical Plücker relations are inherited because all involved subsets lie in \(Y\), and the circuits of \(M|Y\) are exactly the circuits of \(M\) contained in \(Y\).

Let
\[
N'=\operatorname{si}(M/X).
\]
Its lattice of flats is \([X,\hat 1]\). The lattice of flats of \(M|Y\) is \([\hat 0,Y]\). The polarity restricts to an order-reversing isomorphism
\[
[X,\hat 1]\longrightarrow[\hat 0,Y],
\qquad
G\longmapsto G^\perp.
\]
Applying Lemma 7.1 to \(N=M|Y\), \(N'=\operatorname{si}(M/X)\), and \(q\), we get a tropical point \(u\) of \(N'\) defined on atoms by
\[
u_{X\vee c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp),
\qquad c\notin X.
\]
For \(c\notin X\), if \(\alpha(c)\) denotes the atom of \(N'\) represented by \(c\), then
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}=u_{\alpha(c)}.
\]

Suppose for contradiction that
\[
\{p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}:c\in C\}
\]
has a unique finite minimum at \(c_0\in C\). Then \(c_0\notin X\). Since \(C\) is a circuit of \(M\),
\[
c_0\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus\{c_0\})
\subseteq
\operatorname{cl}_M\bigl(X\cup (C\setminus X\setminus\{c_0\})\bigr).
\]
Thus in the contraction \(M/X\), the image of \(c_0\) lies in the closure of the images of
\[
R=C\setminus X\setminus\{c_0\}.
\]

If some \(d\in R\) were parallel to \(c_0\) in \(M/X\), then \(\alpha(d)=\alpha(c_0)\), and hence
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{d\}}
=
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c_0\}},
\]
contradicting uniqueness. Therefore no element of \(R\) represents \(\alpha(c_0)\).

Passing to the simplification \(N'\), we have
\[
\alpha(c_0)\in\operatorname{cl}_{N'}\{\alpha(d):d\in R\}.
\]
Hence there exists a circuit \(D\) of \(N'\) such that
\[
\alpha(c_0)\in D
\subseteq
\{\alpha(c_0)\}\cup\{\alpha(d):d\in R\}.
\]
For every \(\beta\in D\setminus\{\alpha(c_0)\}\), choose \(d\in R\) with \(\beta=\alpha(d)\). By uniqueness of the finite minimum at \(c_0\),
\[
u_\beta
=
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{d\}}
>
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c_0\}}
=
u_{\alpha(c_0)}
\]
or \(u_\beta=\infty\). Thus \(u\) has a unique finite minimum on the circuit \(D\), contradicting that \(u\) is a tropical point of \(N'\).

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the circuit-incidence relations. Together with Section 6, this proves that \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

### 9. Involutivity and projective well-definedness

We now prove
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\) by Section 1, and by definition
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]

Assume \(I\) is independent, and set
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then \(F^\perp\) has rank \(r-k\). If \(J\) is any basis of \(F^\perp\), then
\[
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus the common value of \(p^\dagger\) on bases of \(F^\perp\) is \(p_I\). Therefore
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p_I.
\]
So \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\).

If \(p\) is replaced by \(p+c\) for a constant \(c\in\mathbb R\), then every \(\bar p(F)\) is replaced by \(\bar p(F)+c\), and hence \(p^\dagger\) is replaced by \(p^\dagger+c\). Thus \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) descends to tropical projective equivalence classes.

### 10. Compatibility with flat quotients

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and set
\[
f=\operatorname{rk}(F),
\qquad
n=r-f.
\]
Let
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
be the matroid on \(E\) obtained by contracting \(F\) and making the elements of \(F\) loops. Its rank function is
\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F).
\]
Define
\[
p^F_I=
\begin{cases}
0,& I\text{ is a basis of }N_F,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
\qquad |I|=n.
\]

First, \(p^F\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations because it is the \(0/\infty\)-basis valuation of an ordinary matroid. Explicitly, after contracting the common \((n-2)\)-set in a three-term Plücker relation, the claim reduces to rank two: among four elements, if one perfect matching consists of two bases, then the parallel-class structure forces at least one other perfect matching to consist of two bases as well. Hence the minimum cannot have a unique finite term.

Next, \(p^F\) satisfies the circuit-incidence relations with respect to \(M\). The flats of \(N_F\) are exactly the flats of \(M\) containing \(F\): every flat of \(N_F\) contains the loops \(F\), and for \(H\supseteq F\),
\[
e\notin \operatorname{cl}_{N_F}(H)
\iff
r_M(H\cup\{e\})>r_M(H).
\]
So \(H\) is \(N_F\)-flat iff it is \(M\)-flat.

Assume \(n>0\), let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(n-1\). Suppose there were a unique \(c_0\in C\) such that \(A\cup\{c_0\}\) is an \(N_F\)-basis. Then \(A\) is independent in \(N_F\), and
\[
H=\operatorname{cl}_{N_F}(A)
\]
is a hyperplane of \(N_F\). For every \(c\in C\setminus\{c_0\}\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\) is not an \(N_F\)-basis, so \(c\in H\). Since \(H\) is also an \(M\)-flat, and \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit,
\[
c_0\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus\{c_0\})\subseteq H.
\]
But then \(c_0\in\operatorname{cl}_{N_F}(A)\), contradicting that \(A\cup\{c_0\}\) is an \(N_F\)-basis. Thus \(p^F\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Now compute its dagger. Let \(G\) be a rank-\(n\) flat of \(M\), and let \(I\) be a basis of \(G\). Then
\[
I\text{ is an }N_F\text{-basis}
\iff
r_M(F\cup I)=r
\iff
F\vee G=\hat 1.
\]
Because \(M\) is modular and
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F)+\operatorname{rk}(G)=f+n=r,
\]
this is equivalent to
\[
F\wedge G=\hat 0.
\]
Therefore
\[
\overline{p^F}(G)=
\begin{cases}
0,& G\text{ is a complement of }F,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Now let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(f\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \((p^F)^\dagger_J=\infty\), and \(J\) cannot be a basis of \(N_{F^\perp}\), so also \(p^{F^\perp}_J=\infty\).

Assume \(J\) is independent, and set
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}_M(J).
\]
Then
\[
(p^F)^\dagger_J
=
\overline{p^F}(G^\perp).
\]
This is finite precisely when \(G^\perp\) is a complement of \(F\), i.e.
\[
G^\perp\vee F=\hat 1,
\qquad
G^\perp\wedge F=\hat 0.
\]
Applying \(\perp\), these conditions are equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F^\perp=\hat 0,
\qquad
G\vee F^\perp=\hat 1.
\]
That is, \(G\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\). By the same rank-function criterion, this is precisely the condition that \(J\) be a basis of
\[
N_{F^\perp}=M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}.
\]
Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger_J=p^{F^\perp}_J
\]
for every \(J\), and therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

This proves all stated claims.

   is a proper flat. If \(A_\varepsilon=\varnothing\), then all weights are \(0\). If \(A_\varepsilon\) is an atom \(a\), choose any hyperplane \(B\supset a\), set \(\beta=0\), \(\alpha=w(a)\). If \(A_\varepsilon\) is a hyperplane \(B\), then all atoms outside \(B\) have weight \(0\). Let \(\beta=\min_{x\in B}w_x\). If two distinct atoms of \(B\) had weight \(>\beta\), then for some real \(\tau>\beta\), \(A_\tau\cap B\) would contain two atoms of \(B\) but omit an atom of weight \(\beta\), contradicting flatness since two atoms in a rank-two flat span the whole hyperplane \(B\). Hence at most one atom \(a\in B\) has weight \(>\beta\); all atoms in \(B\setminus\{a\}\) have weight \(\beta\), and \(w(a)=\alpha\ge\beta\). This gives the displayed flag form.

3. **Patch for Lemma 7.1.**  
   Since \(\delta\) is an order-reversing isomorphism between rank-\(n\) geometric lattices, it sends rank \(s\) flats to rank \(n-s\) flats. Thus \(Z=\delta(a\vee b)\) has rank \(n-2\). The atoms on the line \(a\vee b\) in \(N'\) correspond under \(\delta\) to hyperplanes of \(N\) containing \(Z\), i.e. to atoms of \(\operatorname{si}(N/Z)\). The three distinct atoms \(a,b,c\) therefore give three distinct atoms of the simple rank-two matroid \(\operatorname{si}(N/Z)\). In a simple rank-two matroid, any two distinct atoms are independent and any three distinct atoms are dependent, so those three atoms form a circuit.

4. **Patch for Section 10 Plücker verification.**  
   Let \(N\) be any rank-\(n\) matroid and \(p_N\) its \(0/\infty\)-basis valuation. In a three-term Plücker relation, if no displayed sum is finite, there is nothing to prove. If one sum is finite, then the common \((n-2)\)-set \(S\) is independent, and after contracting \(S\) the assertion reduces to rank two. Suppose, in the rank-two contraction, \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{c,d\}\) are bases. Then none of \(a,b,c,d\) is a loop, \(a\) is not parallel to \(b\), and \(c\) is not parallel to \(d\). If both cross matchings failed, then one of \(a\parallel c\) or \(b\parallel d\) would hold, and one of \(a\parallel d\) or \(b\parallel c\) would hold. Each of the four resulting combinations forces either \(a\parallel b\) or \(c\parallel d\), contradiction. Hence at least one other matching also consists of two bases, so the minimum is not uniquely finite.

   Also, in the final dependent-\(J\) step, add: if \(|J|=f\) were a basis of \(N_{F^\perp}\), then
   \[
   r_M(F^\perp\cup J)=r.
   \]
   But \(r_M(F^\perp\cup J)\le r_M(F^\perp)+r_M(J)=(r-f)+r_M(J)\), so \(r_M(J)\ge f=|J|\), forcing \(J\) to be \(M\)-independent.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

<PROBLEM_SOLVED>
With the patches inserted, the proof establishes the stated partial theorem: for finite simple modular \(M\) with an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\), every rank-\(k\) valuated quotient satisfying the given tropical Plücker and \(M\)-circuit-incidence relations is infinite on \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-sets and constant on bases of each rank-\(k\) flat. The formula \(p^\dagger_J=\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)\) on independent \((r-k)\)-sets defines a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated quotient, descends to tropical projective classes, is involutive, and sends \(p^F\) to \(p^{F^\perp}\). It does not prove order-reversal for arbitrary inclusions in the full relative Dressian.
</PROBLEM_SOLVED>

\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

solution_verified:
## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on a finite ground set \(E\), with lattice of flats \(L(M)\). Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), and for a valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), let \(\Trop(p)\subseteq \mathbb T^E/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1)\) denote its tropical linear space. Let \(\Trop(M)\) mean the tropical linear space of the constant valuated matroid associated to \(M\). Define the relative Dressian
\[
\Dr(M)=\{\Trop(p): p \text{ is a valuated matroid quotient of } M\},
\]
ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. Equivalently, these are exactly the tropical linear spaces contained in \(\Trop(M)\). Suppose \(L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp.
\]
For each flat \(F\in L(M)\), let \(U_{0,F}\) denote the rank-zero matroid on \(F\), viewed as adding the elements of \(F\) as loops, so that
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
is a matroid on the original ground set \(E\). Then there exists an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\to \Dr(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\in L(M)\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This is exactly the original question, under the standard finite-ground-set convention used for Dressians and valuated matroids; no weakening is made.

## Proof

### 1. Valuated-matroid conventions and standard facts

Let \(E\) be finite. A rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom{E}{k}\to \mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations: for every \(S\in\binom{E}{k-2}\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\in E\setminus S\),
\[
\min\bigl(
p_{S ab}+p_{S cd},\,
p_{S ac}+p_{S bd},\,
p_{S ad}+p_{S bc}
\bigr)
\]
is attained at least twice. We use the convention that “the minimum is attained at least twice” means “there is no unique finite minimum”; thus if all relevant terms are \(\infty\), the relation is satisfied.

The tropical linear space \(\Trop(p)\) is the set of \(x\in \mathbb T^E/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1)\) such that for every \(J\in\binom{E}{k+1}\),
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

We use the following standard valuated-matroid facts.

1. **Relative spaces are valuated quotients.** If \(M\) is a matroid, regarded as the constant valuated matroid, then tropical linear subspaces of \(\Trop(M)\) are precisely the tropical linear spaces \(\Trop(p)\), where \(p\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

2. **Inclusion is the quotient relation.** If \(p,q\) are valuated matroids of ranks \(k\le \ell\), then
   \[
   \Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(q)
   \]
   if and only if \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\). In coordinates, this quotient relation is given by the valuated flag incidence relations
   \[
   \min_{i\in B\setminus A}
   \bigl(
   p_{A\cup i}+q_{B\setminus i}
   \bigr)
   \]
   having no unique finite minimum for all \(|A|=k-1\) and \(|B|=\ell+1\).

3. **Uniqueness of Plücker coordinates.** A tropical linear space determines its valuated Plücker vector up to adding a global scalar. Hence any construction on valuated Plücker vectors that is invariant under global scalar addition gives a well-defined construction on tropical linear spaces.

4. **Circuit form for quotients of a constant matroid.** If \(p\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of a matroid \(M\), then for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\),
   \[
   \min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
   \]
   has no unique finite minimum, where \(p_{A\cup c}=\infty\) if \(c\in A\). Conversely, these circuit-incidence relations characterize valuated quotients of \(M\).

5. **Boolean annihilator duality.** If \(\mu\) is an ordinary valuated matroid on a finite set \(\Omega\), and \(\mu^*\) is its valuated dual, then for \(u\in\mathbb T^\Omega\),
   \[
   u\in \Trop(\mu^*)
   \]
   if and only if
   \[
   \Trop(\mu)\subseteq
   H_u:=\left\{v\in\mathbb T^\Omega:
   \min_{\omega\in\Omega}(u_\omega+v_\omega)
   \text{ has no unique finite minimum}
   \right\}.
   \]

We now prove the theorem.

---

### 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
be the set of loops of \(M\). Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\), obtained by deleting loops and replacing each non-loop parallel class by one element. Let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map to the set \(\bar E\) of non-loop parallel classes.

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\), and if it contains one element of a parallel class, it contains the whole parallel class. Hence flats of \(M\) are exactly the sets
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F),
\]
where \(\bar F\) is a flat of \(S\). Thus there is a canonical lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0).
\]
The given involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) on \(L(M)\) therefore induces an order-reversing involution
\[
\bar F\longmapsto \bar F^\perp
\]
on \(L(S)\).

Define the diagonal map
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)}, & e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
Loops of \(M\) force the corresponding coordinates of every point of \(\Trop(M)\) to be \(\infty\). If \(e,f\) are parallel non-loops, then \(\{e,f\}\) is a circuit, so every point of \(\Trop(M)\) satisfies \(x_e=x_f\). Conversely, after imposing these equations, the remaining circuit conditions are precisely those for the simplification \(S\). Therefore
\[
\Trop(M)=\Delta(\Trop(S)).
\]

Now let \(p\) be a valuated quotient of \(M\). Since \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(M)\), every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(p\), and each original parallel class of \(M\) remains either entirely looped or a valuated parallel class with zero internal offset. Hence \(p\) descends uniquely to a valuated quotient \(\bar p\) of \(S\), and
\[
\Trop(p)=\Delta(\Trop(\bar p)).
\]
Conversely, every valuated quotient of \(S\) pulls back by adding the loops \(L_0\) and replacing each element of \(\bar E\) by a zero-offset parallel class. Thus \(\Delta\) gives a canonical poset isomorphism
\[
\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

The flat subposet is compatible with this identification. If \(F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F)\), then
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
is the zero-offset parallel extension of
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]

Therefore it suffices to prove the theorem when \(M\) is loopless and simple. At the end we transfer the resulting involution back through \(\Delta\).

---

### 3. The simple case is modular

Assume now that \(M\) is simple. Then \(L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice: it is ranked, atomic, and semimodular.

Because \(L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution, \(L(M)\) is isomorphic to its opposite lattice. Since \(L(M)\) is semimodular, its opposite lattice is also semimodular. Equivalently, \(L(M)\) is both upper and lower semimodular. In rank notation this means that for all flats \(X,Y\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}(X)+\operatorname{rk}(Y)
\ge
\operatorname{rk}(X\wedge Y)+\operatorname{rk}(X\vee Y),
\]
and also the reverse inequality. Therefore equality holds:
\[
\operatorname{rk}(X)+\operatorname{rk}(Y)
=
\operatorname{rk}(X\wedge Y)+\operatorname{rk}(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus \(L(M)\) is modular, and \(M\) is a simple modular matroid.

Henceforth, in the main construction, \(M\) is assumed simple modular of rank
\[
r=\operatorname{rk}M.
\]

---

### 4. Flat-constant coordinates for quotients of a modular matroid

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). We first prove that \(p\) is constant on \(M\)-bases of the same flat.

Let \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) be \(M\)-dependent. Choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then
\[
C\setminus A=\{c\}.
\]
The circuit-incidence relation for \(p\) over \(M\) forces \(p_I=\infty\). Thus finite \(p\)-bases are \(M\)-independent.

Now suppose \(I=A\cup\{a\}\) and \(I'=A\cup\{b\}\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with the same closure:
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I').
\]
Then \(a\in \operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{b\})\), so there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). The circuit-incidence relation gives
\[
\min(p_{A\cup a},p_{A\cup b})
\]
with no unique finite minimum. Hence
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b},
\]
allowing the common value \(\infty\).

The basis graph of a matroid restriction \(M|X\) is connected. Therefore, for every rank-\(k\) flat \(X\), the value \(p_I\) is independent of the choice of \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(X\). Define
\[
\bar p(X):=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(X\). If all such \(I\) have value \(\infty\), then \(\bar p(X)=\infty\).

---

### 5. The polar Plücker vector

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Define a function \(p^\dagger\) on \((r-k)\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),
& J \text{ is } M\text{-independent},\\[2mm]
\infty,
& J \text{ is } M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(J\) is \(M\)-independent of size \(r-k\), \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\) has rank \(r-k\), so \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\) has rank \(k\), and \(\bar p\) is defined there.

We now record the main technical lemma.

#### Lemma 5.1 — polar coordinates define a quotient

Let \(M\) be simple modular of rank \(r\) with order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) on \(L(M)\). If \(p\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), then:

1. \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid.
2. \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).
3. \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), up to the usual global additive scalar.
4. If \(p\) is the constant valuated matroid of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid of \(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}\).

##### Proof

We prove the four assertions.

First, we check the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Put
\[
m=r-k.
\]
Let \(S\in\binom{E}{m-2}\), and let \(a,b,c,d\in E\setminus S\) be distinct. If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every term in the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is \(\infty\), so the relation is automatic. Assume \(S\) is \(M\)-independent and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\).

If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every Plücker matching contains a dependent set, so all three Plücker sums are \(\infty\). Thus assume none lies in \(X\). For \(i\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_i=Y\wedge i^\perp=(X\vee i)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). If \(H_i=H_j\), then \(S\cup\{i,j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, and the corresponding \(p^\dagger\)-coordinate is \(\infty\). If \(H_i\neq H_j\), modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(H_i\wedge H_j)=k,
\]
and
\[
p^\dagger_{Sij}
=
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j).
\]

Therefore the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is equivalent to the following local statement.

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat, and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\), possibly repeated. Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\, [13]+[24],\, [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

If two \(H_i\)'s are equal, two of the three displayed sums are identical and the third contains an \(\infty\)-term, so the relation is immediate. Assume the \(H_i\) are distinct. Put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since \(Y\) has rank \(k+2\), modularity implies
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then all pairwise meets \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equal \(Z\), so all three sums are equal.

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
Then each \(T_i\) has rank \(k-1\), and for \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n.
\]
Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose elements \(t_i\in T_i\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{t_i\})=T_i.
\]
The ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4
\]
is exactly the desired relation.

Finally suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). Contract \(Z\) inside \(Y\). The interval \([Z,Y]\) becomes a rank-three simple modular matroid \(P\). The hyperplanes \(H_i/Z\) become rank-two flats, and the pairwise meets \(H_i\wedge H_j\) become points of \(P\). The function
\[
w(x)=\bar p(Z\vee x)
\]
on points \(x\) of \(P\) lies in \(\Trop(P)\), by the circuit-incidence relations for \(p\). Thus it remains only to know the following rank-three fact:

> If \(P\) is a simple modular rank-three matroid, \(w\in\Trop(P)\), and \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) are rank-two flats of \(P\), then
> \[
> \min\bigl(
> w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\,
> w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\,
> w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
> \bigr)
> \]
> has no unique finite minimum.

This rank-three statement is elementary. If two \(L_i\)'s coincide, it is immediate. Otherwise, if \(P\) is connected, it is a projective plane. By the fine Bergman fan description, after adding a constant,
\[
w=\lambda \mathbf 1_a+\mu \mathbf 1_B
\]
for some point-line flag \(a\le B\) and \(\lambda,\mu\ge0\). Intersect each \(L_i\) with \(B\). The three displayed sums become the three perfect-matching costs on four colored objects, where a pair contributes only when the two colors agree. For four objects, the minimum of the three perfect-matching costs is never unique. If \(P\) is disconnected, it is either Boolean of rank three or a direct sum of a point and a rank-two line; in both cases the same assertion is an immediate check from the circuit condition on the rank-two line. This proves the local relation, and hence the Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\).

Next we verify that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). We use the circuit-incidence characterization. Let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(m-1\), and let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p\bigl((X\vee c)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(Y\wedge c^\perp).
\]
The map \(Z\mapsto Z^\perp\) anti-identifies the interval \([X,E]\), which is the flat lattice of \(M/X\), with the interval \([0,Y]\), which is the flat lattice of \(M|Y\). On every rank-two flat of \(M/X\), the above values are precisely the codimension-one polar of the restriction of \(p\) to the corresponding corank-two pencil in \(Y\). The circuit-incidence relations for \(p\) say exactly that these values have no unique finite minimum on every circuit of that rank-two flat. Since \(M/X\) is modular, membership in \(\Trop(M/X)\) is detected on rank-two flats. Hence the function
\[
c\longmapsto p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
lies in \(\Trop(M/X)\). Therefore its minimum on \(C\setminus X\), and hence on \(C\setminus A\), has no unique finite minimum. Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

The involutivity is immediate from the definition. If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent of size \(k\), then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p\bigl((\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(I))
=
p_I.
\]
If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, both sides are \(\infty\). Thus
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
up to the global scalar ambiguity of valuated Plücker coordinates.

Finally consider a flat \(F\). Let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
A rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}F)\) flat \(G\) has finite \(\bar p^F(G)\) precisely when \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\):
\[
G\vee F=E,
\qquad
G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Because \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an order-reversing lattice involution, \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) if and only if \(G^\perp\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\). Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

---

### 6. Polar bases

We now prove the order-reversing property.

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define its polar basis by
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Because \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is an atom, hence an element of \(E\). Modularity implies that \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis.

More precisely, for every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]
Indeed, the flats generated by subsets of the basis \(B\) form a Boolean sublattice, and polarity turns meets into joins. Thus
\[
\bigvee_{i\in S} b_i^*
=
\bigvee_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp
=
\left(
\bigwedge_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})
\right)^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Its restriction to the Boolean apartment \(B\) is the rank-\(k\) valuated matroid
\[
(p|_B)_T=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Since \(B\) spans \(M\), it spans every quotient of \(M\), so \(p|_B\) has rank \(k\).

Now restrict \(p^\dagger\) to the polar basis \(B^*\). If \(|S|=r-k\), then
\[
(p^\dagger|_{B^*})_S
=
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^{\perp}
\right).
\]
Using the polar-basis identity,
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}.
\]
Therefore
\[
(p^\dagger|_{B^*})_S
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin S\}}.
\]
This is exactly the ordinary valuated dual of \(p|_B\):
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=(p|_B)^*.
\]

---

### 7. The point-annihilator theorem

Let \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Define a rank-\((r-1)\) quotient \(\alpha(x)\) of \(M\) by
\[
\alpha(x)_I=
\begin{cases}
x_{\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp},
& I\in\binom{E}{r-1}\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,
& I\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Equivalently, if \(H\) is a hyperplane of \(M\), then the coordinate of \(\alpha(x)\) at \(H\) is
\[
\alpha(x)_H=x_{H^\perp}.
\]
This is the special case \(k=1\) of the operation \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\).

We prove the key characterization.

#### Theorem 7.1 — point-annihilator characterization

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and let \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Then
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)
\]
if and only if
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

##### Proof

First assume \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\). Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis, and let \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) be the polar basis. Define
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*})\in\mathbb T^B.
\]
Since \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\), its restriction to \(B^*\) lies in
\[
\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
=
\Trop((p|_B)^*).
\]
By ordinary Boolean annihilator duality,
\[
\Trop(p|_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]

Let \(C_A\) be a valuated cocircuit of \(p\), indexed by a \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\):
\[
(C_A)_e=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
Valuated cocircuits lie in \(\Trop(p)\). Hence the restriction \(C_A|_B\) lies in \(\Trop(p|_B)\). Therefore
\[
\min_{i=1}^r\bigl((C_A)_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

But this is exactly the basis-incidence equation saying that \(C_A\in\Trop(\alpha(x))\). Since this holds for every valuated cocircuit \(C_A\), and since \(\Trop(p)\) is the tropical convex hull of its valuated cocircuits, we get
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

Conversely, assume
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), with polar basis \(B^*\), and again set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We claim that
\[
u_B\in \Trop((p|_B)^*).
\]
By Boolean annihilator duality, it suffices to show that \(u_B\) is tropically orthogonal to every valuated cocircuit of \(p|_B\).

A valuated cocircuit of \(p|_B\) has the form
\[
D_S=(p_{S\cup b_i})_{i=1}^r
\]
for some \((k-1)\)-subset \(S\subseteq B\), again with the convention that the coordinate is \(\infty\) if \(b_i\in S\). This is the restriction to \(B\) of the corresponding valuated cocircuit \(C_S\) of \(p\). Since
\[
C_S\in\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)),
\]
testing membership in \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i=1}^r\bigl(p_{S\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum. Thus \(D_S\) is orthogonal to \(u_B\), and hence
\[
u_B\in\Trop((p|_B)^*)
=
\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*}).
\]

Now let \(J\subseteq E\) be \(M\)-independent of size \(r-k+1\). Extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(B^*\). Since every \(M\)-basis is the polar basis of another \(M\)-basis, the preceding paragraph applies. Thus the ordinary circuit equation for \(J\) inside the Boolean apartment \(B^*\) gives
\[
\min_{j\in J}
\bigl(
x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}
\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, then either every \(J\setminus j\) is \(M\)-dependent, in which case all terms are \(\infty\), or \(J\) has rank \(r-k\) and contains a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). In the latter case, the finite \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\)-terms occur exactly for \(j\in C\), and they are all equal because the flats \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus j)\) are the same. Since \(x\in\Trop(M)\), the minimum of \(x_j\) on the circuit \(C\) has no unique finite minimum. Hence the circuit equation for \(p^\dagger\) again holds.

Therefore all defining equations for \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\) hold, and so
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
This proves the theorem. \(\square\)

---

### 8. Construction of the involution in the simple modular case

For \(X\in\Dr(M)\), choose a valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\) such that
\[
X=\Trop(p).
\]
Define
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
This is well-defined because \(p\) is determined by \(X\) up to adding a global scalar, and the construction \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) adds the same scalar.

By Lemma 5.1,
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p,
\]
so \(\Phi\) is an involution.

Now suppose
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(q).
\]
Let \(x\in\Trop(q^\dagger)\). By Theorem 7.1,
\[
\Trop(q)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Since \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(q)\), we get
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Applying Theorem 7.1 again gives
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus
\[
\Trop(q^\dagger)\subseteq\Trop(p^\dagger),
\]
so \(\Phi\) is order-reversing.

Finally, Lemma 5.1 gives, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Therefore \(\Phi\) extends the prescribed flat-lattice involution.

The boundary ranks are included. The unique rank-\(r\) quotient of \(M\) is \(M\) itself, and its polar is the rank-zero all-loop matroid; conversely, the polar of the rank-zero all-loop matroid is \(M\). Thus \(\Phi\) exchanges the top and bottom elements of \(\Dr(M)\).

---

### 9. Returning to the original matroid

For a general finite matroid \(M\), let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). We proved above that
\[
\Delta:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]
is a canonical poset isomorphism compatible with the embedded flat subposets and with the induced polarities on \(L(S)\) and \(L(M)\).

Construct the order-reversing involution \(\Phi_S\) on \(\Dr(S)\) by the simple modular argument. Then define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta^{-1}.
\]
Because \(\Delta\) is a poset isomorphism, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). Compatibility of \(\Delta\) with flat quotients gives, for every flat \(F\in L(M)\),
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Hence the answer to the question is affirmative.

- [Named/standard result: valuated quotients ⇔ inclusions of tropical linear spaces] VERIFIED — Brandt–Eur–Zhang, *Tropical flag varieties*, Theorem 4.3.1 states that a valuated matroid quotient is equivalent to inclusion of the corresponding projective tropical linear spaces; the same passage identifies the tropical incidence-Plücker relations used in the proof. Hypotheses match the finite common-ground-set setting. ([par.nsf.gov](https://par.nsf.gov/servlets/purl/10391296))
- [Named/standard result: tropical linear spaces cut out by circuits and spanned by cocircuits] VERIFIED — Theorem B in the same paper gives the circuit-prevariety and cocircuit-span characterizations; the explicit cocircuit formula also matches the proof’s \(C_A(e)=p_{A\cup e}\). ([par.nsf.gov](https://par.nsf.gov/servlets/purl/10391296))
- [Named/standard result: Plücker coordinates determined by tropical linear space up to scalar] VERIFIED — standard references state that tropical linear spaces \(L(p)\) and \(L(q)\) are equal iff \(p,q\) differ by a global additive scalar, and that \(L_p\) determines \(p\) up to scalar. ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001870818304584?utm_source=openai))
- [Named/standard result: fine Bergman fan description] VERIFIED — standard references state that the fine subdivision of a Bergman fan has rays indexed by flats and cones corresponding to chains of flats; this supports the rank-three use of \(w=c+\lambda\mathbf 1_a+\mu\mathbf 1_B\). ([link.springer.com](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00208-021-02281-9?utm_source=openai))
- [Named/standard result: finite upper+lower semimodular lattice is modular] VERIFIED — standard lattice references state that geometric lattices are finite atomistic semimodular lattices and that a finite lattice is modular iff it is both upper and lower semimodular. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Semimodular_lattice))
- [Named result: “Boolean annihilator duality”] NOT FOUND AS NAMED — I did not find this exact named theorem as a standard citation. The statement is nevertheless derivable from the verified circuit/cocircuit descriptions and cocircuit-span theorem above.

parsed.minor_gaps:
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The proof lists several “standard valuated-matroid facts” without citations or derivations, especially quotient/inclusion equivalence, cocircuit span, circuit cutting, uniqueness of Plücker coordinates, and the circuit-incidence characterization for quotients of a constant matroid.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The reduction to the simplification asserts, but does not prove, that every valuated quotient of \(M\) descends uniquely to the simplification with zero offsets on parallel classes.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Lemma 5.1 is the central technical step; its Plücker verification relies on an unproved rank-three matching fact, and its proof that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\) is too compressed.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The polar-basis section omits details showing that \(B^*\) is a basis, that every basis is polar to another, and that \(p|_B\) has rank \(k\) for every \(M\)-basis \(B\).
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The point-annihilator theorem omits checks for dependent defining \(r\)-subsets in \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\), and it uses the cocircuit tropical-convex-hull theorem and “Boolean annihilator duality” without proof.
- EXPOSITION ONLY — Boundary ranks and global-scalar well-definedness should be stated explicitly, but the intended arguments are straightforward once the cited uniqueness theorem is inserted.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Insert references/derivations for the standard valuated-matroid facts.**  
   Use the projective tropical-linear-space convention. The inclusion/quotient equivalence is exactly: for valuated matroids \(\mu,\nu\) on the same finite ground set, \(\mu\) is a valuated quotient of \(\nu\) iff \(\Trop(\mu)\subseteq\Trop(\nu)\). The incidence relations in the proof are the corresponding tropical incidence-Plücker relations. The circuit form for quotients of a constant matroid \(M\) follows by applying the circuit description of \(\Trop(M)\) to every valuated cocircuit \(C_A^*(p)\), where \(C_A^*(p)_e=p_{A\cup e}\). Since \(\Trop(p)\) is the tropical span of its valuated cocircuits, requiring all \(C_A^*(p)\in\Trop(M)\) is equivalent to \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(M)\).

2. **Patch the simplification reduction.**  
   Let \(L_0\) be the loop set and let \(e,f\) be parallel nonloops of \(M\). If \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\), then for any \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\):
   - the loop circuit \(\{e\}\) forces \(p_{A\cup e}=\infty\), so loops of \(M\) are loops of \(p\);
   - the circuit \(\{e,f\}\) forces \(p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}\) whenever \(A\cap\{e,f\}=\varnothing\), and if \(A\) contains one of \(e,f\), the relevant \(k\)-set is \(M\)-dependent and hence has value \(\infty\).
   Thus each parallel class of \(M\) is either entirely looped in \(p\), or remains a zero-offset parallel class. Hence \(p\) descends to a unique valuated quotient \(\bar p\) of \(\operatorname{si}(M)\), and conversely every quotient of \(\operatorname{si}(M)\) pulls back by adding \(L_0\) as loops and replacing each simplified element by a zero-offset parallel class. This proves the claimed poset isomorphism \(\Dr(\operatorname{si}(M))\cong\Dr(M)\).

3. **Patch Lemma 5.1, Plücker part.**  
   The missing rank-three fact can be inserted as follows. Let \(P\) be a simple modular rank-three matroid and \(w\in\Trop(P)\). If \(P\) is connected, then \(P\) is a projective plane. By the fine Bergman fan description, after adding a constant,
   \[
   w=\lambda\mathbf 1_a+\mu\mathbf 1_B
   \]
   for a point-line flag \(a\le B\) and \(\lambda,\mu\ge0\). For four lines \(L_1,\dots,L_4\), put \(P_{ij}=L_i\cap L_j\) when \(L_i\ne L_j\). If no \(L_i\) equals \(B\), color \(L_i\) by \(\gamma_i=L_i\cap B\). Then
   \[
   w(P_{ij})=\lambda\mathbf 1_{\gamma_i=\gamma_j=a}+\mu\mathbf 1_{\gamma_i=\gamma_j}.
   \]
   Thus the three matching sums are weighted same-color matching costs on four colored objects. By checking the possible color multiplicities \(4\), \(3+1\), \(2+2\), \(2+1+1\), and \(1+1+1+1\), the minimum is never unique. If one \(L_i=B\), each matching contains exactly one pair involving \(B\), contributing the same \(\mu\); subtract this common term and apply the same color-multiplicity check, with the \(\lambda\)-term detecting whether the other line passes through \(a\). If two \(L_i\)’s coincide, the proof’s repeated-line argument already applies. If \(P\) is disconnected, then \(P\cong U_{1,1}\oplus U_{2,n}\) or \(U_{1,1}^{\oplus3}\); in the first case the only nontrivial circuits lie on the \(U_{2,n}\)-line, and the same three-case check gives the matching relation, while in the Boolean case repetitions among the three rank-two flats force a tie.

4. **Patch Lemma 5.1, quotient part.**  
   Add the lemma: in a modular matroid \(N\), a vector \(z\) lies in \(\Trop(N)\) iff its restriction to every rank-two flat of \(N\) lies in that rank-two Bergman fan. Proof: if the rank-two conditions hold, then every upper level set \(\{e:z_e\ge t\}\) is closed under lines; in a modular geometry, line-closed sets are flats. Hence no circuit can have a unique minimum.  
   Now fix \(A\) of size \(m-1=r-k-1\), set \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), \(Y=X^\perp\), and consider
   \[
   f(c)=p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=\bar p(Y\wedge c^\perp)
   \quad(c\notin X).
   \]
   To show \(f\in\Trop(M/X)\), check a rank-two flat \(W/X\). Let \(Z=W^\perp\), a rank-\((k-1)\) flat in \(Y\). For any three distinct atoms \(R_1,R_2,R_3\) of \(W/X\), their polars \(R_i^\perp\) are the rank-\(k\) flats between \(Z\) and \(Y\). Choose a basis \(A_0\) of \(Z\) and elements \(y_i\in R_i^\perp\setminus Z\). In \((M|Y)/Z\), the \(y_i\) form a rank-two circuit, so there is an \(M\)-circuit \(D\subseteq Z\cup\{y_1,y_2,y_3\}\) whose elements outside \(Z\) are exactly \(y_1,y_2,y_3\). The quotient circuit relation for \(p\) with \(A_0\) gives
   \[
   \min_i p_{A_0\cup y_i}
   \]
   with no unique finite minimum, i.e.
   \[
   \min_i \bar p(R_i^\perp)
   \]
   with no unique finite minimum. Parallel pairs are even easier because the two corresponding polars are equal. Therefore \(f\in\Trop(M/X)\), which is precisely the circuit-incidence condition for \(p^\dagger\) to be a quotient of \(M\).

5. **Patch the polar-basis section.**  
   For an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), the flats generated by subsets of \(B\) form a Boolean sublattice:
   \[
   \operatorname{cl}(B_S)\wedge\operatorname{cl}(B_T)=\operatorname{cl}(B_{S\cap T}),\qquad
   \operatorname{cl}(B_S)\vee\operatorname{cl}(B_T)=\operatorname{cl}(B_{S\cup T}).
   \]
   Applying the polarity gives
   \[
   \operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
   =
   \operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
   \]
   For \(S=\{1,\dots,r\}\), the right-hand side is \(E\), so \(B^*\) spans; for \(|S|=s\), it has rank \(s\), so \(B^*\) is independent and hence a basis. The same identity also shows that the polar-basis construction is involutive, so every basis is the polar basis of another.  
   Finally, if \(N\) is the underlying matroid of a quotient \(p\) of \(M\), every flat of \(N\) is a flat of \(M\). Thus an \(M\)-basis \(B\) cannot be contained in a proper \(N\)-flat, so \(B\) spans \(N\), and \(p|_B\) has rank \(k\).

6. **Patch the point-annihilator theorem.**  
   First prove the Boolean annihilator statement directly: for an ordinary valuated matroid \(\mu\) on \(\Omega\),
   \[
   u\in\Trop(\mu^*)
   \]
   iff \(u\) is tropically orthogonal to every valuated cocircuit of \(\mu\). Since \(\Trop(\mu)\) is the tropical span of those cocircuits and a tropical hyperplane is tropically convex, this is equivalent to \(\Trop(\mu)\subseteq H_u\).  
   In the forward direction of Theorem 7.1, after checking the basis equations for \(C_A\in\Trop(\alpha(x))\), also check dependent \(r\)-subsets \(J\). If \(\operatorname{rk}J<r-1\), all relevant \(\alpha(x)\)-coordinates are \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}J=r-1\), then \(J\) has a unique circuit \(C\), the finite terms occur exactly for \(j\in C\), and the \(\alpha(x)_{J\setminus j}\) are all equal. Since \(C_A\in\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(M)\), the minimum of \((C_A)_j\) on \(C\) is not unique. Thus \(C_A\in\Trop(\alpha(x))\).  
   In the reverse direction, the proof’s independent-\(J\) argument via Boolean apartments is valid after adding the polar-basis patch. For dependent \(J\) of size \(r-k+1\), the same unique-circuit argument applies: if \(\operatorname{rk}J<r-k\), all terms are \(\infty\); if \(\operatorname{rk}J=r-k\), the finite \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\)-terms occur exactly on the unique circuit \(C\subseteq J\) and are equal, while \(x\in\Trop(M)\) gives no unique minimum on \(C\).
parsed.problem_solved: After inserting the patches above and working under the standard finite-ground-set/projective tropical linear-space convention, the argument proves the claimed affirmative result: if the lattice of flats of \(M\) has an order-reversing involution, then \(\Dr(M)\), ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces, has an order-reversing involution extending \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) on the embedded flat subposet.

original_problem:
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?
