original_problem:
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?
claim_verified: The proof establishes, modulo routine details, that the flat-lattice involution forces the simplification of \(M\) to be modular; it defines a formal dagger operation on valuated quotients in the simple modular case; it proves involutivity of this formal operation as a function-level construction; and it sketches an annihilator argument that would yield order reversal if \(p^\dagger\) were known to be a valuated matroid quotient. However, the proof does not actually establish that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid, so it does not prove the claimed order-reversing involution on \(\Dr(M)\).
solution_verified:
## Claim

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid with ground set \(E\), and suppose its lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Then there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\longrightarrow \Dr(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

We prove this from first principles, using the standard equivalence between valuated matroids and tropical Plücker vectors.

---

## 1. Conventions

Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), with min-plus conventions.

A rank \(d\) valuated matroid on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom Ed\to \mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), whose finite support is the set of bases of an ordinary matroid and which satisfies the three-term tropical Plücker relations:
for every \(S\in \binom E{d-2}\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\notin S\),
\[
\min\left(
p_{Sab}+p_{Scd},\,
p_{Sac}+p_{Sbd},\,
p_{Sad}+p_{Sbc}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. We set \(p_I=\infty\) if \(|I|\neq d\).

The tropical linear space of \(p\) is
\[
\Trop(p)=
\left\{
x\in \mathbb{TP}^{E-1}:
\text{ for every }J\in\binom E{d+1},
\min_{j\in J}(x_j+p_{J-j})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

For an ordinary matroid \(M\) of rank \(r\), we identify \(M\) with its constant valuated matroid:
\[
M_B=
\begin{cases}
0,&B\text{ a basis of }M,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

A rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\) iff
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(M).
\]
Equivalently, for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. We will call this the circuit-incidence condition.

---

## 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops, and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\). Thus the elements of \(S\) are the nonloop parallel classes of \(M\).

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\) and is a union of parallel classes. Hence there is a canonical lattice isomorphism
\[
\mathcal L(M)\cong \mathcal L(S).
\]
The involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) therefore induces an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\).

Let \(\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E\) be the projection to nonloop parallel classes. Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,&e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},&e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(\Delta\) descends to tropical projective space.

If \(q\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\), then \(\Trop(q)\subseteq \Trop(M)\). The equations defining \(\Trop(M)\) force:

1. every loop of \(M\) to be a loop of \(q\);
2. elements in the same nonloop parallel class of \(M\) to be either all loops of \(q\), or valuated-parallel with zero offset.

Indeed, if a loop \(e\) of \(M\) were nonloop in \(q\), some valuated cocircuit of \(q\) would have finite \(e\)-coordinate, giving a point of \(\Trop(q)\) with \(x_e<\infty\), contradicting \(\Trop(q)\subseteq\{x_e=\infty\}\). Similarly, if \(e,f\) are parallel in \(M\), then \(\Trop(M)\) imposes \(x_e=x_f\). If \(e,f\) were separated in \(q\), a cocircuit of \(q\) would separate their coordinates; if they were valuated-parallel with nonzero offset, their two-element circuit would impose \(x_e-x_f\neq 0\). Both contradict \(x_e=x_f\) on \(\Trop(q)\).

Thus every quotient of \(M\) descends uniquely to a quotient of \(S\), and conversely every quotient of \(S\) has a zero-parallel extension to \(M\). Therefore
\[
\Delta:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]
is a poset isomorphism.

Moreover, if \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\), then
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Hence it suffices to prove the theorem for simple matroids.

---

## 3. The simple case is modular

Assume now that \(M\) is simple of rank \(r\). Its lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice. Since \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution, it is isomorphic to its dual lattice. Hence both \(\mathcal L(M)\) and its dual are semimodular.

Let \(\rho\) be the rank function. Semimodularity gives
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Semimodularity of the dual gives the reverse inequality. Therefore
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
=
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Thus \(M\) is modular.

From now on, \(M\) is simple modular of rank \(r\), equipped with a polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\). Then
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]

---

## 4. Flat-constancy of valuated quotients

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

### Lemma 4.1

If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is dependent in \(M\), then \(p_I=\infty\).

**Proof.** Choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\), and pick \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I-c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\). The circuit-incidence condition says that
\[
\min_{x\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup x}=p_I
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(p_I=\infty\). ∎

### Lemma 4.2

If \(I,I'\in\binom Ek\) are \(M\)-bases of the same rank \(k\) flat \(F\), then
\[
p_I=p_{I'}.
\]

**Proof.** It is enough to consider the case
\[
I=A\cup\{a\},\qquad I'=A\cup\{b\}.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{cl}_M(Aa)=\operatorname{cl}_M(Ab)\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying the circuit-incidence condition to \(A\) and \(C\), we obtain that
\[
\min(p_{Aa},p_{Ab})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(p_{Aa}=p_{Ab}\), allowing the value \(\infty\).

The basis graph of the restriction \(M|F\) is connected, so the conclusion follows. ∎

Thus for every rank \(k\) flat \(F\), we may define
\[
\bar p(F)=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(F\).

---

## 5. The formal dagger operation

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Define a rank \(r-k\) function \(p^\dagger\) on \(E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),
&J\text{ is \(M\)-independent and }|J|=r-k,\\[3pt]
\infty,
&J\text{ is \(M\)-dependent and }|J|=r-k.
\end{cases}
\]

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is again a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Let \(N\) be the ordinary support matroid of \(p\). Since \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\). Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(A)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A))
\]
for all \(A\subseteq E\).

Set \(n=r-k\).

---

## 6. The support of \(p^\dagger\) is a matroid

For a flat \(G\in\mathcal L(M)\), define
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp).
\]
Extend this to arbitrary subsets by
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)=\rho^\dagger(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)).
\]

We check that \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function.

First,
\[
\rho^\dagger(\varnothing)
=
0-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(E)
=
0.
\]
Also
\[
\rho^\dagger(E)
=
r-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(\varnothing)
=
r-k=n.
\]

If \(G\subseteq H\) are flats, then \(H^\perp\subseteq G^\perp\). Since \(N\) is a quotient of \(M\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_M(H^\perp)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]
Therefore
\[
\rho^\dagger(H)-\rho^\dagger(G)\ge 0.
\]
Thus \(\rho^\dagger\) is monotone.

For submodularity, let \(G,H\) be flats. Since \(M\) is modular,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)+\operatorname{rk}_M(H)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G\wedge H)+\operatorname{rk}_M(G\vee H).
\]
Also, using submodularity of \(\operatorname{rk}_N\) and the quotient property,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)+\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\ge
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp\cap H^\perp)
+
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp\cup H^\perp),
\]
and
\[
G^\perp\cap H^\perp=(G\vee H)^\perp,
\]
while
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(G^\perp\cup H^\perp)
=
G^\perp\vee H^\perp
=
(G\wedge H)^\perp.
\]
Hence
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)+\rho^\dagger(H)
\ge
\rho^\dagger(G\wedge H)+\rho^\dagger(G\vee H).
\]
Thus \(\rho^\dagger\) is submodular. Since \(\rho^\dagger(A)\le \operatorname{rk}_M(A)\le |A|\) and adjoining one element increases \(\rho^\dagger\) by at most one, \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function.

Let \(N^\dagger\) be the resulting rank \(n\) matroid.

Now let \(J\in\binom En\). Then \(J\) is a basis of \(N^\dagger\) iff
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)=n.
\]
This forces \(J\) to be \(M\)-independent, and then
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)
=
n-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp).
\]
Thus \(\rho^\dagger(J)=n\) iff
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)=k.
\]
Equivalently, \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\) is a rank \(k\) flat on which \(\bar p\) is finite. Hence
\[
p^\dagger_J<\infty
\]
exactly for the bases \(J\) of \(N^\dagger\). Therefore the finite support of \(p^\dagger\) is the set of bases of a matroid.

---

## 7. The tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We prove the three-term tropical Plücker relations.

Let \(S\in\binom E{n-2}\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must show
\[
\min\left(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},\,
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},\,
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all three terms are \(\infty\). If \(S\) is independent but one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), then every displayed summand contains a dependent \(n\)-set, so again all three terms are \(\infty\).

Assume therefore that \(S\) is independent and that \(a,b,c,d\notin X:=\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\). Then
\[
\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-2.
\]
Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2.
\]
For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\).

If \(H_e=H_f\), then \(Sef\) is \(M\)-dependent, so \(p^\dagger_{Sef}=\infty\). If \(H_e\neq H_f\), then modularity gives
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]

Thus the desired Plücker relation follows from the following local statement.

### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min([12]+[34],\,[13]+[24],\,[14]+[23])
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]+[24]=[14]+[23].
\]
So the claim is immediate.

Assume the \(H_i\) are distinct. Put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since \(Y\) has rank \(k+2\), one has
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then all pairwise intersections \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equal \(Z\), so all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\). Work inside the rank-four interval \([Z,Y]\). For \(i=1,2,3,4\), define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
Each \(T_i\) has rank \(k-1\). Indeed, in the rank-four interval, the intersection of three hyperplanes has rank at least one, and if it had rank at least two then intersecting with the fourth hyperplane could not give the bottom element \(Z\), by modularity.

For \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n.
\]
Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i.
\]
Then
\[
[12]=p_{A t_3 t_4},\qquad [34]=p_{A t_1 t_2},
\]
and similarly for the other two pairings. Therefore the required relation is exactly the tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\).

It remains to consider \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\) has rank three. Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) with \(Z<P<Y\). Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim that \(w\in\Trop([Z,Y])\), where \([Z,Y]\) is regarded as the rank-three simple matroid whose flats are the interval flats.

Let \(D\) be a circuit in this interval. Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). For each point \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). By the standard circuit-lifting property for contractions, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
whose image in the contraction by \(Z\) is exactly \(D\). Applying the circuit-incidence condition for \(p\) to \(A\) and \(C\), the elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), and we obtain that
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(w\in\Trop([Z,Y])\).

Now use the following rank-three fact.

If \(N\) is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid, \(w\in\Trop(N)\), and \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) are distinct lines of \(N\), then
\[
\min\bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\,
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\,
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Indeed, for a vector \(w\) on the points of a matroid, \(w\in\Trop(N)\) iff every superlevel set
\[
\{x:w_x\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat. In rank three, the nontrivial superlevel flats form a flag
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a line. After subtracting the minimum value of \(w\), we may write
\[
w(x)=\lambda\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\mu\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}
\]
for some \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\), allowing \(\lambda=0\) or \(\mu=0\).

If none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), put \(y_i=L_i\wedge B\). Then \(L_i\wedge L_j\le B\) exactly when \(y_i=y_j\). Thus a matching receives positive cost only from pairs of equal colors \(y_i\). Among four colored objects, the minimum total matching cost is never unique. If one line, say \(L_1\), equals \(B\), then the three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),\quad
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),\quad
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2),
\]
where \(c(a)=\lambda+\mu\) and \(c(y)=\mu\) for \(y\neq a\). A direct check according to the multiplicities of \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) shows that the minimum is again attained at least twice.

Applying this rank-three fact to the interval \([Z,Y]\) proves the final case, and hence the local lemma. ∎

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations. Since its finite support is the set of bases of the matroid \(N^\dagger\), \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

---

## 8. \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\)

We must prove the circuit-incidence condition for \(p^\dagger\).

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\). We need
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
to have no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-1\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\]
so \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

For every point \(P\) of the contraction \(M/X\), define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Here \(P\) is a rank \(n\) flat containing \(X\), and \(P^\perp\) is a rank \(k\) flat contained in \(Y\).

We claim that
\[
u\in\Trop(M/X).
\]

It is enough to show that every superlevel set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(M/X\). Since \(M/X\) is modular, it is enough to show that \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed.

Take \(P_1,P_2\in \mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(M/X\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
Then the \(H_i\) are hyperplanes of \(Y\), and \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\) implies
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), then \(u(P_3)=u(P_1)\) or \(u(P_2)\), so \(P_3\in\mathcal S_\lambda\).

Otherwise \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) are distinct hyperplanes of the rank \(k+1\) flat \(Y\). Their pairwise intersections have rank \(k-1\). Choose a basis \(B\) of \(H_1\wedge H_2\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two interval over \(H_1\wedge H_2\), the three points \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit. Lifting this circuit to \(M\) and applying the circuit-incidence condition for \(p\) to \(B\), we get that
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Hence \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, and therefore a flat.

Thus \(u\in\Trop(M/X)\).

Now return to the circuit \(C\). If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms in the desired minimum are \(\infty\). Otherwise, for every \(c\in C\setminus X\), the image of \(c\) in \(M/X\) lies in a circuit contained in the image of \(C\setminus X\). Since \(u\in\Trop(M/X)\), the minimum of \(u\) on \(C\setminus X\) has no unique finite minimizer. This is precisely the circuit-incidence condition for \(p^\dagger\). Hence
\[
\Trop(p^\dagger)\subseteq \Trop(M).
\]

So \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

## 9. Involutivity of dagger

We prove
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, both sides are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus dagger is an involution on valuated quotients of \(M\).

---

## 10. Flat quotients

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\). Let
\[
p^F
\]
be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\).

Let \(G\) be a rank \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat of \(M\). Then
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
iff an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis of \(M/F\), equivalently iff
\[
G\vee F=E.
\]
Since \(M\) is modular and
\[
\operatorname{rk}(G)+\operatorname{rk}(F)=r,
\]
this is equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Thus \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) precisely when \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\).

Polarity exchanges complements. Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Trop((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

## 11. The annihilator theorem

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define the polar basis
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp.
\]
Because \(M\) is modular, for every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^\perp.
\]
Thus \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis.

For a rank \(k\) quotient \(p\), define a valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \(\{1,\dots,r\}\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Because \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), every \(M\)-basis contains a basis of the support matroid of \(p\), so \(\mu_B\) has rank \(k\).

The restriction of dagger to the polar apartment is ordinary valuated duality:
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]
Indeed, for \(|S|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^\perp
\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}
\right)
=
\mu_B(\{1,\dots,r\}\setminus S).
\]

Now let \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Since \(x\) is a rank-one valuated quotient of \(M\), define its polar hyperplane to be the rank \(r-1\) quotient
\[
x^\perp:=x^\dagger.
\]

We prove the key equivalence:
\[
\boxed{
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(x^\perp).
}
\]

For an ordinary valuated matroid \(\mu\) on a finite set \(B\), the standard valuated annihilator theorem says
\[
u\in\Trop(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Trop(\mu)\subseteq H_u,
\]
where
\[
H_u=\{y:\min_i(u_i+y_i)\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
\]
This follows because the valuated circuits of \(\mu^*\) are the valuated cocircuits of \(\mu\), and \(\Trop(\mu)\) is the tropical convex span of its valuated cocircuits.

### Forward implication

Assume \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Since \(p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*\), we have
\[
u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*).
\]
Hence
\[
\Trop(\mu_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]

Let \(y\in\Trop(p)\). Then \(y|_B\in\Trop(\mu_B)\), so
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. But
\[
x^\perp_{B-b_i}=x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Thus the tropical hyperplane equation for \(x^\perp\) holds on the basis \(B\).

If \(K\in\binom Er\) is \(M\)-dependent, then either \(\operatorname{rk}_M(K)\le r-2\), in which case all terms are \(\infty\), or \(\operatorname{rk}_M(K)=r-1\), in which case \(K\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms in the equation for \(x^\perp\) occur exactly for \(i\in D\), and for those \(i\), the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(K-i)\) is constant. The equation therefore reduces to the circuit equation for \(y\in\Trop(M)\), which holds.

Hence \(y\in\Trop(x^\perp)\). Since \(y\) was arbitrary,
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp).
\]

### Reverse implication

Assume
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We show \(u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*)\).

By the ordinary annihilator theorem, it is enough to test orthogonality against the valuated cocircuits of \(\mu_B\). Let \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(|S|=k-1\), and put
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The corresponding cocircuit of \(\mu_B\) is
\[
i\longmapsto p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
This is the restriction to \(B\) of the valuated cocircuit
\[
e\longmapsto p_{A\cup e}
\]
of \(p\). If it is nonzero, it lies in \(\Trop(p)\), hence in \(\Trop(x^\perp)\). Testing the equation for \(x^\perp\) on the basis \(B\), we get
\[
\min_i(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*)\).

Because \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on the \(M\)-bases, this means that \(x\) restricts to \(\Trop(p^\dagger)\) on every Boolean apartment.

Now let \(J\in\binom E{r-k+1}\). We verify the equation for \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis and use the apartment calculation. If \(J\) is dependent with \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)\le r-k-1\), all terms are \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\), and the finite terms occur exactly for \(j\in D\). For those \(j\), the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J-j)\) is constant, so the equation reduces to the circuit equation for \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Hence
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]

This proves the annihilator equivalence.

---

## 12. Construction of the involution in the simple modular case

For \(X\in\Dr(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(X)
=
\{x\in\Trop(M):X\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp)\}.
\]
If \(X=\Trop(p)\), then by the annihilator theorem,
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
Hence \(\Phi(X)\in\Dr(M)\), and the definition depends only on \(X\), not on the chosen valuated Plücker vector \(p\).

Since \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\),
\[
\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]

If \(X\subseteq Y\), then
\[
Y\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp)
\implies
X\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp),
\]
so
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq\Phi(X).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is order-reversing.

Finally, by the flat quotient computation,
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\).

This proves the theorem when \(M\) is simple.

---

## 13. Transport back to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). From Section 2,
\[
\Delta:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]
is a poset isomorphism, and the given involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) induces a polarity on \(\mathcal L(S)\).

By the simple case, there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S)
\]
extending the induced polarity.

Define
\[
\Phi_M:\Dr(M)\to\Dr(M)
\]
by
\[
\Phi_M(\Delta(X))=\Delta(\Phi_S(X)).
\]
Because \(\Delta\) is a poset isomorphism, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution.

If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\), then
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Hence \(\Phi_M\) is the desired order-reversing involution on \(\Dr(M)\). ∎

1. **Quotient/circuit-incidence criterion.**  
For a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(p\), its valuated cocircuits are
\[
C_A(e)=p_{A\cup e},\qquad |A|=k-1.
\]
For an ordinary matroid \(M\),
\[
\Trop(M)=\bigcap_{C\in\mathcal C(M)}
\{x:\min_{c\in C}x_c\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
\]
Since \(\Trop(p)\) is the tropical convex hull of the valuated cocircuits of \(p\), and each circuit hyperplane of \(M\) is tropically convex, \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(M)\) is equivalent to requiring every cocircuit \(C_A\) to satisfy every \(M\)-circuit equation:
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. This is exactly the circuit-incidence condition.

2. **Support quotient.**  
Let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\). The support-level circuit-incidence condition implies that every \(M\)-circuit is a union of \(N\)-circuits. Indeed, if some \(e\in C\) were a coloop of \(N|C\), choose an \(N\)-hyperplane \(H\) containing \(C-e\) but not \(e\), and choose an \(N\)-basis \(A\) of \(H\). Then \(A\cup e\) is an \(N\)-basis, while \(A\cup c\) is \(N\)-dependent for all \(c\in C-e\), contradicting circuit-incidence. Hence \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\), equivalently
\[
r_N(A)=r_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A))
\]
for all \(A\subseteq E\).

3. **Simplification reduction.**  
If \(e\) is an \(M\)-loop, apply circuit-incidence to the circuit \(\{e\}\); this forces \(p_{A\cup e}=\infty\) for all \(A\), so \(e\) is a loop of every quotient \(p\). If \(e,f\) are \(M\)-parallel, apply circuit-incidence to \(\{e,f\}\). For every \((k-1)\)-set \(A\) disjoint from \(\{e,f\}\),
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}.
\]
Thus a quotient is constant on each nonloop parallel class, with zero valuated offset, or the whole class becomes loops. This gives a well-defined quotient on the simplification, and conversely the zero-parallel extension of a quotient of \(\operatorname{si}(M)\) is a quotient of \(M\). Therefore the map \(\Delta:\Dr(\operatorname{si}(M))\to\Dr(M)\) is a poset isomorphism.

4. **Rank function \(\rho^\dagger\).**  
After proving \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\), the inequality
\[
r_N(G^\perp)-r_N(H^\perp)
\le r_M(G^\perp)-r_M(H^\perp)
\]
for \(G\subseteq H\) follows from the strong-map quotient property. For arbitrary subsets \(A,B\), apply the flat inequality to \(G=\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), \(H=\operatorname{cl}_M(B)\), and use
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup B)=G\vee H,\qquad
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cap B)\subseteq G\wedge H.
\]
This gives submodularity for all subsets. Also \(\rho^\dagger(\emptyset)=0\), monotonicity gives nonnegativity, and
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)\le r_M(A)\le |A|.
\]
Submodularity plus \(\rho^\dagger(\{e\})\le1\) gives
\[
\rho^\dagger(A\cup e)-\rho^\dagger(A)\le1.
\]
Thus \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function.

5. **Superlevel-flat criterion.**  
For a vector \(w\) on a simple matroid \(N\),
\[
w\in\Trop(N)
\]
iff every superlevel set \(\{e:w_e\ge\lambda\}\) is a flat. If a superlevel set is not flat, some circuit has a unique element below level \(\lambda\), giving a unique minimum. Conversely, a circuit with unique minimum produces a non-flat superlevel set just above that minimum.

6. **Line-closed sets in modular geometries.**  
Let \(G\) be a simple modular matroid and \(S\) a line-closed set of points. Prove \(S\) is a flat by induction on \(r(\operatorname{cl}S)\). Choose a basis \(a_1,\dots,a_m\subseteq S\), let \(H=\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1})\), and use induction to get all points of \(H\) in \(S\). For any point \(x\in\operatorname{cl}(S)\setminus H\), modularity of the hyperplane \(H\) implies the line \(\operatorname{cl}(a_m,x)\) meets \(H\) in a point \(h\). Since \(h,a_m\in S\) and \(S\) is line-closed, \(x\in S\). Hence \(S\) is flat.

7. **Contraction circuit step in Section 8.**  
If \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit not contained in \(X\), set \(D=C\setminus X\). For each \(c\in D\),
\[
r_{M/X}(D-c)
= r_M(X\cup C-c)-r_M(X)
= r_M(X\cup C)-r_M(X)
= r_{M/X}(D),
\]
because \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C-c)\). Thus \(c\) is not a coloop of \((M/X)|D\), so \(c\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\). Hence a unique minimum of \(u\) on \(D\) would restrict to a unique minimum on one such circuit, contradiction.

8. **Ordinary valuated annihilator theorem.**  
For an ordinary valuated matroid \(\mu\),
\[
u\in\Trop(\mu^*)
\iff
u\perp C\text{ for every valuated cocircuit }C\text{ of }\mu
\iff
\Trop(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
The first equivalence is the circuit description of \(\Trop(\mu^*)\), since cocircuits of \(\mu\) are circuits of \(\mu^*\). The second follows because \(\Trop(\mu)\) is the tropical convex hull of the cocircuits of \(\mu\), and a tropical hyperplane \(H_u\) is tropically convex.

9. **Apartment verification in Section 11.**  
For every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_i\}\), prove explicitly that
\[
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]
Then \(B^*\) is a basis and \((B^*)^*=B\), so \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on \(M\)-bases. Restricting \(p^\dagger\) to \(B^*\) gives the ordinary dual \(\mu_B^*\). The dependent \(r\)-set and \((r-k+1)\)-set cases reduce to ordinary \(M\)-circuit equations because the only finite Plücker terms occur on the unique circuit of the dependent set; if the common coefficient is \(\infty\), the equation is trivial.

10. **Edge cases.**  
If \(k=0\) or \(k=r\), the relevant Plücker or quotient conditions are vacuous or reduce to the unique rank \(0\) or rank \(r\) quotient. If an auxiliary vector \(w\) or \(u\) is identically \(\infty\), all minima in the relevant local relations have no unique finite minimizer, so the local arguments are trivial.

- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Section 2’s simplification reduction is plausible but not fully rigorous as written; the descent of valuated quotients through loops and zero-parallel classes needs an explicit Plücker/quotient check.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Section 6’s proof that \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function omits details for arbitrary subsets, one-element increments, and the ordinary quotient property of the support matroid \(N\).
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Section 7’s proof that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies tropical Plücker relations uses several nontrivial local geometric facts; they are correct but should be stated as lemmas with proofs, including the all-\(\infty\) and edge-rank cases.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Section 8’s proof that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\) needs details for “line-closed implies flat” in modular contractions and for the assertion that \(C\setminus X\) is covered by circuits in \(M/X\).
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Section 11’s annihilator argument needs the precise ordinary valuated annihilator theorem, plus verification that restriction to Boolean apartments and the dependent \(r\)-set cases really test all equations.

1. **Quotient/circuit-incidence criterion.**

\]
For an ordinary matroid \(M\),
\[
\Trop(M)=\bigcap_{C\in\mathcal C(M)}
\{x:\min_{c\in C}x_c\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
\]
Since \(\Trop(p)\) is the tropical convex hull of the valuated cocircuits of \(p\), and each circuit hyperplane of \(M\) is tropically convex, \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(M)\) is equivalent to requiring every cocircuit \(C_A\) to satisfy every \(M\)-circuit equation:
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. This is exactly the circuit-incidence condition.

original_problem:
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?
claim_verified: With the listed routine patches and standard valuated-matroid background inserted, the proof establishes the stated finite-ground-set/projective-convention theorem: if the flat lattice of a finite matroid \(M\) has an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\), then the poset of tropical linear spaces contained in \(\Trop(M)\), ordered by inclusion, admits an order-reversing involution \(\Phi\) that sends each embedded flat object \(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\) to \(\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})\).
solution_verified:
## Theorem

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on \(E\). Work in the projective tropical torus with boundary
\[
\mathbb{TP}^{E}
=\bigl((\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^{E}\setminus\{(\infty,\ldots,\infty)\}\bigr)/\mathbb R(1,\ldots,1).
\]
Let \(\Dr(M)\) be the poset of tropical linear spaces contained in \(\Trop(M)\), ordered by inclusion. Suppose the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Then \(\Dr(M)\) has an order-reversing involution \(\Phi\) such that, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Here \(U_{0,F}\) denotes the rank-zero matroid on \(F\), so \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is viewed as a matroid on the original ground set \(E\).

---

## 1. Valuated-matroid facts used throughout

Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). A rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom Ek\to \mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations. We use the convention that a minimum is “attained at least twice” if there is no unique finite minimizing term. We also allow the formal all-\(\infty\) vector in intermediate affine calculations; it satisfies every “no unique finite minimum” condition, although it is not a point of tropical projective space.

For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\), its tropical linear space is
\[
\Trop(p)
=
\left\{
x\in\mathbb{TP}^{E}:
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimum for every }J\in\binom E{k+1}
\right\}.
\]
For \(k=0\), this space is empty in the above projective convention.

For \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\), define the formal valuated cocircuit
\[
C_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]

We use the following standard valuated-matroid facts.

### Lemma 1.1: Quotients, inclusion, and annihilators

Let \(p,q\) be valuated matroids on the same finite set \(E\), of ranks \(k\le \ell\).

1. \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\) if and only if
   \[
   \Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(q).
   \]
   Equivalently, the tropical incidence relations
   \[
   \min_{i\in B\setminus A}
   \bigl(p_{A\cup i}+q_{B\setminus i}\bigr)
   \]
   have no unique finite minimum for every
   \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\) and \(B\in\binom E{\ell+1}\).

2. If \(q=M\) is an ordinary matroid, viewed as the constant valuated matroid with values \(0,\infty\), then \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\) if and only if, for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\),
   \[
   \min_{c\in C} C_A^p(c)
   =
   \min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
   \]
   has no unique finite minimum.

3. If \(\mu\) is a valuated matroid on a finite set \(\Omega\), with dual \(\mu^*\), and
   \[
   H_u=
   \left\{
   v\in\mathbb{TP}^{\Omega}:
   \min_{\omega\in\Omega}(u_\omega+v_\omega)
   \text{ has no unique finite minimum}
   \right\},
   \]
   then
   \[
   u\in\Trop(\mu^*)
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   \Trop(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
   \]

4. If \(p\) is a valuated quotient of an ordinary matroid \(M\), then every \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-subset \(I\) satisfies \(p_I=\infty\). Moreover, if \(N\) is the underlying matroid of \(p\), then every flat of \(N\) is a flat of \(M\).

5. If \(R\subseteq E\) spans the underlying matroid of \(p\), so that \(p|_R\) has rank \(k\), then for every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\), the restriction \(C_A^p|_R\) satisfies the tropical linear-space equations of \(p|_R\).

Sketch of proof. The cocircuit-generation theorem for valuated matroids says that \(\Trop(p)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of the valuated cocircuits \(C_A^p\). Thus \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(q)\) is equivalent to every \(C_A^p\) satisfying the equations of \(\Trop(q)\), which are exactly the displayed incidence relations. For ordinary \(M\), the equations of \(\Trop(M)\) are precisely the Bergman circuit equations, giving (2). For (3), \(u\in\Trop(\mu^*)\) means that \(u\) is tropically orthogonal to every valuated cocircuit of \(\mu\); by cocircuit generation, this is equivalent to \(\Trop(\mu)\subseteq H_u\). For (4), if \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). With \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\), the circuit relation for \(C\) has only the term \(p_I\), so \(p_I=\infty\). If \(F\) is a flat of the underlying matroid \(N\) of \(p\), \(e\notin F\), and \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_M(F)\), choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq F\cup\{e\}\) containing \(e\). Let \(I\) be an \(N\)-basis of \(F\); since \(F\) is \(N\)-flat, \(I\cup\{e\}\) is \(N\)-independent, and it extends to an \(N\)-basis \(B\). Put \(A=B\setminus\{e\}\). In the circuit relation for \(C\), the term indexed by \(e\) is finite, while every term indexed by \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq F\) is infinite, contradiction. Thus \(F\) is \(M\)-closed. Finally, (5) is the Plücker incidence relation of \(p\) with itself, restricted to subsets of \(R\). \(\square\)

---

## 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the loop set of \(M\), and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\). Let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the map sending a nonloop to its parallel class.

If \(\operatorname{rk}(M)=0\), then \(\Trop(M)=\varnothing\), \(\Dr(M)=\{\varnothing\}\), and the theorem is immediate. Hence assume \(\operatorname{rk}(M)>0\).

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\) and is a union of parallel classes, so
\[
F\longmapsto \bar F:=\pi(F\setminus L_0)
\]
is a lattice isomorphism
\[
\mathcal L(M)\cong \mathcal L(S).
\]
Thus the given polarity induces an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\).

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb{TP}^{\bar E}\to\mathbb{TP}^{E}
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
The loop circuits \(\{e\}\) force \(x_e=\infty\), and the parallel-pair circuits \(\{e,f\}\) force \(x_e=x_f\). Therefore
\[
\Trop(M)=\Delta(\Trop(S)).
\]

Now let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, Lemma 1.1 gives \(p_I=\infty\). In particular, loops of \(M\) are loops of \(p\). If \(e,f\) are parallel nonloops in \(M\), then \(\{e,f\}\) is an \(M\)-circuit, and the quotient circuit relation gives
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}
\]
for all \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\) with \(A\cap\{e,f\}=\varnothing\), with both sides possibly \(\infty\). Thus \(p\) is constant on choices of representatives from a parallel class and is infinite on \(k\)-sets containing two elements from the same parallel class.

Hence \(p\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar p\) on \(\bar E\), defined by
\[
\bar p_{\bar I}=p_I
\]
for any choice \(I\) of representatives of the classes \(\bar I\). The Plücker relations and the quotient circuit relations for \(\bar p\) are exactly the corresponding relations for \(p\) after choosing representatives, so \(\bar p\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\).

Conversely, every valuated quotient \(\bar p\) of \(S\) pulls back to a valuated quotient of \(M\) by adding the elements of \(L_0\) as loops and replacing every element of \(\bar E\) by a zero-offset parallel class. The two operations are inverse, and
\[
\Trop(p)=\Delta(\Trop(\bar p)).
\]
Therefore \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

Moreover, if \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\), then the ordinary quotient \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) descends to
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Thus
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]
It is therefore enough to prove the theorem when \(M\) is loopless and simple. We do so from now on.

---

## 3. The simple case is modular

Assume \(M\) is simple of rank \(r\). Its lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice. Let \(\rho\) denote rank, and let \(\hat 0=\varnothing\), \(\hat 1=E\).

An order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) sends the interval \([\hat 0,F]\) anti-isomorphically onto \([F^\perp,\hat 1]\). Since \(\mathcal L(M)\) is graded,
\[
\rho(F)=r-\rho(F^\perp),
\]
so
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]

The lattice \(\mathcal L(M)\) is semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives
\[
\rho(X^\perp)+\rho(Y^\perp)
\ge
\rho(X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp)+\rho(X^\perp\vee Y^\perp).
\]
Using rank reversal and De Morgan’s laws, this becomes
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\le
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus equality holds:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
=
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Hence \(M\) is modular.

---

## 4. Flat-constancy of quotients in the simple modular case

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of the simple modular matroid \(M\).

If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\) by Lemma 1.1.

Now suppose \(I=A\cup\{a\}\) and \(I'=A\cup\{b\}\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I').
\]
Then \(b\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{a\})\) but \(b\notin\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), so the fundamental circuit of \(b\) with respect to \(A\cup\{a\}\) is contained in \(A\cup\{a,b\}\) and contains both \(a\) and \(b\). The quotient circuit relation for that circuit gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Since the basis graph of every restriction \(M|X\) is connected, it follows that \(p_I\) depends only on the flat \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\).

Thus, for every rank-\(k\) flat \(X\), define
\[
\bar p(X):=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(X\). This is well-defined, possibly with value \(\infty\).

---

## 5. Two local lemmas for modular matroids

### Lemma 5.1: Rank-two testing

Let \(N\) be a finite modular matroid. Let \(z\in\mathbb T^{E(N)}\) be a formal vector such that \(z_e=\infty\) for loops and \(z\) is constant on parallel classes. Then \(z\) satisfies the Bergman circuit conditions of \(N\) if and only if its restriction to every rank-two flat of \(N\) satisfies the rank-two Bergman circuit conditions.

Proof. Because \(z\) is infinite on loops and constant on parallel classes, we may pass to the simplification of \(N\). Thus assume \(N\) is simple and loopless.

For each real \(t\), set
\[
U_t=\{e\in E(N): z_e\ge t\}.
\]
A vector satisfies the Bergman circuit conditions if and only if every \(U_t\) is a flat. Indeed, if \(U_t\) is not closed, then there is a circuit \(C\subseteq U_t\cup\{e\}\) with \(e\notin U_t\); then \(e\) is the unique finite minimum of \(z\) on \(C\). Conversely, if a circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\), choose \(t\) strictly between \(z_e\) and the second-smallest value on \(C\); then \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq U_t\) but \(e\notin U_t\), so \(U_t\) is not closed.

The rank-two hypotheses say precisely that every \(U_t\) is closed under lines: if two atoms of a rank-two flat lie in \(U_t\), then every atom on that line lies in \(U_t\).

It remains to use modularity. Let \(U\) be a line-closed set of atoms, and let \(F=\bigvee U\). We show every atom \(e\le F\) lies in \(U\). Choose a minimal subset \(\{a_1,\dots,a_m\}\subseteq U\) with
\[
e\le a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_m.
\]
By minimality, the \(a_i\) are independent. If \(m\le 2\), then line-closure gives \(e\in U\). Suppose \(m>2\). Let
\[
A=a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_{m-1},\qquad b=a_m.
\]
Then \(e\not\le A\), \(b\not\le A\), and \(e\le A\vee b\). Modularity gives
\[
\rho\bigl(A\wedge(e\vee b)\bigr)
=
\rho(A)+\rho(e\vee b)-\rho(A\vee b)
=
(m-1)+2-m
=
1.
\]
Thus \(A\wedge(e\vee b)\) is an atom \(y\le A\). By induction on \(m\), \(y\in U\). Since \(y,b\in U\) and \(e\le y\vee b\), line-closure gives \(e\in U\). Hence every line-closed set of atoms is the set of atoms of a flat, proving the lemma. \(\square\)

---

### Lemma 5.2: A rank-three matching lemma

Let \(P\) be a simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\mathbb T^{E(P)}\) satisfy the Bergman circuit conditions of \(P\). Let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be rank-two flats of \(P\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),& L_i\ne L_j,\\
\infty,& L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\,[13]+[24],\,[14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

Proof. If two of the \(L_i\) coincide, then two of the three displayed sums are equal and the remaining sum contains an \(\infty\)-term, so the claim is immediate. Assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If \(P\) is disconnected, then either
\[
P\cong U_{1,1}^{\oplus 3}
\]
or
\[
P\cong U_{1,1}\oplus U_{2,n}.
\]
In the first case there are only three rank-two flats, contradicting distinctness of the four \(L_i\). In the second case, let \(a\) be the rank-one component and let \(Q\) be the rank-two component. The rank-two flats are \(Q\) and the flats \(a\vee x\) for \(x\in Q\). If none of the \(L_i\) is \(Q\), all pairwise meets are \(a\), so all three sums are equal. If one of them is \(Q\), say \(L_1=Q\), then \(L_i=a\vee x_i\) for \(i=2,3,4\), and the three sums are
\[
w(x_2)+w(a),\qquad
w(x_3)+w(a),\qquad
w(x_4)+w(a).
\]
Since \(x_2,x_3,x_4\) form a circuit of the rank-two uniform component \(Q\), the minimum among \(w(x_2),w(x_3),w(x_4)\) is not unique.

Now assume \(P\) is connected. Then any two distinct lines meet in a point, because modularity gives
\[
\rho(L\wedge L')=\rho(L)+\rho(L')-\rho(L\vee L')=2+2-3=1.
\]
Thus \(P\) is a projective plane in the matroidal sense.

If \(w\) is identically \(\infty\), the result is trivial. Otherwise subtract \(\min w\), so \(\min w=0\). By Lemma 5.1, all upper level sets of \(w\) are flats. In a rank-three projective plane, a chain of proper flats has the form
\[
\varnothing \subseteq \{a\}\subseteq B\subseteq E
\]
for a point-line flag \(a\le B\). Therefore
\[
w=\lambda\mathbf 1_a+\mu\mathbf 1_B
\]
for some \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\).

If none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), let
\[
\gamma_i=L_i\cap B.
\]
Then
\[
w(L_i\cap L_j)
=
\mu\,\mathbf 1_{\gamma_i=\gamma_j}
+
\lambda\,\mathbf 1_{\gamma_i=\gamma_j=a}.
\]
Thus the three displayed sums are matching costs for four colored objects, where pairing two objects of different colors has cost \(0\), pairing two objects of the same color \(\gamma\ne a\) has cost \(\mu\), and pairing two objects of color \(a\) has cost \(\mu+\lambda\). Checking the possible color multiplicities \(4\), \(3+1\), \(2+2\), \(2+1+1\), and \(1+1+1+1\), the minimum is never unique.

If one of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\), write
\[
\gamma_i=L_i\cap B,\qquad i=2,3,4.
\]
If all three \(\gamma_i\) are equal, all three sums are equal. If exactly two are equal, then the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those two lines tie for the minimum. If the three \(\gamma_i\) are distinct, at most one equals \(a\), so at least two of the three sums have the same smallest value. This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

---

## 6. The polar transform on valuated quotients

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of the simple modular matroid \(M\). Put \(m=r-k\). Define a function \(p^\dagger\) on \(m\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(J\) is independent of size \(m\), the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\) has rank \(m\), and its polar has rank \(k\), so \(\bar p\) is defined there.

### Proposition 6.1

The function \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\(m\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Moreover,
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
as valuated matroids up to global tropical scalar. Finally, for every flat \(F\),
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp},
\]
where \(p^F\) denotes the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]

Proof. First, \(p^\dagger\) is not identically \(\infty\). Choose \(I\) with \(p_I<\infty\). Then \(I\) is \(M\)-independent. Let \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Choose a basis \(J\) of \(X^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(X)<\infty.
\]

The boundary cases are immediate. If \(k=0\), then \(p\) is the rank-zero all-loop valuated matroid, and \(p^\dagger\) is \(M\) up to scalar. If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is \(M\) up to scalar, and \(p^\dagger\) is rank zero. Hence assume \(1\le k\le r-1\).

We verify the three-term tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
S\in\binom E{m-2},\qquad a,b,c,d\in E\setminus S
\]
be distinct. If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all relevant \(p^\dagger\)-coordinates are \(\infty\). Suppose \(S\) is independent, and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S),\qquad Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(Y)=k+2\). If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every Plücker sum contains an \(\infty\)-term, so the relation holds. Thus assume none lies in \(X\).

For \(i\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_i=(X\vee i)^\perp=Y\wedge i^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). If \(H_i=H_j\), then \(S\cup\{i,j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, and therefore \(p^\dagger_{Sij}=\infty\). If \(H_i\ne H_j\), modularity gives
\[
\rho(H_i\wedge H_j)=k,
\]
and
\[
p^\dagger_{Sij}=\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j).
\]

Thus the Plücker relation reduces to the following local statement.

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat, and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Put
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\,[13]+[24],\,[14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

If two hyperplanes coincide, the claim is immediate. Assume they are distinct, and set
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since each \(H_i\) has corank one in \(Y\), and \(H_i\wedge H_j\) has rank \(k\) for \(i\ne j\), we have
\[
\rho(Z)\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}.
\]

If \(\rho(Z)=k\), then every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(Z\), so all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-2\). Define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i} H_j.
\]
Then each \(T_i\) has rank \(k-1\). For \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), one has
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n.
\]
Indeed, \(T_m,T_n\le H_i\wedge H_j\), their meet is \(Z\), and modularity gives
\[
\rho(T_m\vee T_n)
=
(k-1)+(k-1)-(k-2)
=
k
=
\rho(H_i\wedge H_j).
\]
Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}(A\cup\{t_i\})=T_i.
\]
The tropical Plücker relation for \(p\), applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\), is exactly the required relation.

Finally suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-1\). Consider the simplification \(P\) of \((M|Y)/Z\). It is a simple modular rank-three matroid. The hyperplanes \(H_i\) become rank-two flats of \(P\), and \(H_i\wedge H_j\) becomes their point of intersection. Define
\[
w(R)=\bar p(R)
\]
for every point \(R\) of \(P\), i.e. every rank-\(k\) flat \(R\) with \(Z<R<Y\).

We claim that \(w\) satisfies the Bergman circuit conditions of \(P\). Let \(R_1,\dots,R_s\) be a circuit of \(P\). Choose a basis \(A_0\) of \(Z\) and representatives \(y_i\in R_i\setminus Z\). The set \(\{y_1,\dots,y_s\}\) is a circuit of the simplification of \((M|Y)/Z\), so it lifts to an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq Z\cup\{y_1,\dots,y_s\}
\]
with \(D\setminus Z=\{y_1,\dots,y_s\}\). Applying the quotient circuit relation for \(p\) with \(A_0\), the terms from \(D\cap Z\) are \(\infty\), because \(A_0\) spans \(Z\), and the remaining terms are
\[
p_{A_0\cup y_i}=\bar p(R_i)=w(R_i).
\]
Thus \(w\) satisfies the circuit condition on \(R_1,\dots,R_s\). Hence \(w\in\Trop(P)\) in the affine sense. Lemma 5.2 gives the desired matching relation.

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations.

It remains to prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\). By Lemma 1.1, it is enough to verify the circuit quotient relations. Let
\[
A\in\binom E{m-1}.
\]
If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then all terms \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\) are \(\infty\). Suppose \(A\) is independent, and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),\qquad Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(Y)=k+1\). Define a formal vector \(f\) on \(M/X\) by
\[
f(c)=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& c\in X,\\
\bar p(Y\wedge c^\perp),& c\notin X.
\end{cases}
\]
For \(c\notin X\), this equals \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\).

We claim \(f\) satisfies the Bergman circuit conditions of \(M/X\). It is constant on parallel classes because parallel elements in \(M/X\) have the same flat \(X\vee c\). By Lemma 5.1, it suffices to check rank-two flats of \(M/X\).

Let \(W/X\) be a rank-two flat of \(M/X\), and put
\[
Z=W^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(Z)=k-1\). Let \(R_1,R_2,R_3\) be three distinct atoms of \(W/X\). Their polars \(R_i^\perp\) are rank-\(k\) flats between \(Z\) and \(Y\). Choose a basis \(A_0\) of \(Z\) and elements
\[
y_i\in R_i^\perp\setminus Z.
\]
In the rank-two matroid \((M|Y)/Z\), the elements \(y_1,y_2,y_3\) form a circuit. Therefore there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq Z\cup\{y_1,y_2,y_3\}
\]
with \(D\setminus Z=\{y_1,y_2,y_3\}\). Applying the quotient circuit relation for \(p\) with \(A_0\), the terms from \(Z\) are \(\infty\), and we obtain that
\[
\min_i \bar p(R_i^\perp)
\]
has no unique finite minimum. This is exactly the rank-two Bergman condition for \(f\) on \(W/X\). Thus \(f\in\Trop(M/X)\) in the affine sense.

Now let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. If \(C\subseteq X\), then all terms \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\) are \(\infty\). Otherwise \(C\setminus X\) is a dependent set in \(M/X\) with no coloops: for each \(c\in C\setminus X\),
\[
c\in \operatorname{cl}_{M/X}\bigl((C\setminus X)\setminus\{c\}\bigr).
\]
If \(f\) had a unique finite minimum on \(C\setminus X\), then that minimum element would lie in some circuit of \((M/X)|(C\setminus X)\), contradicting \(f\in\Trop(M/X)\). Hence the minimum of
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
over \(c\in C\) has no unique finite minimum. Therefore \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Now we prove involutivity. Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then both \(p_I\) and \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I\) are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is independent, then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(\operatorname{cl}(I)^\perp)
=
\bar p\bigl((\operatorname{cl}(I)^\perp)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}(I))
=
p_I.
\]
Thus \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), up to the irrelevant global tropical scalar.

Finally let \(F\) be a flat, and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\). A rank-\((r-\rho(F))\) flat \(G\) satisfies
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
if and only if \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\), namely
\[
G\vee F=E,\qquad G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Indeed, finite bases of \(p^F\) are precisely bases of the contraction \(M/F\), and modularity gives the meet condition from the rank count:
\[
\rho(G\wedge F)=\rho(G)+\rho(F)-\rho(E)=0.
\]
Polarity swaps joins and meets, so \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) if and only if \(G^\perp\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
\(\square\)

---

## 7. Polar bases

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_i\})\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is a rank-one flat, hence a unique element of \(E\), because \(M\) is simple.

For \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\), write
\[
B_S=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The flats
\[
F_S:=\operatorname{cl}(B_S)
\]
form a Boolean sublattice. Indeed,
\[
F_S\vee F_T=F_{S\cup T},
\]
and modularity gives
\[
\rho(F_S\wedge F_T)
=
|S|+|T|-|S\cup T|
=
|S\cap T|,
\]
so
\[
F_S\wedge F_T=F_{S\cap T}.
\]
Applying the polarity to this Boolean sublattice yields
\[
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
F_{\{1,\dots,r\}\setminus S}^{\perp}.
\]
In particular, this flat has rank \(|S|\), so
\[
B^*:=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis. The same identity shows that the polar-basis construction is involutive:
\[
(B^*)^*=B.
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Let \(N\) be its underlying matroid. By Lemma 1.1, every \(N\)-flat is an \(M\)-flat. Therefore an \(M\)-basis cannot be contained in a proper \(N\)-flat, so every \(M\)-basis spans \(N\). Hence \(p|_B\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid.

For \(|S|=r-k\),
\[
(p^\dagger|_{B^*})_S
=
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\left(
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^{\perp}
\right)
=
\bar p(F_{\{1,\dots,r\}\setminus S})
=
p_{B_{\{1,\dots,r\}\setminus S}}.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=(p|_B)^*.
\]

---

## 8. The point-annihilator theorem

For \(x\in\Trop(M)\), view \(x\) as a rank-one valuated quotient \(p_x\) of \(M\), with
\[
(p_x)_e=x_e.
\]
Define
\[
\alpha(x):=p_x^\dagger.
\]
Thus \(\alpha(x)\) is a rank-\((r-1)\) valuated quotient of \(M\), with
\[
\alpha(x)_I=
\begin{cases}
x_{\operatorname{cl}(I)^\perp},& I\in\binom E{r-1}\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& I\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

### Theorem 8.1

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and let \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Then
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

Proof. If \(k=0\), then \(\Trop(p)=\varnothing\), while \(p^\dagger=M\), so both sides are true.

If \(k=r\), then \(p=M\) up to scalar and \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero, so
\[
\Trop(p^\dagger)=\varnothing.
\]
Thus the left side is false. We show the right side is also false. Choose \(e\in E\) with \(x_e<\infty\), extend \(e\) to a basis \(B^*\), and let \(B=(B^*)^*\). Write \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) with \(b_i^*=e\). Let
\[
A=B\setminus\{b_i\}.
\]
The valuated cocircuit \(C_A^M\) is a point of \(\Trop(M)\). In the defining equation of \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\) indexed by the basis \(B\), all terms are \(\infty\) except the one indexed by \(b_i\), which equals \(x_e<\infty\). Hence \(C_A^M\notin\Trop(\alpha(x))\). Therefore
\[
\Trop(M)\nsubseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

Now assume \(1\le k\le r-1\).

First suppose \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\). Let
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
be an \(M\)-basis, and let \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) be the polar basis. Put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Since \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\),
\[
u_B\in\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
=
\Trop((p|_B)^*).
\]
By Boolean annihilator duality,
\[
\Trop(p|_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]

Fix \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\). By Lemma 1.1, the restriction \(C_A^p|_B\) satisfies the equations of \(\Trop(p|_B)\). Hence
\[
\min_{i=1}^r\bigl(C_A^p(b_i)+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum. Since
\[
\alpha(x)_{B\setminus b_i}
=
x_{\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus b_i)^\perp}
=
x_{b_i^*},
\]
this is exactly the equation of \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\) indexed by the basis \(B\) for the vector \(C_A^p\).

It remains to check equations indexed by dependent \(r\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\). If \(\rho(J)<r-1\), then every \(J\setminus j\) is dependent, so all corresponding \(\alpha(x)\)-coordinates are \(\infty\). If \(\rho(J)=r-1\), then \(J\) has a unique circuit \(C\). The finite \(\alpha(x)\)-terms occur exactly for \(j\in C\), and for those \(j\), the values \(\alpha(x)_{J\setminus j}\) are all equal. Because \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), the minimum of \(C_A^p\) on \(C\) has no unique finite minimum. Therefore \(C_A^p\) satisfies every defining equation of \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\).

Thus every valuated cocircuit of \(p\) lies in \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\). By Lemma 1.1,
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis, with polar basis \(B^*\), and put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We prove
\[
u_B\in\Trop((p|_B)^*).
\]
By Boolean annihilator duality, it suffices to check orthogonality to every valuated cocircuit of \(p|_B\). Such a cocircuit has the form
\[
D_S(b_i)=p_{S\cup b_i}
\]
for some \(S\subseteq B\), \(|S|=k-1\), and is the restriction of \(C_S^p\). Since \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x))\), the vector \(C_S^p\) satisfies the equations of \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\). Applying the equation indexed by the basis \(B\), we obtain
\[
\min_{i=1}^r \bigl(p_{S\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum. Thus \(D_S\) is tropically orthogonal to \(u_B\), proving
\[
u_B\in\Trop((p|_B)^*).
\]

Using
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=(p|_B)^*,
\]
we get
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
\]
for every \(M\)-basis \(B\). Since the polar-basis construction is involutive, every \(M\)-basis occurs as some \(B^*\).

Now let \(J\in\binom E{r-k+1}\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B^*\). The equation for \(J\) in \(\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*})\) gives
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\rho(J)<r-k\), then all terms \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\) are \(\infty\). If \(\rho(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique circuit \(C\). The finite terms occur exactly for \(j\in C\), and for those \(j\), the values \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\) are all equal. Since \(x\in\Trop(M)\), the minimum of \(x_j\) over \(j\in C\) has no unique finite minimum. Hence the defining equation holds for every \(J\), and therefore
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
\(\square\)

---

## 9. Construction of the involution in the simple modular case

For \(X\in\Dr(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(X)
=
\{x\in\Trop(M): X\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x))\}.
\]
If \(X=\Trop(p)\), Theorem 8.1 gives
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\Phi(X)\in\Dr(M)\).

Since \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), we get
\[
\Phi^2(X)=X.
\]
Moreover, if \(X\subseteq Y\), then
\[
Y\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x))
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
X\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)),
\]
so
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq\Phi(X).
\]
Therefore \(\Phi\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\).

Finally, for a flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Phi(\Trop(p^F))
=
\Trop((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(p^{F^\perp})
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Thus the theorem holds for simple \(M\).

---

## 10. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) be arbitrary finite, and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). Section 2 gives a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]
compatible with the embedded flat subposets. The polarity on \(\mathcal L(M)\) induces a polarity on \(\mathcal L(S)\), and the simple case gives an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S).
\]
Define
\[
\Phi_M:=\Delta\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\).

If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\), then
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr),
\]
and \(\bar F^\perp\) corresponds to \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This proves the theorem. \(\square\)

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Patch for Proposition 6.1: matroidal support of \(p^\dagger\).**

   Insert before concluding that the three-term checks make \(p^\dagger\) a valuated matroid.

   Let \(N\) be the underlying rank-\(k\) matroid of \(p\). Since \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\), \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\); in particular every \(N\)-flat is an \(M\)-flat, and the quotient rank inequality
   \[
   r_N(B)-r_N(A)\le r_M(B)-r_M(A)\qquad(A\subseteq B)
   \]
   holds. For an \(M\)-flat \(X\) of rank \(k\),
   \[
   \bar p(X)<\infty \iff r_N(X)=k.
   \]
   Indeed, \(\bar p(X)<\infty\) means that some/every \(M\)-basis of \(X\) is an \(N\)-basis; conversely, if \(r_N(X)=k\), an \(N\)-basis inside \(X\) is \(M\)-independent and spans \(X\) in \(M\).

   Define, for \(S\subseteq E\) and \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\),
   \[
   r^\dagger(S):=\rho_M(X)-k+r_N(X^\perp).
   \]
   This is a matroid rank function. Nonnegativity follows from the quotient rank inequality:
   \[
   k-r_N(X^\perp)\le r-\rho(X^\perp)=\rho(X).
   \]
   Also \(r^\dagger(S)\le \rho(X)\le |S|\). Monotonicity follows from the same quotient rank inequality. For submodularity, for \(M\)-flats \(X,Y\), put \(A=X^\perp\), \(B=Y^\perp\). Modularity of \(M\) cancels the \(\rho\)-terms, and De Morgan gives
   \[
   (X\vee Y)^\perp=A\cap B,\qquad
   (X\wedge Y)^\perp=A\vee_M B=\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup B).
   \]
   Since \(N\)-flats are \(M\)-flats,
   \[
   r_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup B))=r_N(A\cup B).
   \]
   Hence submodularity of \(r^\dagger\) reduces to submodularity of \(r_N\):
   \[
   r_N(A)+r_N(B)\ge r_N(A\cap B)+r_N(A\cup B).
   \]
   Thus \(r^\dagger\) defines a rank-\(m=r-k\) matroid \(N^\dagger\).

   For an \(m\)-subset \(J\), \(r^\dagger(J)=m\) iff \(J\) is \(M\)-independent and \(r_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)=k\), which is equivalent to
   \[
   p^\dagger_J=\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)<\infty.
   \]
   Therefore the finite support of \(p^\dagger\) is matroidal. Since the proof then verifies all short three-term tropical Plücker relations, the standard short-Plücker criterion with matroidal support implies that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

2. **Patch for modular minors.**

   Whenever the proof applies Lemma 5.1 or Lemma 5.2 to a minor such as \(M/X\) or \((M|Y)/Z\), insert: the lattice of flats of that minor is the interval \([X,E]\) or \([Z,Y]\) in \(\mathcal L(M)\). Intervals in a modular lattice are modular because the modular rank identity restricts to intervals. Simplification preserves the flat lattice up to the usual identification of parallel classes. Hence all minors/simplifications used there are finite modular matroids.

3. **Patch for all-\(\infty\) formal cocircuits in Theorem 8.1.**

   In the converse direction, replace “Since \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x))\), the vector \(C_S^p\) satisfies...” by:
   If \(C_S^p\) is the all-\(\infty\) vector, then its restriction \(D_S\) is all \(\infty\), so the required tropical orthogonality condition is automatic. Otherwise \(C_S^p\) represents a genuine projective point of \(\Trop(p)\), and the assumed inclusion implies that it satisfies the equations of \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\).

4. **Patch for Section 2 simplification.**

   For descent, after proving loops are loops and parallel elements have equal \(p\)-coordinates, explicitly define
   \[
   \bar p_{\bar I}=p_I
   \]
   for any choice of representatives. Any Plücker or quotient relation for \(\bar p\) lifts to the corresponding relation for \(p\) by choosing representatives; relations involving repeated representatives are automatic because the relevant \(p\)-coordinate is \(\infty\). Conversely, for a valuated quotient \(\bar p\) of \(S\), define the pullback \(\tilde p\) by assigning \(\infty\) to sets meeting \(L_0\) or containing two elements of the same parallel class, and otherwise setting \(\tilde p_I=\bar p_{\pi(I)}\). The Plücker and quotient relations either descend to those for \(\bar p\) or are forced by loop/parallel circuits. The equations for the tropical linear spaces then give exactly
   \[
   \Trop(\tilde p)=\Delta(\Trop(\bar p)).
   \]
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
