solution_verified:
## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

We prove the following self-contained finite-ground-set theorem.

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on a finite ground set \(E\). Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), with min-plus tropical conventions. For a valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), let \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\mathbb T^E\) denote its affine tropical linear space, including the all-\(\infty\) vector, and let
\[
\Trop(p)=(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\})/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1)
\]
be its projectivization, possibly empty. Regard an ordinary matroid \(M\) as its constant valuated matroid. Define
\[
\Dr(M)=\{\Trop(p): p\text{ is a valuated matroid on }E\text{ and }\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\},
\]
ordered by inclusion of projective tropical linear spaces. For a flat \(F\) of \(M\), let \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) mean the matroid on the original ground set \(E\) obtained by contracting \(F\) and making every element of \(F\) a loop; this gives the standard embedded flat subposet
\[
F\longmapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]

If the flat lattice \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp,
\]
then there exists an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\to\Dr(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

This is the original problem with the finite-ground-set convention made explicit; no other hypothesis is changed.

## Proof

### 1. Affine/projective conventions and standard valuated-matroid facts

Let \(E\) be finite and let
\[
\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}.
\]
We use min-plus conventions: tropical scalar multiplication is addition of a constant, and tropical addition is minimum. A statement that a minimum is “attained at least twice” always means “there is no unique finite minimizer”; in particular, a minimum whose every term is \(\infty\) satisfies the condition.

A rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom Ek\to\mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), whose finite support is the set of bases of an ordinary rank \(k\) matroid and whose Plücker coordinates satisfy the tropical Plücker relations. We extend \(p\) by \(p_I=\infty\) whenever \(|I|\ne k\).

The affine tropical linear space of \(p\) is
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in\mathbb T^E:
\forall J\in\binom E{k+1},\
\min_{j\in J}(x_j+p_{J-j})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
It always contains \(\infty^E=(\infty,\dots,\infty)\). Its projectivization is
\[
\Trop(p)=
(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\})/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1).
\]
If \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), then \(\Trop(p)=\varnothing\).

An ordinary matroid \(M\) of rank \(r\) is identified with the constant valuated matroid
\[
p^M_B=
\begin{cases}
0,&B\text{ is a basis of }M,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

We use the following standard facts about valuated matroids.

**Standard Fact 1: uniqueness.**  
The affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) determines the valuated matroid \(p\) up to adding one global scalar to all Plücker coordinates. Therefore \(\Trop(p)\) also determines \(p\) up to global scalar.

**Standard Fact 2: containment and quotients.**  
For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same finite ground set,
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
\]
if and only if \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\). In particular, a valuated matroid \(p\) is a quotient of an ordinary matroid \(M\) precisely when
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

For a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(p\), and a \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\), define the coordinate cocircuit
\[
C_A^p(e)=p_{A\cup e},
\]
with the convention that \(C_A^p(e)=\infty\) if \(e\in A\). Then \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), unless it is the all-\(\infty\) vector.

**Standard Fact 3: tropical span of cocircuits.**  
The affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) is the tropical convex span of its valuated cocircuits \(C_A^p\). Consequently, because ordinary tropical hyperplanes are tropically convex, for an ordinary matroid \(M\),
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)
\]
if and only if every coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) satisfies every circuit equation of \(M\). Equivalently, \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\) iff, for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
\tag{CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

We shall use this circuit-incidence criterion repeatedly.

**Standard Fact 4: ordinary annihilator duality.**  
Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\), and let \(\mu^*\) be its valuated dual:
\[
\mu^*_{N\setminus B}=\mu_B.
\]
For \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), define
\[
H_u=\left\{v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
This includes the boundary ranks: if \(\mu\) has rank \(0\), then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)=\{\infty^N\}\); if \(\mu\) has full rank, then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)=\mathbb T^N\).

Finally, we record a useful support-level consequence of (CI). Let \(p\) be a valuated quotient of an ordinary matroid \(M\), and let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\) be the ordinary matroid whose bases are the finite \(p\)-bases. Then \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\), in the following sense:
\[
\tag{1}
\operatorname{rk}_N(A)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A))
\qquad\text{for all }A\subseteq E.
\]
Indeed, if \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit and \(e\in C\), then \(e\) cannot be a coloop of \(N|C\). Otherwise choose an \(N\)-hyperplane \(H\) containing \(C-e\) but not \(e\), choose an \(N\)-basis \(A\) of \(H\), and apply (CI) to \(C\) and \(A\): the term \(p_{A\cup e}\) would be the unique finite term. Thus every element of every \(M\)-circuit lies in some \(N\)-circuit contained in that \(M\)-circuit. If \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq A\cup e\) containing \(e\); then an \(N\)-circuit contained in \(C\) contains \(e\), so \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(A)\). Hence \(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\subseteq\operatorname{cl}_N(A)\), which proves (1).

In particular, if \(G\subseteq H\) are flats of \(M\), then
\[
\tag{2}
\operatorname{rk}_N(H)-\operatorname{rk}_N(G)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]

---

### 2. Simplification invariance

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on \(E\). Let
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
be the loop set, and let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\). Write
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to\bar E
\]
for the quotient map, and let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\), viewed as a simple matroid on \(\bar E\).

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,&e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},&e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
The map \(\Delta\) commutes with tropical scalar translation, so it descends to projective tropical space.

Given a valuated matroid \(q\) on \(\bar E\), define its zero-parallel extension \(q^\uparrow\) to \(E\) by
\[
q^\uparrow_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
The tropical Plücker relations for \(q^\uparrow\) reduce to those for \(q\): a finite term can occur only when no loop and no repeated parallel class appears; if exactly one parallel class is repeated, the two potentially finite terms are equal; otherwise all terms are \(\infty\). Hence \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover,
\[
\tag{3}
\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\uparrow)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)).
\]
Indeed, loops force the corresponding coordinates to be \(\infty\), zero-offset parallel pairs force equality of coordinates inside each original parallel class, and after imposing these equations the remaining circuit equations are exactly those of \(q\). Applying this to the constant valuated matroid of \(S\), we get
\[
\tag{4}
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)).
\]

Now let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), so
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

If \(k=0\), every element is a loop of \(p\), so descent is immediate. Suppose \(k>0\).

First, every loop of \(M\) remains a loop of \(p\). If \(\ell\in L_0\) occurred in a finite \(p\)-basis \(B\), put \(A=B-\ell\). Then
\[
C_A^p(\ell)=p_B<\infty.
\]
But \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), while every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has coordinate \(\ell\) equal to \(\infty\), contradiction.

Second, original parallel classes remain zero-offset parallel classes. Let \(e,f\) be parallel nonloops of \(M\). Then every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) satisfies \(x_e=x_f\). Therefore, for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
C_A^p(e)=C_A^p(f),
\]
i.e.
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}.
\]
This has two consequences.

1. No finite \(p\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\). If \(B\) did, take \(A=B-f\). Then \(e\in A\), so \(p_{A\cup e}=\infty\), while \(p_{A\cup f}=p_B<\infty\), contradiction.

2. Replacing one representative of an original parallel class by another preserves both finiteness and valuation:
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}.
\]

Thus \(p\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar p\) on \(\bar E\), defined by
\[
\bar p_{\bar I}=p_I
\]
for any representative set \(I\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\). The Plücker relations for \(\bar p\) are inherited from those for \(p\), and \(p=\bar p^\uparrow\). Hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar p)).
\]
Using (4) and injectivity of \(\Delta\) on the non-loop coordinates,
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]
So \(\bar p\) is a quotient of \(S\).

Conversely, if \(q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\), then by (3) and (4),
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\uparrow)
=
\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(q))
\subseteq
\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(S))
=
\widetilde{\Trop}(M),
\]
so \(q^\uparrow\) is a quotient of \(M\).

Therefore \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\tag{5}
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

It remains to check compatibility with the embedded flat subposets. Flats of \(M\) are exactly \(L_0\) together with unions of non-loop parallel classes. Thus
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism.

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and put \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). For
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F},
\qquad
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F},
\]
we have, for every \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{N_F}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(F\cup A)-\operatorname{rk}_M(F).
\]
Since
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(A)=\operatorname{rk}_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)),
\]
we get
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{N_F}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_{\bar N_{\bar F}}(\pi(A\setminus L_0)).
\]
Thus \(N_F\) is precisely the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\). Consequently,
\[
\tag{6}
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\!\left(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right).
\]

---

### 3. Reduction to the simple modular case

An order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends through the lattice isomorphism \(\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to\mathcal L(S)\) to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\). Therefore, by Section 2, it is enough to prove the theorem for simple finite matroids.

Assume now that \(M\) is simple of rank \(r\). Its lattice of flats is a finite geometric lattice, hence semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Let \(\alpha(F)=F^\perp\) be the order-reversing involution. Since \(\alpha\) reverses chains,
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Apply semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\):
\[
\rho(X^\perp)+\rho(Y^\perp)
\ge
\rho(X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp)+\rho(X^\perp\vee Y^\perp).
\]
Using
\[
X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp=(X\vee Y)^\perp,
\qquad
X^\perp\vee Y^\perp=(X\wedge Y)^\perp,
\]
and \(\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F)\), this becomes
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\le \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Together with semimodularity, equality holds for all flats:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular, and \(M\) is a simple modular matroid. We do not use any classification of finite modular matroids.

---

### 4. The simple modular dagger construction

Assume throughout this section that \(M\) is finite, simple, modular, of rank \(r\), and that \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is the given order-reversing involution of its flat lattice.

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]

#### 4.1 Flat constancy

If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Let \(A=I-c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so (CI) forces \(p_I=\infty\).

Now suppose \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets spanning the same rank \(k\) flat. Then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying (CI) to \(C\) and \(A\) shows that
\[
\min(p_{A\cup a},p_{A\cup b})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Since the basis graph of every restriction \(M|F\) is connected, \(p_I\) is constant on all \(M\)-bases of a fixed rank \(k\) flat \(F\). Define
\[
\bar p(F)=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(F\).

#### 4.2 Definition of \(p^\dagger\)

For \(J\in\binom En\), define
\[
\tag{7}
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),
&J\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,
&J\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

This is not identically \(\infty\). If \(I\) is a finite \(p\)-basis and \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), then an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\) satisfies
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

Boundary ranks are immediate.

- If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid \(M\), up to global scalar.
- If \(k=r\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the rank \(0\) valuated matroid, up to global scalar.

Assume for the rest of this section that \(0<k<r\).

#### 4.3 The support of \(p^\dagger\)

Let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\). Define, for flats \(G\in\mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)=\operatorname{rk}_M(G)-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp),
\]
and for arbitrary \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)=\rho^\dagger(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)).
\]

We claim that \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function.

First,
\[
\rho^\dagger(\varnothing)=0-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(E)=0,
\]
and
\[
\rho^\dagger(E)=r-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(\varnothing)=n.
\]

If \(G\subseteq H\) are flats of \(M\), then \(H^\perp\subseteq G^\perp\). By (2),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_M(H^\perp).
\]
Since
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_M(H^\perp)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G),
\]
we get
\[
\rho^\dagger(H)\ge\rho^\dagger(G).
\]
Thus \(\rho^\dagger\) is monotone.

For flats \(G,H\), modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)+\operatorname{rk}_M(H)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G\wedge H)+\operatorname{rk}_M(G\vee H).
\]
Submodularity of \(\operatorname{rk}_N\) gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)+\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\ge
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp\cap H^\perp)
+
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp\cup H^\perp).
\]
Now
\[
G^\perp\cap H^\perp=(G\vee H)^\perp,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(G^\perp\cup H^\perp)
=
G^\perp\vee H^\perp
=
(G\wedge H)^\perp.
\]
Since \(N\) is a quotient of \(M\), ranks in \(N\) do not change after taking \(M\)-closure. Hence
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)+\rho^\dagger(H)
\ge
\rho^\dagger(G\wedge H)+\rho^\dagger(G\vee H).
\]
Using monotonicity, this extends to arbitrary subsets \(A,B\):
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)+\rho^\dagger(B)
\ge
\rho^\dagger(A\cap B)+\rho^\dagger(A\cup B).
\]
Also
\[
0\le\rho^\dagger(A)\le \operatorname{rk}_M(A)\le |A|.
\]
Submodularity implies single-element increments are at most \(1\). Therefore \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function; call the resulting rank \(n\) matroid \(N^\dagger\).

For \(J\in\binom En\), \(J\) is a basis of \(N^\dagger\) iff \(\rho^\dagger(J)=n\). Since \(\rho^\dagger(J)\le\operatorname{rk}_M(J)\le n\), this forces \(J\) to be \(M\)-independent. Then
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)=n
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)=k
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)<\infty.
\]
Thus the finite support of \(p^\dagger\) is precisely the set of bases of the matroid \(N^\dagger\).

#### 4.4 Tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We prove the three-term Plücker relations. Let
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2}
\]
and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must show that
\[
\min\left(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},\
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},\
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every appearing \(n\)-set is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Assume \(S\) is independent and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then in every displayed summand at least one of the two \(n\)-sets is dependent; hence all three summands are \(\infty\).

So assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\). Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-2\). Put
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\]
so \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane of \(Y\)
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),&H_e\ne H_f,\\
\infty,&H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
Indeed, \(H_e=H_f\) iff \(X\vee e=X\vee f\), equivalently \(Sef\) is dependent.

Thus the desired Plücker relation follows from the following lemma.

#### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23])
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Thus the second and third displayed sums are equal, possibly both \(\infty\). So the claim holds.

Assume now that \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct. Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since \(Y\) has rank \(k+2\), modularity implies
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then every \(H_i\wedge H_j\) is a rank \(k\) flat containing \(Z\), hence equals \(Z\). All three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i}H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\), and if \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), modularity gives
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) so that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i.
\]
Then
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup t_m\cup t_n}
\quad
\text{whenever }\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}.
\]
Hence the displayed relation is exactly the usual tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\).

It remains to treat \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\), after simplification, is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) with
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim \(w\in\Trop([Z,Y])\). Let \(D\) be a circuit in the simplified interval. Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). By circuit lifting for contractions, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
whose image in the contraction by \(Z\) is \(D\). Applying (CI) to \(C\) and \(A\), the elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Thus
\[
\min_{P\in D}w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Therefore \(w\in\Trop([Z,Y])\).

We now use a rank-three edge lemma.

#### Rank-three edge lemma

Let \(G\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\Trop(G)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be lines of \(G\). Then
\[
\min\bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer, with the convention that if two lines coincide then the corresponding meet term is \(\infty\).

If two lines coincide, the same repeated-hyperplane argument used above applies. Assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

For an ordinary matroid, \(w\in\Trop(G)\) iff every superlevel set
\[
\{x:w_x\ge\lambda\}
\]
is a flat. Since \(G\) has rank three, the nontrivial superlevel flats form a chain consisting of possibly a point \(a\) and possibly a line \(B\) with \(a\le B\). After subtracting the minimum value of \(w\), we may write
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\), allowing \(\alpha=0\) or \(\beta=0\) when one level is absent.

If none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), set
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
Then
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
So a pair \(\{i,j\}\) contributes a nonzero cost exactly when the colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree, and the cost depends only on that common color. Among the three perfect matchings of four colored objects, the minimum equal-color matching cost is never unique: the color multiplicities are \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\), and in every case at least two matchings attain the minimum.

If one line equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B,\qquad j=2,3,4.
\]
Let
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,&y=a,\\
\beta,&y\ne a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+\mathbf 1_{y_3=y_4}c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_4}c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_3}c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two have the same minimal \(c\)-value. If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors tie. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Hence the minimum is never uniquely finite.

This proves the rank-three edge lemma. Applying it inside \([Z,Y]\), with \(L_i=H_i/Z\), proves the local dual Plücker lemma. Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations.

Together with the support calculation, \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

#### 4.5 Quotient incidence for \(p^\dagger\)

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). It suffices to verify (CI) for \(p^\dagger\).

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit and let \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\). We must show
\[
\tag{\(\dagger\)-CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-1\) and \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

Let \(G\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
We first prove \(u\in\Trop(G)\).

By the superlevel-flat criterion, it is enough to show that every set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge\lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(G\). In a finite simple modular matroid, a set of points is a flat iff it is line-closed. Indeed, if \(S\) is line-closed and \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in S\) is an independent set spanning a flat \(F\), then induction on \(m\) shows every point of \(F\) lies in \(S\): for \(m\le2\) this is line-closedness; for \(m\ge3\), write \(H=\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1})\). If \(x\in F\setminus H\), modularity implies the line through \(x\) and \(a_m\) meets \(H\) in a point \(h\). By induction \(h\in S\), and line-closedness gives \(x\in S\).

Now take \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(G\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
These are hyperplanes of \(Y\), and
\[
P_3\le P_1\vee P_2
\]
implies
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), then \(u(P_3)=u(P_1)\) or \(u(P_2)\). Otherwise set
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Choose a basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three distinct hyperplanes \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit after simplification. Lifting this circuit to \(M\) and applying (CI) to \(B\) gives
\[
\min(u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3))
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Thus \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, hence a flat. Therefore \(u\in\Trop(G)\).

Return to the \(M\)-circuit \(C\). If \(C\subseteq X\), then every term in \((\dagger\)-CI) is \(\infty\). Otherwise let
\[
D=C\setminus X.
\]
For \(c\in D\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=u(\operatorname{cl}_M(X\cup c)).
\]
Every element \(c\in D\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\): indeed,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{M/X}(D-c)=\operatorname{rk}_{M/X}(D)
\]
because \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C-c)\). Thus \(c\) is not a coloop of \((M/X)|D\).

If the minimum of \(u\) on \(D\) had a unique finite minimizer \(c\), then a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\) and containing \(c\) would violate \(u\in\Trop(M/X)\). Hence \((\dagger\)-CI) holds. Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

So \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

---

### 5. Involutivity and flat compatibility

Since \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\), the flat-constancy result applies to it as well.

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and by definition
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]
If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, set
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Therefore
\[
\tag{8}
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Now let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\).

For a rank \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat \(G\) of \(M\),
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
if and only if \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\):
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing,
\qquad
G\vee F=E.
\]
Indeed, \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) means an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis after contracting \(F\), equivalently \(G\vee F=E\); by modularity and the rank condition this is equivalent to \(G\wedge F=\varnothing\).

Let \(H\) be a rank \(\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat. The dagger \((p^F)^\dagger\) is finite on an \(M\)-basis of \(H\) iff \(H^\perp\) is a complement of \(F\). Applying the polarity, this is equivalent to \(H\) being a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
\tag{9}
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp},
\]
up to the same harmless global scalar. Hence
\[
\Trop((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

### 6. The affine point-annihilator theorem

We continue in the simple modular case.

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define the polar atom
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp.
\]
Since \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\).

For every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\tag{10}
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]
Indeed,
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\bigvee_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp
=
\left(\bigwedge_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)\right)^\perp,
\]
and
\[
\bigwedge_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}.
\]
The last equality follows from fundamental circuits with respect to the basis \(B\).

Taking ranks in (10), \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis, and the operation \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases.

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis: if \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\), then for an \(M\)-basis \(B\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(B)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(B))=\operatorname{rk}_N(E)=k.
\]

Define a valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \(\{1,\dots,r\}\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
It has rank \(k\). By (10), for \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}
\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}
\right)
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\]
Thus
\[
\tag{11}
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*
\]
as ordinary valuated matroids.

For \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), define the affine polar hyperplane inside \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) by
\[
\widetilde H_x
=
\left\{
y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M):
\forall M\text{-bases }B=\{b_i\},\
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
If \(x\ne\infty^E\), then \(x\) is a rank-one valuated quotient of \(M\), and the rank \(r-1\) quotient \(x^\dagger\) satisfies
\[
\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(x^\dagger).
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

We prove the affine point-annihilator theorem:

\[
\tag{12}
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

Boundary ranks first. If \(k=0\), then
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\},
\qquad
p^\dagger=M,
\]
so both sides of (12) hold for every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), both sides hold. Conversely, suppose \(x\ne\infty^E\). Choose \(e\in E\) with \(x_e<\infty\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) and an index \(i\) with \(b_i^*=e\). Let \(A=B-b_i\). The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has, on \(B\), only one finite coordinate, namely at \(b_i\). Thus the basis equation defining \(\widetilde H_x\) has a unique finite minimizer. Hence \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\nsubseteq\widetilde H_x\). So (12) holds for \(k=r\).

Now assume \(0<k<r\).

First suppose
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Let \(y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
By (11),
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Also
\[
y|_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B).
\]
By ordinary annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. This holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\), so
\[
y\in\widetilde H_x.
\]
Thus
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We show
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Let \(T\subseteq[r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and let \(S=[r]\setminus T\), so \(|S|=k-1\). Put
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit
\[
C_A^p(e)=p_{A\cup e}
\]
lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), unless it is all \(\infty\), in which case the desired equation is automatic. Since \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\), testing \(C_A^p\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. But this is exactly the circuit equation for
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*),
\]
because
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T-i}=\mu_B(S\cup i)=p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]

Therefore \(x\) restricts to \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\) on every polar Boolean apartment \(B^*\). Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts correctly on every \(M\)-basis.

Now check all circuit equations of \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
J\in\binom E{r-k+1}.
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis; the corresponding apartment equation gives the desired relation.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), then every \(J-j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms in the equation for \(p^\dagger\) occur exactly for \(j\in D\), and for those \(j\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J-j)
\]
is independent of \(j\). Thus the equation reduces to the \(M\)-circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in D}x_j
\]
which holds because \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

Hence
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
This proves (12).

Projectivization is harmless: deleting \(\infty^E\) and quotienting by tropical scalar translation gives the corresponding projective statement. Rank-zero affine cones become empty projective tropical spaces.

---

### 7. Construction of the involution and extension to the original matroid

First assume \(M\) is simple modular. For \(X\in\Dr(M)\), choose a valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\) such that
\[
X=\Trop(p).
\]
Define
\[
\tag{13}
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]

This is well-defined. Indeed, by the uniqueness theorem for tropical linear spaces, \(p\) is determined by \(\Trop(p)\) up to global scalar, and adding a global scalar to \(p\) adds the same scalar to \(p^\dagger\). Equivalently, by the point-annihilator theorem,
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
=
\{x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M):\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\},
\]
which depends only on \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\).

By (8),
\[
\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
If
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(q),
\]
then for every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\dagger)\), the point-annihilator theorem gives
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x,
\]
so again by the point-annihilator theorem,
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Trop(q^\dagger)\subseteq\Trop(p^\dagger),
\]
and \(\Phi\) reverses inclusions.

Finally, by (9), for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Thus the theorem is proved for simple \(M\).

Now return to an arbitrary finite matroid \(M\). Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). Section 2 gives a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M),
\]
compatible with flat quotients. The order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends to one on \(\mathcal L(S)\). By the simple case, there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S)
\]
extending that descended flat-lattice involution.

Define
\[
\Phi_M:\Dr(M)\to\Dr(M)
\]
by conjugation:
\[
\Phi_M(\Delta_*(X))=\Delta_*(\Phi_S(X)).
\]
Because \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism and \(\Phi_S\) is an order-reversing involution, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution.

If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\), then \(F^\perp\) corresponds to \(\bar F^\perp\), and by (6),
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\!\left(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right).
\]
Hence
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Thus \(\Phi_M\) is the desired order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). ∎

original_problem:
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?
claim_verified:
With the listed routine patches inserted, the proof establishes the finite-ground-set theorem: for every finite matroid \(M\) whose lattice of flats has an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\), there is an inclusion-reversing involution \(\Phi\) of the relative Dressian \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) such that
\[
\Phi\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\).
solution_verified:
## Theorem

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on ground set \(E\). Suppose the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Then there is an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Here \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is regarded as a matroid on the original ground set \(E\), with the elements of \(F\) made loops.

---

## Proof

We use min-plus tropical conventions. Put \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). A minimum is said to be attained twice if it has no unique finite minimizer; an all-\(\infty\) minimum is allowed.

For a rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), extend \(p_I=\infty\) when \(|I|\neq d\), and define its affine tropical linear space by
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in \mathbb T^E:
\forall J\in\binom E{d+1},\
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J-j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Its projectivization \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\) is obtained by deleting \(\infty^E\) and quotienting by tropical scalar translation \(x\sim x+\lambda\mathbf 1\).

We identify an ordinary matroid \(M\) with its constant valuated matroid:
\[
p^M_B=
\begin{cases}
0,& B\text{ a basis of }M,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) is the affine Bergman fan of \(M\), including \(\infty^E\).

The elements of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) are precisely the projective tropical linear spaces \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)\), equivalently the projectivizations of valuated matroid quotients \(p\) of \(M\).

We first record several standard facts that will be used explicitly.

### 1. Basic valuated-matroid facts

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\). For \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\), define the valuated cocircuit
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(D_A^p\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). Indeed, for every \(J\in\binom E{k+1}\), the condition
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(D_A^p(j)+p_{J-j}\bigr)
\]
is exactly the tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) indexed by \(A\) and \(J\).

The affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\) is the tropical cone generated by these valuated cocircuits. Consequently, for an ordinary matroid \(M\), a rank \(k>0\) valuated matroid \(p\) satisfies
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
\]
if and only if, for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Rank \(0\) valuated matroids are quotients of every \(M\), since their affine tropical linear space is \(\{\infty^E\}\).

If \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), its finite support
\[
\operatorname{supp}(p)=\{I\in\binom Ek:p_I<\infty\}
\]
is the set of bases of an ordinary rank \(k\) matroid \(N\), and \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\). Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(A)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A))
\qquad
\text{for all }A\subseteq E.
\]
In particular, if \(X\le Y\) are flats of \(M\), then
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(Y)-\operatorname{rk}_N(X)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(Y)-\operatorname{rk}_M(X).
\]

We shall also use the standard local criterion for valuated matroids: if a function \(q:\binom Ed\to\mathbb T\) has matroidal finite support, then \(q\) is a valuated matroid if and only if all three-term tropical Plücker relations
\[
\min\bigl(
q_{Sab}+q_{Scd},\
q_{Sac}+q_{Sbd},\
q_{Sad}+q_{Sbc}
\bigr)
\]
have no unique finite minimizer, for every \(S\in\binom E{d-2}\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\notin S\).

Finally, for a valuated matroid \(\mu\) on a finite set \(N\), let \(\mu^*\) be its valuated dual:
\[
\mu^*_R=\mu_{N\setminus R}.
\]
For \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), put
\[
H_u=\left\{v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
Indeed, \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\) is tropically generated by the valuated cocircuits of \(\mu\), and those cocircuits are precisely the valuated circuits defining \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)\). Boundary ranks agree: rank \(0\) gives \(\{\infty^N\}\), and full rank gives all of \(\mathbb T^N\).

We shall also use the following Bergman criterion. If \(G\) is a finite simple matroid and \(w\in\mathbb T^{E(G)}\), then
\[
w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)
\]
if and only if, for every real \(\lambda\),
\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(G\). If a superlevel set is not a flat, choose \(e\notin S_\lambda\) with \(e\in\operatorname{cl}(S_\lambda)\); then some circuit \(C\subseteq S_\lambda\cup\{e\}\) contains \(e\), and \(e\) is the unique finite minimizer on \(C\). Conversely, if a circuit has a unique finite minimizer \(e\), choose \(\lambda\) strictly between \(w_e\) and the next smallest value on the circuit; then \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq S_\lambda\) but \(e\notin S_\lambda\), so \(S_\lambda\) is not flat.

---

## 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops of \(M\), and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\). Its ground set \(\bar E\) is the set of nonloop parallel classes of \(M\). Let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the projection to parallel classes.

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
This commutes with tropical scalar translation, so it descends projectively.

We claim that \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

Let \(q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Define its zero-parallel extension \(q^\uparrow\) to \(E\) by
\[
q^\uparrow_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
The finite support of \(q^\uparrow\) is obtained from the finite support of \(q\) by replacing each element of \(\bar E\) by a zero-offset parallel class and adding loops. Hence its support is matroidal. The three-term Plücker relations for \(q^\uparrow\) are either ordinary Plücker relations for \(q\), or all-\(\infty\), or have two identical finite terms because two elements lie in the same parallel class. Thus \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q^\uparrow)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\bigr).
\]
Indeed, loop elements force the corresponding coordinates to be \(\infty\), zero-offset parallel pairs force equality of the corresponding coordinates, and after those equations are imposed the remaining tropical linear equations are exactly the equations defining \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\).

In particular,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr).
\]
Thus, if \(q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\), then \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Conversely, let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(k=0\), it descends uniquely to the rank \(0\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Assume \(k>0\).

Every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(p\). If a loop \(\ell\in L_0\) belonged to a finite \(p\)-basis \(B\), then with \(A=B-\ell\) the cocircuit \(D_A^p\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) would have finite \(\ell\)-coordinate, contradicting the loop equation for \(M\).

Similarly, every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent. If a finite basis \(B\) of \(p\) contained an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), choose \(c\in C\), set \(A=B-c\), and observe that \(D_A^p\) has a unique finite minimum on \(C\).

Now let \(e,f\) be parallel nonloops of \(M\). Since \(\{e,f\}\) is a circuit of \(M\), every vector of \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) has equal \(e\)- and \(f\)-coordinates. Hence
\[
D_A^p(e)=D_A^p(f)
\]
for every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\). Therefore no finite \(p\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\), and whenever \(A\cup e\) is a finite basis with \(f\) parallel to \(e\), one has
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}.
\]
Thus \(p\) descends to a unique valuated matroid \(\bar p\) on \(\bar E\), and \(p=\bar p^\uparrow\). Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar p)\bigr).
\]
Since \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), it follows that
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S),
\]
so \(\bar p\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\).

Hence \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong \operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

The flat lattices of \(M\) and \(S\) are canonically isomorphic:
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0).
\]
For a flat \(F\) of \(M\), writing \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), the matroid \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is the zero-parallel extension of \(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}\). Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\right).
\]

Thus it suffices to prove the theorem for finite simple matroids.

---

## 3. The simple case is modular

Assume now that \(M\) is finite, simple, and loopless, of rank \(r\), and that its lattice of flats has an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

A finite geometric lattice is semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Because \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an anti-automorphism of a ranked lattice of rank \(r\),
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp\) and \(Y^\perp\), and translating back using
\[
(X\vee Y)^\perp=X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp,
\qquad
(X\wedge Y)^\perp=X^\perp\vee Y^\perp,
\]
gives the reverse inequality
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\le \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Therefore equality holds:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Hence \(M\) is modular.

Since \(M\) is simple, the atoms of \(\mathcal L(M)\) are the elements of \(E\). The polarity sends hyperplanes to atoms.

---

## 4. Flat constancy for valuated quotients

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of this simple modular \(M\).

If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, if \(C\subseteq I\) is an \(M\)-circuit and \(c\in C\), then for \(A=I-c\) the quotient incidence condition gives a one-term minimum unless \(p_I=\infty\).

Now suppose \(I,J\in\binom Ek\) are \(M\)-bases of the same rank \(k\) flat \(X\). The basis graph of \(M|X\) is connected, so it suffices to handle adjacent bases \(I=A\cup a\) and \(J=A\cup b\). Since both span \(X\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing \(a\) and \(b\). Applying quotient incidence to \(C\) and \(A\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Therefore \(p_I=p_J\).

Hence for every rank \(k\) flat \(X\) of \(M\), one may define
\[
\bar p(X)=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(X\).

---

## 5. The dagger construction

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and set
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define a rank \(n\) function \(p^\dagger\) on \(E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),&
J\in\binom En\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,&
J\in\binom En\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

We prove that \(p^\dagger\) is again a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\), a rank \(k\) matroid quotient of \(M\), and write
\[
\nu(X)=\operatorname{rk}_N(X)
\]
for flats \(X\) of \(M\).

Define, for \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
h(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(A)
+
\nu(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)^\perp)
-
k.
\]
We claim that \(h\) is a matroid rank function of rank \(r-k=n\).

Indeed, \(h(\varnothing)=0\) and \(h(E)=r-k\). Also
\[
0\le h(A)\le |A|.
\]
For \(X\le Y\) flats of \(M\),
\[
h(Y)-h(X)
=
\rho(Y)-\rho(X)
-
\bigl(\nu(X^\perp)-\nu(Y^\perp)\bigr)
\ge 0,
\]
because \(N\) is a quotient of \(M\), so rank increments in \(N\) are bounded by rank increments in \(M\). Thus \(h\) is monotone.

For flats \(X,Y\), modularity of \(M\) cancels the \(\rho\)-terms:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&h(X)+h(Y)-h(X\vee Y)-h(X\wedge Y)\\
&=
\nu(X^\perp)+\nu(Y^\perp)
-\nu(X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp)
-\nu(X^\perp\vee Y^\perp)
\ge 0,
\end{aligned}
\]
by submodularity of \(\nu\). For arbitrary subsets, replace them by their closures and use monotonicity. Therefore \(h\) is submodular, hence a matroid rank function.

The bases of this matroid are exactly the finite-support sets of \(p^\dagger\). Indeed, for \(J\in\binom En\),
\[
h(J)=n
\]
if and only if \(J\) is \(M\)-independent and
\[
\nu(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)=k,
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)<\infty.
\]
Thus \(\operatorname{supp}(p^\dagger)\) is matroidal.

It remains to prove the valuated Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Because the support is already matroidal, it suffices to prove the three-term relations.

Let
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2},
\]
and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must show that
\[
\min\left(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},\
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},\
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all six terms are \(\infty\). Suppose \(S\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every opposite pairing contains a dependent \(n\)-set, so again all three sums are \(\infty\).

Thus assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\]
so \(\rho(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), put
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),& H_e\neq H_f,\\
\infty,& H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]

We need the following local lemma.

### Lemma 5.1

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
[12]+[34],\
[13]+[24],\
[14]+[23]
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If two hyperplanes repeat, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Hence the second and third sums are equal, so the minimum is not uniquely finite.

Assume now that the \(H_i\) are distinct. In a modular rank \(k+2\) interval, distinct hyperplanes meet in rank \(k\). Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting successively with hyperplanes lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\rho(Z)\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}.
\]

If \(\rho(Z)=k\), every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(Z\), and all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i} H_j.
\]
Then \(\rho(T_i)=k-1\). If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), modularity gives
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\). Then
\[
A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis of \(H_i\wedge H_j\), so
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup t_m\cup t_n}.
\]
The desired relation is exactly the three-term Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\).

It remains to consider \(\rho(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\) is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying \(Z<P<Y\). Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim that \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}([Z,Y])\). Let \(D\) be a circuit in the interval. Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\) for each \(P\in D\). The circuit \(D\) lifts to an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
whose image in the contraction by \(Z\) is \(D\). Applying quotient incidence for \(p\) to \(C\) and \(A\), all elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), and we obtain
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}([Z,Y])\).

Now apply the following rank-three edge lemma to the four lines \(H_i/Z\) in \([Z,Y]\). ∎

### Lemma 5.2

Let \(G\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)\). For lines \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) of \(G\), define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),& L_i\neq L_j,\\
\infty,& L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If two lines coincide, two of the three opposite sums are equal, so there is nothing to prove. Thus assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If \(w\equiv\infty\), the result is immediate. Otherwise subtract the finite minimum of \(w\), so \(\min w=0\). By the Bergman superlevel-flat criterion, the nontrivial superlevel sets of \(w\) form a chain of flats. In rank three, this chain has the form
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a line, either of which may be absent. Thus
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}
+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\).

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Let
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For \(i\neq j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
Therefore a pair \(\{i,j\}\) contributes positive cost only when \(y_i=y_j\). Among four colored objects, the three perfect matchings never have a unique minimum equal-color cost: the color multiplicities are
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad
2+1+1,\quad
2+2,\quad
3+1,\quad
4,
\]
and in each case either all matchings tie or at least two avoid the repeated color. The conclusion follows.

Now suppose one of the lines is \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
Let
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,& y=a,\\
\beta,& y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+\mathbf 1_{y_3=y_4}c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_4}c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_3}c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of the \(c(y_j)\) attain the minimum. If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Thus the minimum is never uniquely finite. ∎

Lemmas 5.1 and 5.2 prove the three-term Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Since the support of \(p^\dagger\) is matroidal, \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\). We must show that
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(X)=n-1\) and \(\rho(Y)=k+1\).

Let \(G\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]

We claim that
\[
u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G).
\]
By the superlevel-flat criterion, it suffices to prove that for every real \(\lambda\),
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(G\).

In a finite simple modular matroid, a set of points is a flat if and only if it is line-closed. Indeed, if a line-closed set \(S\) spans a flat \(F\), choose an independent spanning set \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in S\). Inducting on \(m\), all points of \(\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1})\) lie in \(S\). If \(x\in F\) is outside that hyperplane, modularity forces the line through \(x\) and \(a_m\) to meet the hyperplane in a point already in \(S\); line-closedness then gives \(x\in S\).

Now take \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\). Set
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
These are hyperplanes of \(Y\), and \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\) implies
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), then \(u(P_3)=u(P_1)\) or \(u(P_2)\). Otherwise the three hyperplanes \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) contain the rank \(k-1\) flat
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\). In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three points \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit. Lifting to \(M\) and applying quotient incidence for \(p\) to \(B\), we get
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Hence \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, and therefore flat. Thus \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)\).

Return to the circuit \(C\). Elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). For \(c\notin X\), let
\[
P_c=\operatorname{cl}_M(X\cup c).
\]
Then
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=u(P_c).
\]
The vector \(u\) lifts to a vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\), constant on parallel classes and \(\infty\) on loops.

If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise let \(D=C\setminus X\). Every \(c\in D\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\), because \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C-c)\) implies \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_{M/X}(D-c)\). Therefore a unique finite minimum on \(D\) would violate the fact that the lifted \(u\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\). Hence the quotient incidence condition holds.

Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

## 6. Dagger is involutive and sends flat quotients to flat quotients

Since \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\), the flat-constancy result applies to it. We show
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, both sides are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is independent, set
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Therefore dagger is an involution.

Now let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\rho(F)\). For a rank \(r-\rho(F)\) flat \(G\), the value \(\bar p^F(G)\) is finite exactly when \(G\vee F=E\). Since \(M\) is modular and
\[
\rho(G)+\rho(F)=r,
\]
this is equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Thus \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\). The polarity sends complements of \(F\) to complements of \(F^\perp\). Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp},
\]
where \(p^{F^\perp}\) is the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}.
\]

---

## 7. Affine annihilator duality inside \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\)

For \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), define an affine polar hyperplane \(\widetilde H_x\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) as follows.

For every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), put
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp.
\]
Because \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\). We declare that \(y\in\widetilde H_x\) if for every \(M\)-basis \(B\),
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

We prove the key equivalence:
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Assume first \(0<k<r\). Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. For \(S\subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i).
\]
Applying polarity gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
Hence
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on bases.

Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Because \(\operatorname{supp}(p)\) is a quotient of \(M\), every \(M\)-basis has support-rank \(k\), so \(\mu_B\) is not identically \(\infty\). Its Plücker relations are inherited from \(p\).

The restriction of \(p^\dagger\) to \(B^*\) is the ordinary valuated dual \(\mu_B^*\). Indeed, for \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}
\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}
\right)
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\]

Suppose \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\), and let \(y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). For a fixed \(M\)-basis \(B\), put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Then
\[
u_B\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B).
\]
By ordinary affine annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(B\), we have \(y\in\widetilde H_x\). Thus
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and again set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We show \(u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*)\).

Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and put \(S=[r]\setminus T\), so \(|S|=k-1\). Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
Consider the valuated cocircuit \(D_A^p\). If \(D_A^p\equiv \infty\), the corresponding equation for \(\mu_B^*\) is automatic. Otherwise,
\[
D_A^p\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Testing the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) on \(B\), we obtain
\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. But this is precisely the tropical linear equation for \(u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*)\). Therefore
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on the set of \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts to \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\) on every Boolean apartment coming from an \(M\)-basis.

We now check the global equations of \(p^\dagger\). Let \(J\in\binom E{r-k+1}\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis; the desired equation follows from the apartment calculation.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), then every \(J-j\) is dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Finally suppose \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\). Then \(J\) contains a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms in the \(p^\dagger\)-equation occur exactly for \(j\in D\), and for such \(j\), the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J-j)\) is independent of \(j\). Thus the equation reduces to the circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in D}x_j
\]
for \(x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Hence \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\).

The boundary cases are immediate. If \(k=0\), then \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\) and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), so both sides hold for every \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\) and \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Thus
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x\), testing the cocircuits associated to \(M\)-bases forces every coordinate of \(x\) to be \(\infty\). Hence the equivalence also holds in rank \(r\).

Therefore, for every valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\),
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Projectivizing gives the corresponding statement in tropical projective space.

---

## 8. The involution in the simple modular case

For a projective tropical linear subspace \(X\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(X)=
\left\{
[x]\in \operatorname{Trop}(M):
X\subseteq H_x
\right\},
\]
where \(H_x\) is the projectivization of \(\widetilde H_x\).

If \(X=\operatorname{Trop}(p)\), then the projectivized annihilator equivalence gives
\[
\Phi(X)=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\Phi(X)\in\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Since dagger is involutive,
\[
\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
If \(X\subseteq Y\), then
\[
Y\subseteq H_x \implies X\subseteq H_x,
\]
so
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq \Phi(X).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is inclusion-reversing.

Finally, for every flat \(F\),
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This proves the theorem for finite simple matroids.

---

## 9. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) now be arbitrary finite, and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). If \(M\) is all loops, then \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) is empty projectively, \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) has one element, and the result is trivial.

Otherwise, the flat-lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
transports the given order-reversing involution to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\):
\[
\bar F\longmapsto \bar F^\perp
=
\psi\bigl(\psi^{-1}(\bar F)^\perp\bigr).
\]
By the simple case, \(S\) has an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S)
\]
extending this polarity on flats.

Using the poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M),
\]
define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and write \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). From the simplification compatibility,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\right).
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_M\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\Phi_S\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
\right)\\
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F^\perp\oplus U_{0,\bar F^\perp})
\right)\\
&=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus \(\Phi_M\) extends the original flat-lattice involution.

This completes the proof. ∎
