solution_verified:
## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on a finite ground set \(E\). Let \(\mathcal L(M)\) be its lattice of flats, ordered by inclusion, and suppose there is an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp
\]
of \(\mathcal L(M)\). Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), with tropical minimum conventions as below. Let \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) denote the poset of projective tropical linear spaces contained in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), equivalently the projectivizations of affine tropical linear spaces \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\) where \(p\) ranges over valuated matroid quotients of the constant valuated matroid \(M\), modulo addition of a global tropical scalar to all Plücker coordinates. The order is inclusion of projective tropical linear spaces.

For a flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), let
\[
N_F:=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
be the matroid on \(E\) obtained from the contraction \(M/F\) on \(E\setminus F\) by adding all elements of \(F\) back as loops. Equivalently,
\[
r_{N_F}(A)=r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F)
\qquad(A\subseteq E).
\]
The embedded flat object associated to \(F\) is \(\operatorname{Trop}(N_F)\in\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

We prove that there is an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
such that
\[
\Phi\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\). Thus the original question has an affirmative answer in the finite-ground-set setting in which relative Dressians and tropical projective spaces are defined here. No weakening is made beyond making the finite-ground-set convention explicit.

## Proof

### 1. Affine conventions and standard valuated-matroid facts

We work first with affine tropical cones. Put
\[
\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}.
\]
Throughout, a tropical condition saying that a minimum is “attained at least twice” means:

> the minimum has no unique finite minimizer.

Thus a minimum whose all terms are \(\infty\) satisfies the condition.

Let \(p\) be a rank \(d\) valuated matroid on \(E\), with Plücker coordinates \(p_I\in\mathbb T\) for \(I\in\binom Ed\). Its affine tropical linear space is
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \mathbb T^E
\]
defined by the circuit equations
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
\iff
\forall J\in\binom E{d+1},\quad
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}.
\]
For \(d=0\), these equations force
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
For \(d=|E|\), there are no equations, so
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\mathbb T^E.
\]

Projectivization is obtained by deleting the all-\(\infty\) vector and quotienting by tropical scalar translation:
\[
x\sim x+\lambda\mathbf 1,\qquad \lambda\in\mathbb R.
\]
Thus a rank-zero affine tropical linear space becomes empty projectively.

We identify an ordinary matroid \(M\) with its constant valuated matroid:
\[
M_I=
\begin{cases}
0,& I\text{ is a basis of }M,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) is the affine Bergman fan of \(M\), including \(\infty^E\).

We use the following standard facts from valuated matroid theory.

#### Standard Fact 1: containment equals valuated quotient

For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same finite ground set,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
\]
if and only if \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\).

In particular, if \(q=M\) is an ordinary matroid with constant valuation and \(p\) has rank \(d>0\), then \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\) if and only if, for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((d-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^p(c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer, where
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

For \(d=0\), \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), so it is contained in every affine tropical linear space.

#### Standard Fact 2: coordinate cocircuits lie in the tropical linear space

If \(p\) has rank \(d>0\) and \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\), then the vector
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A
\end{cases}
\]
lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\), possibly as the all-\(\infty\) vector.

#### Standard Fact 3: ordinary affine tropical annihilator duality

Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\). For \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), define
\[
H_u^N=
\left\{
v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
\iff
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\subseteq H_u^N.
\]
This includes the boundary ranks. If \(\operatorname{rk}\mu=0\), then \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)=\{\infty^N\}\) and \(\mu^*\) has full rank, so both sides hold for every \(u\). If \(\operatorname{rk}\mu=|N|\), then \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)=\mathbb T^N\), and the right side holds exactly when \(u=\infty^N\), matching the rank-zero space \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)=\{\infty^N\}\).

#### Standard Fact 4: uniqueness of valuated Plücker coordinates

The affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\) determines the valuated matroid \(p\) up to addition of a global tropical scalar to all Plücker coordinates. Hence constructions invariant under global scalar addition are well-defined on tropical linear spaces rather than only on chosen Plücker representatives.

We shall also use the following elementary characterization of ordinary Bergman fans.

#### Lemma 1.1: superlevel-flat criterion

Let \(N\) be an ordinary matroid on a finite set \(E_N\). For \(w\in\mathbb T^{E_N}\),
\[
w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)
\]
if and only if, for every real \(\lambda\), the superlevel set
\[
\{e\in E_N:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

**Proof.** The ordinary Bergman condition says that no circuit has a unique finite minimum.

Suppose first that \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). Let
\[
S_\lambda=\{e:w_e\ge\lambda\}.
\]
If \(S_\lambda\) were not a flat, there would be \(e\notin S_\lambda\) with \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(S_\lambda)\). Then some circuit \(C\) satisfies
\[
e\in C\subseteq S_\lambda\cup\{e\}.
\]
Since \(w_e<\lambda\le w_c\) for all \(c\in C\setminus\{e\}\), \(e\) is the unique finite minimizer on \(C\), contradiction.

Conversely, suppose every real superlevel set is a flat. If a circuit \(C\) had a unique finite minimum at \(e\), choose a real number \(\lambda\) strictly between \(w_e\) and all other finite values on \(C\), and larger than \(w_e\) if all other values are \(\infty\). Then
\[
C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq S_\lambda,
\qquad
e\notin S_\lambda.
\]
But \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(C\setminus\{e\})\subseteq\operatorname{cl}_N(S_\lambda)\), so \(S_\lambda\) is not a flat. This contradiction proves the criterion. \(\square\)

---

### 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(M\) be any finite matroid on \(E\). Let
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
be its loop set. Let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\), and let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\longrightarrow \bar E
\]
be the quotient map. Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\), viewed as a simple matroid on \(\bar E\).

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\longrightarrow \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
This map commutes with tropical scalar translation, so it descends to projective tropical spaces.

#### 2.1. Zero-parallel extension

Let \(\bar q\) be a rank \(d\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Its zero-parallel extension \(q=\Delta\bar q\) to \(E\) is defined by
\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing
\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
\qquad I\in\binom Ed.
\]
This is a valuated matroid: its tropical Plücker relations either project to those of \(\bar q\), or contain only \(\infty\)-terms, or, when two representatives of one parallel class are interchanged, contain two equal finite terms. Thus adding loops and replacing an element by a zero-offset parallel class preserves valuated matroids.

Moreover,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]
Indeed, the loop circuits force \(x_\ell=\infty\) for \(\ell\in L_0\), the two-element zero-parallel circuits force \(x_e=x_f\) when \(e,f\) lie in the same non-loop parallel class of \(M\), and after imposing these equations the remaining circuit equations are precisely those for \(\bar q\).

Applying this to the constant valuated matroid \(S\) gives
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr).
\]

#### 2.2. Every quotient of \(M\) descends to \(S\)

Let \(q\) be a rank \(d\) valuated matroid on \(E\) such that
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
If \(d=0\), then \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)=\{\infty^E\}\), and \(q\) is the zero-parallel extension of the unique rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\).

Assume \(d>0\). For every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\), the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^q\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\), hence in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

First, every finite \(q\)-basis is \(M\)-independent. If \(I\) is a finite \(q\)-basis containing an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), choose \(c\in C\) and set \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then on the circuit \(C\),
\[
D_A^q(c)=q_I<\infty,
\]
while
\[
D_A^q(e)=\infty
\qquad(e\in C\setminus\{c\}),
\]
because \(C\setminus\{c\}\subseteq A\). Thus \(D_A^q\) has a unique finite minimum on the \(M\)-circuit \(C\), contradicting \(D_A^q\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

Second, loops of \(M\) remain loops of \(q\). If \(\ell\in L_0\), every vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) has \(\ell\)-coordinate equal to \(\infty\). If some finite \(q\)-basis \(I\) contained \(\ell\), then with \(A=I\setminus\{\ell\}\) we would have
\[
D_A^q(\ell)=q_I<\infty,
\]
again contradicting \(D_A^q\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

Third, original parallel classes are not split and have zero valuated offset. Let \(e,f\) be parallel non-loops in \(M\). Then every vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) satisfies
\[
x_e=x_f,
\]
because \(\{e,f\}\) is an \(M\)-circuit. Hence for every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\),
\[
D_A^q(e)=D_A^q(f).
\]
It follows that no finite \(q\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\). Indeed, if \(I\) contained both, take \(A=I\setminus\{f\}\). Then
\[
D_A^q(e)=\infty,
\qquad
D_A^q(f)=q_I<\infty,
\]
a contradiction. Also, if \(I=A\cup\{e\}\) is a finite \(q\)-basis and \(f\) lies in the same original parallel class, then \(f\notin A\), and
\[
q_{A\cup e}=D_A^q(e)=D_A^q(f)=q_{A\cup f}.
\]
Thus replacement within an original parallel class preserves both finiteness and valuation.

Now define a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\) by choosing representatives. For a \(d\)-subset \(\bar I\subseteq\bar E\), choose any \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\), and set
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]
The preceding paragraph shows that this is independent of the representative set \(I\). The tropical Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) are inherited from those for \(q\) by choosing representatives of the involved parallel classes. Moreover, \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\), so
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]
Since
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr)
\]
and \(\Delta\) is injective on \(\mathbb T^{\bar E}\), we get
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S).
\]
Thus \(\bar q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\).

Conversely, if \(\bar q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\), then its zero-parallel extension \(q=\Delta\bar q\) satisfies
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr)
\subseteq
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr)
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M),
\]
so \(q\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Therefore \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

#### 2.3. Compatibility with flat quotients

Flats of \(M\) are exactly the loop set \(L_0\) together with unions of non-loop parallel classes. Therefore
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\longrightarrow \mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism.

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and put \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Let
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
on \(E\), and
\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}
\]
on \(\bar E\). For every \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F).
\]
Using
\[
r_M(A)=r_S\bigl(\pi(A\setminus L_0)\bigr),
\]
we get
\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_S\bigl(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0)\bigr)-r_S(\bar F)
=
r_{\bar N_{\bar F}}\bigl(\pi(A\setminus L_0)\bigr).
\]
Thus \(N_F\) is the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\), and
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N_F)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar N_{\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Hence the isomorphism \(\Delta_*\) identifies the flat object indexed by \(\bar F\) in \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\) with the flat object indexed by \(F\) in \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

The flat-lattice involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends through \(\psi\) to an order-reversing involution of \(\mathcal L(S)\):
\[
\bar\alpha
=
\psi\circ(\,\cdot\,)^\perp\circ\psi^{-1}.
\]

If \(M\) has rank \(0\), then all elements are loops, \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) is empty projectively, and \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) has only the empty object. The desired involution is then the identity. Hence, from now on in the construction, we may reduce to the case where \(M\) is simple and loopless.

---

### 3. Polarity forces modularity

Let \(L\) be a finite geometric lattice of rank \(r\), with rank function \(\rho\). Suppose \(L\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
X\longmapsto X^\perp.
\]
Because \(L\) is graded, order reversal sends an interval of length \(\rho(X)\) from the bottom to \(X\) to an interval of length \(\rho(X)\) from \(X^\perp\) to the top. Therefore
\[
\rho(X^\perp)=r-\rho(X).
\]

Geometric lattices are semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\ge
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Apply semimodularity to \(X^\perp\) and \(Y^\perp\). Since
\[
X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp=(X\vee Y)^\perp,
\qquad
X^\perp\vee Y^\perp=(X\wedge Y)^\perp,
\]
we get
\[
\rho(X^\perp)+\rho(Y^\perp)
\ge
\rho((X\vee Y)^\perp)+\rho((X\wedge Y)^\perp).
\]
Substituting \(\rho(Z^\perp)=r-\rho(Z)\) gives
\[
2r-\rho(X)-\rho(Y)
\ge
2r-\rho(X\vee Y)-\rho(X\wedge Y),
\]
or
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\le
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Together with semimodularity, equality holds for all \(X,Y\):
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
=
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus \(L\) is modular.

Applying this to the flat lattice of the simplification \(S\), we conclude that after the simplification reduction we are in the following situation:

- \(M\) is finite, simple, and loopless;
- \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular;
- \(M\) has rank \(r\);
- there is an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) of \(\mathcal L(M)\).

We now construct the desired involution in this simple modular case.

---

### 4. The simple modular dagger construction

Assume throughout this section that \(M\) is finite, simple, loopless, modular of rank \(r\), and equipped with a flat-lattice polarity
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp.
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\). Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]

#### 4.1. Flat constancy for quotients

Assume first \(k>0\). Since \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), Standard Fact 1 gives the circuit-incidence relations
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^p(c)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\).

If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Let \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then all elements of \(C\setminus\{c\}\) lie in \(A\), so
\[
D_A^p(e)=\infty
\qquad(e\in C\setminus\{c\}),
\]
while
\[
D_A^p(c)=p_I.
\]
The circuit-incidence relation forces \(p_I=\infty\). Thus
\[
I\text{ \(M\)-dependent}
\implies
p_I=\infty.
\]

Now suppose \(I=A\cup\{a\}\) and \(I'=A\cup\{b\}\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with the same \(M\)-closure. Since \(a\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{b\})\) and \(a\notin\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying circuit-incidence to \(A\) and \(C\), all terms indexed by \(C\cap A\) are \(\infty\), so
\[
\min\{p_{A\cup a},p_{A\cup b}\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}
\]
in \(\mathbb T\).

The basis graph of the restriction \(M|F\) is connected for every flat \(F\). Therefore \(p_I\) is constant on all \(M\)-bases \(I\) of a fixed rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). We write this common value as
\[
\bar p(F).
\]

We shall also need the following consequence. Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis. Choose one finite \(p\)-basis \(I\). It is \(M\)-independent by the preceding paragraph, so extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\). Let \(B\) be any \(M\)-basis. Connect \(B_0\) to \(B\) in the basis graph of \(M\). Suppose at one step we move from \(B'\) to
\[
B''=B'-\{b\}\cup\{e\}.
\]
If the current finite \(p\)-basis \(I\subseteq B'\) does not contain \(b\), then \(I\subseteq B''\). If \(b\in I\), put \(A=I\setminus\{b\}\). Let \(C\subseteq B'\cup\{e\}\) be the fundamental \(M\)-circuit containing \(b\) and \(e\). Since
\[
p_{A\cup b}=p_I<\infty,
\]
circuit-incidence forces another finite term
\[
p_{A\cup c}<\infty
\]
for some \(c\in C\setminus(A\cup\{b\})\). Such \(c\) lies in \(B''\), so \(A\cup\{c\}\subseteq B''\) is a finite \(p\)-basis. Moving along the path proves the claim.

#### 4.2. Definition of \(p^\dagger\)

For \(|J|=n=r-k\), define
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),
& J\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,
& J\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

If \(0<k<r\), this is not identically \(\infty\). Indeed, choose a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), put \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), and choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

The boundary ranks are immediate:

- If \(k=0\), then \(p\) has only the coordinate \(p_\varnothing\), and \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid \(M\), shifted by \(p_\varnothing\).
- If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of the constant valuated matroid \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) is the rank-zero valuated matroid.

So for the main construction we assume
\[
0<k<r.
\]

#### 4.3. Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We verify the three-term tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2}
\]
and let \(a,b,c,d\in E\setminus S\) be distinct. We must show that
\[
\min\bigl(
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup b}+p^\dagger_{S\cup c\cup d},\,
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup c}+p^\dagger_{S\cup b\cup d},\,
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup d}+p^\dagger_{S\cup b\cup c}
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(S\cup\{i,j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, and all terms are \(\infty\).

Assume \(S\) is \(M\)-independent and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every Plücker matching contains a dependent set involving that element, so all three matching sums are \(\infty\).

Thus assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-2\), we have
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2.
\]
For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane of \(Y\)
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
For distinct \(e,f\), \(S\cup\{e,f\}\) is \(M\)-dependent exactly when \(X\vee e=X\vee f\), equivalently \(H_e=H_f\). If \(H_e\neq H_f\), then
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}
=
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]
Therefore the Plücker relation follows from the following local lemma.

#### Lemma 4.1: local four-hyperplane lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let
\[
H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4
\]
be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two hyperplanes coincide, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Thus the other two matching sums are equal, so the minimum is not uniquely finite.

Assume now that \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct. Since \(M\) is modular, two distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\):
\[
\operatorname{rk}(H_i\wedge H_j)=k
\qquad(i\neq j).
\]
Set
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
The possible ranks of \(Z\) are \(k\), \(k-1\), and, when \(k\ge2\), \(k-2\).

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) is equal to \(Z\). Hence all three matching sums are equal to \(2\bar p(Z)\).

Suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\). Also
\[
T_i\wedge T_j=Z.
\]
If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then \(T_m,T_n\le H_i\wedge H_j\), and modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(T_m\vee T_n)
=
(k-1)+(k-1)-(k-2)=k.
\]
Since \(H_i\wedge H_j\) also has rank \(k\), we get
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose
\[
t_i\in T_i\setminus Z.
\]
Then \(A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}\) is an \(M\)-basis of \(T_m\vee T_n\). The ordinary Plücker relation for \(p\), applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4,
\]
is exactly
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr),
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

It remains to handle \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). Consider the rank-three interval \([Z,Y]\). Its simplification \(N\) is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Its points are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) with
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We first show \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(N\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each point \(P\in D\), choose an element \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). Circuit lifting from the contraction by \(Z\) gives an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
with
\[
C\setminus Z=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
\]
Apply the quotient circuit-incidence relation for \(p\) to \(A\) and \(C\). Elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Therefore
\[
\min_{P\in D}w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\).

The hyperplanes \(H_i\) descend to lines \(L_i=H_i/Z\) of \(N\), and
\[
H_i\wedge H_j
\]
corresponds to the point \(L_i\wedge L_j\). Thus the desired relation follows from the rank-three edge lemma below. \(\square\)

#### Lemma 4.2: rank-three edge lemma

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let
\[
w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]
Let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be lines of \(N\), and define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),& L_i\neq L_j,\\
\infty,& L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\,
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\,
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two lines coincide, the same repeated-hyperplane argument as above applies. So assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If \(w=\infty^E\), the statement is immediate. Otherwise subtract a global scalar so that \(\min w=0\). By Lemma 1.1, the real superlevel sets of \(w\) are flats. In a simple rank-three matroid, proper nonempty flats are points and lines. Since superlevel sets are nested, the nonzero part of \(w\) is supported on a point-line flag: there are a line \(B\), a point \(a\le B\), and numbers \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\) such that
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}},
\]
with the conventions that \(\alpha=0\) or \(\beta=0\) allows one level of the flag to be absent.

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Let
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For distinct \(i,j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\iff
y_i=y_j.
\]
Moreover,
\[
L_i\wedge L_j=a
\iff
y_i=y_j=a.
\]
Thus the edge \(\{i,j\}\) contributes nonzero cost only when the colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree; the cost is \(\beta\), or \(\alpha+\beta\) if the common color is \(a\).

For four colored objects, the minimum perfect-matching cost is never unique. The color multiplicities are \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\). In these cases, respectively, all matchings tie, or two matchings avoid the repeated color, or two cross-matchings tie, or all matchings have the same one repeated-color edge, or all matchings are identical in cost. This remains true with cost \(\infty\).

Now suppose one line equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), set
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
Define
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,& y=a,\\
\beta,& y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, then at least two of \(c(y_2),c(y_3),c(y_4)\) are equal to the minimum value \(\beta\). If exactly two of them are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of that equal pair tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three matching costs are equal. Hence no unique finite minimum occurs. \(\square\)

Lemmas 4.1 and 4.2 prove the Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Hence \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\(n\) valuated matroid.

#### 4.4. Quotient incidence for \(p^\dagger\)

We now show that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\). By Standard Fact 1, it is enough to prove the circuit-incidence relations:
for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((n-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, every \(A\cup\{c\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Thus assume \(A\) is \(M\)-independent. Set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-1,
\qquad
\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1.
\]

We need the following lemma.

#### Lemma 4.3: codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a rank-\((n-1)\) flat, and let \(N\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]

**Proof.** By Lemma 1.1, it suffices to show that every real superlevel set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge\lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

First we prove that \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed. Let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line spanned by \(P_1,P_2\) in \(N\). If \(P_3=P_1\) or \(P_3=P_2\), there is nothing to prove.

Assume \(P_1,P_2,P_3\) are distinct. Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). Since \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\), polarity gives
\[
H_3=P_3^\perp
\supseteq
(P_1\vee P_2)^\perp
=
P_1^\perp\wedge P_2^\perp
=
H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Let
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(K)=k-1\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B\) of \(K\). For \(i=1,2,3\), choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\). In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three atoms \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit after simplification. Circuit lifting gives an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq K\cup\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}
\]
with
\[
D\setminus K=\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}.
\]
Apply circuit-incidence for \(p\) to \(B\) and \(D\). Terms coming from \(D\cap K\) are \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{B\cup e_i}=\bar p(H_i)=u(P_i).
\]
Therefore
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces
\[
u(P_3)\ge\lambda.
\]
So \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed.

It remains to show that in a finite simple modular matroid, every line-closed set of points is a flat. Let \(\mathcal S\) be line-closed. We prove that every point in \(\operatorname{cl}(\mathcal S)\) lies in \(\mathcal S\). Choose an independent set
\[
b_1,\dots,b_m\in\mathcal S
\]
spanning a flat \(F\). We prove by induction on \(m\) that every point of \(F\) lies in \(\mathcal S\).

For \(m\le2\), this is exactly line-closedness. Suppose \(m\ge3\), and let
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}(b_1,\dots,b_{m-1}).
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(\mathcal S\). Let \(p\) be a point of
\[
F=G\vee b_m.
\]
If \(p\le G\) or \(p=b_m\), then \(p\in\mathcal S\). Otherwise consider the line
\[
\ell=p\vee b_m.
\]
Since \(M\) is modular,
\[
\operatorname{rk}(\ell\wedge G)
=
\operatorname{rk}(\ell)+\operatorname{rk}(G)-\operatorname{rk}(F)
=
2+(m-1)-m
=
1.
\]
Thus \(\ell\wedge G\) is a point \(q\in G\), hence \(q\in\mathcal S\). Since \(q,b_m\in\mathcal S\) and \(\mathcal S\) is line-closed, every point of the line \(q\vee b_m=\ell\), including \(p\), lies in \(\mathcal S\).

Thus line-closed point sets are flats. Therefore every \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is a flat, so \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). \(\square\)

We return to the quotient-incidence relation for \(p^\dagger\). For \(c\in X\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so
\[
D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)=\infty.
\]
For \(c\notin X\), the flat \(P=X\vee c\) is a point of \(M/X\), and
\[
D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)
=
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p(P^\perp)
=
u(P).
\]
By Lemma 4.3, \(u\) lies in the Bergman fan of the simplification of \(M/X\). Lifting \(u\) to \(M/X\) gives a vector with value \(\infty\) on loops and equal values on parallel elements, hence an element of \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\).

If \(C\subseteq X\), then all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in the contraction \(M/X\). If the minimum of \(D_A^{p^\dagger}\) on \(C\) had a unique finite minimizer \(e\), then some circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\) and containing \(e\) would also have a unique finite minimum, contradicting the fact that the lifted \(u\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\).

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the quotient circuit-incidence relations. Hence \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

#### 4.5. Involutivity

Since \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\), the flat-constancy argument applies to \(p^\dagger\) as well.

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then
\[
p_I=\infty,
\qquad
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]
If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p\bigl((F^\perp)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

#### 4.6. Compatibility with ordinary flat quotients

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Let \(f=\operatorname{rk}(F)\). The rank of \(p^F\) is \(r-f\). Its finite bases are precisely the \(M\)-bases of rank-\((r-f)\) flats \(G\) complementary to \(F\), meaning
\[
G\wedge F=0,
\qquad
G\vee F=E.
\]
Indeed, if \(G\) is spanned by a basis of \(M/F\), then \(G\vee F=E\), and modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(G\wedge F)
=
\operatorname{rk}(G)+\operatorname{rk}(F)-\operatorname{rk}(G\vee F)
=
(r-f)+f-r=0.
\]
The converse is immediate from the rank formula.

Now let \(H\) be a rank-\(f\) flat. The dagger \((p^F)^\dagger\) is finite on \(M\)-bases of \(H\) exactly when \(H^\perp\) is complementary to \(F\). By polarity,
\[
H^\perp\wedge F=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
H^\perp\vee F=E
\]
are equivalent to
\[
H\vee F^\perp=E
\quad\text{and}\quad
H\wedge F^\perp=0.
\]
That is, \(H\) is complementary to \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}
\]
with the same normalization of finite coordinates.

---

### 5. The point-annihilator theorem

Continue to assume \(M\) is simple, loopless, modular of rank \(r\), with flat polarity.

For an \(M\)-basis
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},
\]
define
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Because \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is a rank-one flat, identified with its unique element.

For \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x
=
\left\{
y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M):
\forall M\text{-bases }B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},
\quad
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

We prove:

#### Theorem 5.1: affine point-annihilator theorem

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Then for every
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M),
\]
one has
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\iff
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

**Proof.**

We first record the Boolean-apartment calculation. Let
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
be an \(M\)-basis, and put
\[
H_i=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\}).
\]
For \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}H_i.
\]
One inclusion is immediate. For the other, if an element \(e\) lies in every \(H_i\) with \(i\notin S\), then its fundamental circuit over \(B\), if \(e\notin B\), uses only elements \(b_i\) with \(i\in S\). Hence \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}\). The case \(e\in B\) is immediate.

Applying polarity gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
In particular,
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and the operation \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on the set of \(M\)-bases.

Assume first \(0<k<r\). Let
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Because every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis, \(\mu_B\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \([r]\). The preceding Boolean-apartment identity gives, for \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}
\right)
=
\bar p\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}
\right)
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin R\}}.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

Now suppose
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
Let \(y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). For every \(M\)-basis \(B\),
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B).
\]
By ordinary affine tropical annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(B\), we have
\[
y\in\widetilde H_x.
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, assume
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\). We prove
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Let \(T\subseteq[r]\) have size
\[
r-k+1,
\]
and put
\[
S=[r]\setminus T,
\qquad |S|=k-1.
\]
Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The global coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\), hence in \(\widetilde H_x\). Testing the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i\bigl(D_A^p(b_i)+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
=
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

But this is exactly the circuit equation for
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*),
\]
because
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T\setminus\{i\}}
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus(T\setminus\{i\}))
=
\mu_B(S\cup\{i\})
=
p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
Thus \(x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*)\) for every \(B\). Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts correctly to every \(M\)-basis.

It remains to check all global circuit equations of \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
J\in\binom E{r-k+1}.
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(C\). The restriction of \(x\) to \(C\) lies in the tropical linear space of \(p^\dagger|_C\), so
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus\{j\}}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), then every \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, and all terms are \(\infty\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). For \(j\notin C\), \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so the corresponding term is \(\infty\). For \(j\in C\), the set \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is \(M\)-independent and has closure \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\), so
\[
p^\dagger_{J\setminus\{j\}}
\]
is independent of \(j\in C\). If this common value is \(\infty\), the equation is automatic. Otherwise the equation reduces to the \(M\)-circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in C}x_j
\]
for \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

Thus
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]

It remains to check the boundary ranks.

If \(k=0\), then
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\},
\qquad
p^\dagger=M
\]
up to scalar. Hence for every \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\),
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger),
\]
and also \(\{\infty^E\}\subseteq\widetilde H_x\), since all basis sums are \(\infty\).

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero:
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\). Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) and an index \(i\). Let
\[
A=B\setminus\{b_i\}.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Testing \(D_A^p\in\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\), the only possibly finite term is
\[
p_B+x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Therefore this term cannot be finite, so
\[
x_{b_i^*}=\infty.
\]
As \(B\) and \(i\) vary, the elements \(b_i^*\) cover all of \(E\), because \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases. Hence \(x=\infty^E\). Thus the theorem also holds for \(k=r\). \(\square\)

---

### 6. Construction of the involution and projectivization

We first finish the proof in the simple loopless modular case.

For an affine tropical linear space
\[
X=\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M),
\]
define
\[
\widetilde\Phi(X)
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
This is well-defined on affine tropical linear spaces by Standard Fact 4: replacing \(p\) by \(p+\lambda\) replaces \(p^\dagger\) by \(p^\dagger+\lambda\), which does not change the tropical linear space.

Equivalently, by the point-annihilator theorem,
\[
\widetilde\Phi(X)
=
\{x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M):X\subseteq \widetilde H_x\}.
\]
This intrinsic formula also makes order reversal immediate. If
\[
X\subseteq Y,
\]
then every polar hyperplane containing \(Y\) also contains \(X\). Therefore
\[
\widetilde\Phi(Y)\subseteq\widetilde\Phi(X).
\]
The identity
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
proved above gives
\[
\widetilde\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
Thus \(\widetilde\Phi\) is an inclusion-reversing involution on the affine relative Dressian.

For the ordinary flat quotient indexed by a flat \(F\), Section 4.6 gives
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde\Phi\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Now pass to projective tropical spaces. All constructions commute with tropical scalar translation. The all-\(\infty\) vector is present in every affine cone and is deleted in projectivization. Rank-zero affine spaces become empty projective spaces. Thus \(\widetilde\Phi\) descends to an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
in the simple loopless modular case, with the required flat-object compatibility.

Finally return to an arbitrary finite matroid \(M\). If \(M\) has rank \(0\), the projective \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) is empty and \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) has one object, so the identity map is the desired involution.

Otherwise let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). By Section 2,
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
is a poset isomorphism compatible with flat quotients, and the flat-lattice polarity on \(M\) descends to one on \(S\). By Section 3, \(S\) is modular. Construct \(\Phi_S\) on \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\) by the simple modular construction, and define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Because \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism and \(\Phi_S\) is an inclusion-reversing involution, \(\Phi_M\) is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\in\mathcal L(S)\). Compatibility of \(\Delta_*\) with flat quotients gives
\[
\Delta_*
\bigl(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]
Since the polarity on \(S\) is \(\bar F\mapsto\psi(F^\perp)\),
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This proves the theorem. \(\square\)

   T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i}H_j
   \]
   has rank \(k-1\): it has rank at least \(k-1\), and if it had rank \(k\), intersecting with the remaining hyperplane \(H_i\) could lower rank by at most \(1\), giving \(\rho(Z)\ge k-1\), contradiction.

4. **Patch circuit lifting.** Insert the standard lemma:

   > Let \(X\) be a flat of \(M\). If \(D\) is a circuit of the simplification of \(M/X\) and one representative \(e_P\) is chosen from each parallel class \(P\in D\), then there exists an \(M\)-circuit
   > \[
   > C\subseteq X\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
   > \]
   > with \(C\setminus X=\{e_P:P\in D\}\).

   Proof: the chosen representatives form a circuit of \(M/X\). By the circuit description of contraction, some \(M\)-circuit \(C\) has \(C\setminus X\) contained in those representatives. Minimality of \(D\) in the simplification forces equality.

   Also add: if \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit not contained in \(X\), then every \(e\in C\setminus X\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\). Indeed,
   \[
   e\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus\{e\})\subseteq
   \operatorname{cl}_M(X\cup(C\setminus X\setminus\{e\})),
   \]
   so \(e\) is dependent on \(C\setminus X\setminus\{e\}\) in \(M/X\), and a minimal dependent subset gives the desired circuit.

5. **Patch Section 2.3 wording.** Replace the flat description by:

   \[
   F\in\mathcal L(M)
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F)
   \text{ for a flat }\bar F\in\mathcal L(\operatorname{si}(M)).
   \]
   Then \(\psi(F)=\bar F\) is a lattice isomorphism, and the displayed rank computation proves compatibility of flat quotients.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The four “Standard Facts” from valuated matroid theory are central black boxes. They are correct, but the proof should either cite them or include short derivations, especially for affine/projective conventions and boundary ranks.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The proof verifies only the three-term tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\) but does not explicitly state the standard cryptomorphism that these relations, with nonzero support, are sufficient to define a valuated matroid.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Several standard matroid-theoretic facts are invoked tersely: circuit lifting from contractions, the claim that \(C\setminus X\) contains a contraction circuit through a chosen element, and rank computations for intersections of hyperplanes in a modular lattice. These are all patchable but should be stated.
- EXPOSITION ONLY — Section 2.3 says “Flats of \(M\) are exactly the loop set together with unions of non-loop parallel classes”; this should read “the loop set together with inverse images of flats of the simplification.” The intended lattice isomorphism is correct.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Patch the valuated-matroid black boxes.** Insert after the list of Standard Facts:

   - For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same finite ground set, use the standard theorem that \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\) iff \(\overline{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \overline{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\). The affine version follows because the affine tropical linear space is the cone over the projective one, together with \(\infty^E\).

   - For the coordinate cocircuit claim: if \(A\in\binom{E}{d-1}\), then for every \(J\in\binom{E}{d+1}\),
     \[
     \min_{j\in J}\bigl(D_A^p(j)+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
     =
     \min_{j\in J\setminus A}\bigl(p_{A\cup j}+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr),
     \]
     which is exactly a tropical Plücker relation for \(p\). Hence \(D_A^p\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\).

   - For the annihilator duality: circuits of \(\mu^*\) are cocircuits of \(\mu\). Thus
     \[
     u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
     \]
     iff \(u\) is tropically orthogonal to every valuated cocircuit of \(\mu\). Since \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\) is the tropical span of the valuated cocircuits and tropical hyperplanes \(H_u\) are tropically convex, this is equivalent to
     \[
     \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
     \]
     The rank \(0\) and full-rank boundary cases are exactly as stated in the proof.

   - For uniqueness, cite the standard result that \(L_p\) determines \(p\) up to global tropical scalar; alternatively define \(\Phi(X)\) intrinsically by

     \Phi(X)=\{x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M):X\subseteq \widetilde H_x\},
