original_problem:
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?
claim_verified: Modulo the listed standard valuated-matroid facts and routine matroid patches, the proof establishes the finite-ground-set version: if a finite matroid \(M\) has an order-reversing involution on its lattice of flats, then the relative Dressian of tropical linear subspaces contained in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) admits an inclusion-reversing involution extending \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) on the embedded flat-quotient objects.
solution_verified:
## Theorem proved

Let \(M\) be a **finite** matroid on ground set \(E\). Suppose the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Let \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) denote the poset of projective tropical linear spaces contained in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), ordered by inclusion. Then there is an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\to \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
such that for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

We prove this finite-ground-set version.

---

## 1. Conventions and standard facts

Work over \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), with minimum convention. A minimum is said to be attained at least twice if it has no unique finite minimizer.

For a rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), write
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \mathbb T^E
\]
for its affine tropical linear space:
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
\iff
\forall J\in \binom E{d+1},\quad
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}.
\]
For \(d=0\), this gives \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\). Projectivization deletes \(\infty^E\) and quotients by \(x\sim x+\lambda\mathbf 1\), \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\).

We use the following standard valuated-matroid facts.

1. **Plücker cryptomorphism.** A nonzero vector \(p\in \mathbb T^{\binom Ed}\) is a rank \(d\) valuated matroid iff it satisfies the three-term tropical Plücker relations
   \[
   \min\bigl(
   p_{Sij}+p_{Skl},\,
   p_{Sik}+p_{Sjl},\,
   p_{Sil}+p_{Sjk}
   \bigr)
   \]
   with no unique finite minimizer for every \(S\in \binom E{d-2}\) and distinct \(i,j,k,l\notin S\). Equivalently, it satisfies the usual valuated basis-exchange relations.

2. **Quotient-containment theorem.** For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same finite ground set,
   \[
   \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
   \]
   iff \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\). If \(q=M\) is an ordinary matroid with constant valuation, then for \(\operatorname{rk}(p)=d>0\) this is equivalent to the following circuit-incidence condition: for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \(A\in \binom E{d-1}\),
   \[
   \min_{c\in C}D_A^p(c)
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer, where
   \[
   D_A^p(e)=
   \begin{cases}
   p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
   \infty,& e\in A.
   \end{cases}
   \]
   For \(d=0\), \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), so containment is automatic.

3. **Coordinate cocircuits.** If \(p\) has rank \(d>0\), then for every \(A\in \binom E{d-1}\), the vector \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). Indeed, for every \(J\in \binom E{d+1}\),
   \[
   \min_{j\in J}\bigl(D_A^p(j)+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
   =
   \min_{j\in J\setminus A}\bigl(p_{A\cup j}+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr),
   \]
   which is one of the valuated Plücker exchange relations.

4. **Affine annihilator duality.** If \(\mu\) is a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\), and
   \[
   H_u^N=\left\{v\in\mathbb T^N:
   \min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
   \text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
   \right\},
   \]
   then
   \[
   u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
   \iff
   \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\subseteq H_u^N.
   \]
   This includes the rank \(0\) and full-rank boundary cases.

5. **Uniqueness.** The affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\) determines \(p\) up to adding a global tropical scalar to all Plücker coordinates.

We also use the following elementary characterization of ordinary Bergman fans.

### Lemma 1.1: superlevel-flat criterion

Let \(N\) be an ordinary matroid on a finite set \(E_N\). For \(w\in\mathbb T^{E_N}\),
\[
w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)
\]
iff for every real \(\lambda\), the superlevel set
\[
S_\lambda(w)=\{e\in E_N:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

Proof. If \(w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\) and \(S_\lambda(w)\) is not flat, choose \(e\notin S_\lambda(w)\) with \(e\in \operatorname{cl}_N(S_\lambda(w))\). Then there is a circuit
\[
C\subseteq S_\lambda(w)\cup\{e\}
\]
containing \(e\). On \(C\), the coordinate \(e\) is the unique finite minimum, contradiction.

Conversely, suppose every \(S_\lambda(w)\) is a flat. If some circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\), choose \(\lambda\) strictly between \(w_e\) and all other finite values on \(C\), and larger than \(w_e\) if all other values are \(\infty\). Then
\[
C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq S_\lambda(w),
\qquad
e\notin S_\lambda(w),
\]
but \(e\in \operatorname{cl}_N(C\setminus\{e\})\subseteq \operatorname{cl}_N(S_\lambda(w))\), contradicting flatness. ∎

---

## 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops of \(M\). Let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\), and let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map. Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\), viewed as a simple matroid on \(\bar E\).

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
This commutes with tropical scalar translation, so it descends projectively.

For a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\), define its zero-parallel extension \(q=\Delta\bar q\) on \(E\) by
\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing
\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is again a valuated matroid: the Plücker relations either project to those of \(\bar q\), or have all terms \(\infty\), or have equal finite terms coming from interchangeable parallel representatives. Moreover,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]
The loop circuits force coordinates in \(L_0\) to be \(\infty\), the two-element parallel circuits force equal coordinates within each parallel class, and the remaining equations are exactly those of \(\bar q\).

In particular,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr).
\]

Conversely, suppose \(q\) is a valuated matroid on \(E\) with
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
If \(\operatorname{rk}(q)=0\), then \(q\) is the zero-parallel extension of the unique rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Assume \(\operatorname{rk}(q)=d>0\).

For every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\), the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^q\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Hence every finite \(q\)-basis is \(M\)-independent: if a finite \(q\)-basis \(I\) contained an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), choose \(c\in C\) and set \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then \(D_A^q(c)=q_I<\infty\), while \(D_A^q(e)=\infty\) for \(e\in C\setminus\{c\}\), contradicting the \(M\)-circuit equation.

If \(e,f\) are parallel non-loops in \(M\), then \(\{e,f\}\) is an \(M\)-circuit, so every \(D_A^q\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) satisfies
\[
D_A^q(e)=D_A^q(f).
\]
Thus no finite \(q\)-basis contains two elements from the same parallel class, and replacement of one representative by another preserves both finiteness and valuation.

Therefore \(q\) descends to a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\): for \(\bar I\in \binom{\bar E}{d}\), choose any representative set \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\), and set
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]
This is independent of representatives, satisfies the Plücker relations, and \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\). Since
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr)
\subseteq
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr),
\]
and \(\Delta\) is injective on \(\mathbb T^{\bar E}\), we get
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S).
\]

Thus \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

Now describe flats. A subset \(F\subseteq E\) is a flat of \(M\) iff
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F)
\]
for a flat \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\). Hence
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism.

For a flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). The matroid
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
has rank function
\[
r_{N_F}(A)=r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F).
\]
Using
\[
r_M(A)=r_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)),
\]
we obtain
\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-r_S(\bar F).
\]
Thus \(N_F\) is the zero-parallel extension of
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Consequently \(\Delta_*\) identifies the flat object indexed by \(\bar F\) for \(S\) with the flat object indexed by \(F\) for \(M\).

The given order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) therefore descends to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\),
\[
\bar F\longmapsto \psi\bigl(\psi^{-1}(\bar F)^\perp\bigr).
\]

It remains to prove the theorem for finite simple loopless matroids. The general case will then follow by conjugating through \(\Delta_*\).

---

## 3. A polarity forces modularity

Let \(L\) be a finite geometric lattice of rank \(r\), with rank function \(\rho\), and suppose \(L\) has an order-reversing involution \(X\mapsto X^\perp\).

Since \(L\) is graded,
\[
\rho(X^\perp)=r-\rho(X).
\]
Geometric lattices are semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives
\[
\rho(X^\perp)+\rho(Y^\perp)
\ge
\rho(X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp)+\rho(X^\perp\vee Y^\perp).
\]
Using De Morgan identities,
\[
X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp=(X\vee Y)^\perp,
\qquad
X^\perp\vee Y^\perp=(X\wedge Y)^\perp,
\]
and substituting \(\rho(Z^\perp)=r-\rho(Z)\), we get the opposite inequality
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\le \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Therefore equality holds for all \(X,Y\), so \(L\) is modular.

Hence, after simplification, we may assume:

- \(M\) is finite, simple, and loopless;
- \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular;
- \(M\) has rank \(r\);
- the flat-lattice polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is fixed.

We now construct the involution in this case.

---

## 4. The dagger construction

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), so
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
Then \(0\le k\le r\).

Assume first \(k>0\). By the quotient-incidence condition, for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\),
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^p(c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

### Lemma 4.1: flat constancy

For every rank \(k\) flat \(F\) of \(M\), the value \(p_I\) is constant as \(I\) ranges over the \(M\)-bases of \(F\). Denote this common value by \(\bar p(F)\).

Proof. First, every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent. If \(I\) is a finite \(p\)-basis containing an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), choose \(c\in C\) and set \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then \(D_A^p(c)=p_I<\infty\), while all other elements of \(C\) lie in \(A\) and hence have value \(\infty\), a contradiction.

Now suppose
\[
I=A\cup\{a\},
\qquad
I'=A\cup\{b\}
\]
are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with the same closure. Then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying the quotient-incidence condition to \(A\) and \(C\), the only possibly finite terms are \(p_{A\cup a}\) and \(p_{A\cup b}\), so
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
The basis graph of the restriction \(M|F\) is connected, and adjacent bases differ by such an exchange. Hence \(p_I\) is constant on the bases of \(F\). ∎

We also need the following consequence.

### Lemma 4.2

Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis.

Proof. Choose one finite \(p\)-basis \(I\). It is \(M\)-independent, so extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\). Let \(B\) be any \(M\)-basis. Connect \(B_0\) to \(B\) in the basis graph of \(M\). It suffices to pass across one exchange
\[
B''=B'-\{b\}\cup\{e\}.
\]
Assume \(I\subseteq B'\) is a finite \(p\)-basis. If \(b\notin I\), then \(I\subseteq B''\). If \(b\in I\), put \(A=I\setminus\{b\}\). Let \(C\subseteq B'\cup\{e\}\) be the fundamental circuit containing \(b\) and \(e\). Since \(p_{A\cup b}<\infty\), the quotient-incidence condition forces another finite term \(p_{A\cup c}<\infty\) for some
\[
c\in C\setminus(A\cup\{b\}).
\]
Then \(A\cup\{c\}\subseteq B''\) is a finite \(p\)-basis. ∎

Define the dagger \(p^\dagger\) as follows.

- If \(k=0\), let \(p^\dagger\) be the constant valuated matroid \(M\), shifted by \(p_\varnothing\).

- If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar shift of \(M\), say all \(M\)-basis values equal \(c\). Let \(p^\dagger\) be the rank-zero valuated matroid with coordinate \(c\).

- If \(0<k<r\), put \(n=r-k\). For \(J\in\binom En\), define
  \[
  p^\dagger_J=
  \begin{cases}
  \bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),
  &J\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
  \infty,
  &J\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
  \end{cases}
  \]

For \(0<k<r\), \(p^\dagger\not\equiv \infty\): choose a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), set \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), and choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

---

## 5. \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid

We verify the three-term tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Only the case \(0<k<r\) needs proof.

We first prove two auxiliary lemmas.

### Lemma 5.1: rank-three edge lemma

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let
\[
w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]
For lines \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) of \(N\), define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),&L_i\neq L_j,\\
\infty,&L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\,
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\,
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Proof. If two lines coincide, say \(L_1=L_2\), then \(\langle12\rangle=\infty\), while
\[
\langle13\rangle=\langle23\rangle,
\qquad
\langle14\rangle=\langle24\rangle.
\]
Thus the second and third matching sums are equal.

Assume now that the \(L_i\) are distinct. If \(w=\infty^E\), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise subtract a global scalar so that \(\min w=0\). By Lemma 1.1, the positive superlevel sets of \(w\) are nested proper flats. In a simple rank-three matroid, such a chain is contained in a point-line flag. Thus there exist a line \(B\), a point \(a\le B\), and \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\) such that
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}.
\]

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For \(i\neq j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\iff
y_i=y_j.
\]
Thus an edge \(\{i,j\}\) has nonzero cost only when the colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree. Among four colored objects, the minimum perfect-matching cost is never unique: the color multiplicities are \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\), and in each case at least two matchings attain the minimum.

Now suppose \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put \(y_j=L_j\wedge B\), and define
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,&y=a,\\
\beta,&y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of the \(c(y_j)\) equal the minimum value \(\beta\). If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of that equal pair tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three matching costs are equal. ∎

### Lemma 5.2: local four-hyperplane lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
[12]+[34],\,
[13]+[24],\,
[14]+[23]
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Proof. If two hyperplanes coincide, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Thus the second and third matching sums are equal.

Assume the four hyperplanes are distinct. Since the lattice is modular, two distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\). Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
In a modular lattice, intersecting a flat \(A\le Y\) with a hyperplane \(H\le Y\) lowers rank by at most one:
\[
\rho(A\wedge H)
=
\rho(A)+\rho(H)-\rho(A\vee H)
\ge \rho(A)-1.
\]
Therefore \(\rho(Z)\ge k-2\). Since \(Z\le H_i\wedge H_j\), we also have \(\rho(Z)\le k\).

If \(\rho(Z)=k\), then all pairwise meets \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equal \(Z\), so all three matching sums are equal.

Suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
Then \(\rho(T_i)=k-1\): the rank is at least \(k-1\), and if it were \(k\), intersecting with the remaining \(H_i\) would lower rank by at most one, forcing \(\rho(Z)\ge k-1\), contradiction.

Also
\[
T_i\wedge T_j=Z.
\]
If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then \(T_m,T_n\le H_i\wedge H_j\), and modularity gives
\[
\rho(T_m\vee T_n)
=
(k-1)+(k-1)-(k-2)
=
k.
\]
Since \(H_i\wedge H_j\) also has rank \(k\), we get
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose
\[
t_i\in T_i\setminus Z.
\]
Then \(A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}\) is an \(M\)-basis of \(H_i\wedge H_j\). The three-term Plücker relation for \(p\), applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\), is exactly
\[
\min\bigl(
[12]+[34],\,
[13]+[24],\,
[14]+[23]
\bigr),
\]
so the desired condition holds.

It remains to consider \(\rho(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\), after simplification, is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid \(N\). Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying \(Z<P<Y\). Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(N\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each point \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). By circuit lifting from the contraction by \(Z\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
with
\[
C\setminus Z=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
\]
Applying the quotient-incidence condition for \(p\) to \(A\) and \(C\), the elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Hence \(\min_{P\in D}w(P)\) has no unique finite minimizer, proving \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\).

Each \(H_i\) descends to a line \(L_i=H_i/Z\) of \(N\), and \(H_i\wedge H_j\) descends to \(L_i\wedge L_j\). The result follows from Lemma 5.1. ∎

Now verify the Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2},
\qquad
a,b,c,d\in E\setminus S
\]
be distinct. We need
\[
\min\bigl(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},\,
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},\,
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\bigr)
\]
to have no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every matching sum has an \(\infty\) summand. So assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\).

Set
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
These are hyperplanes of \(Y\). For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
S\cup\{e,f\}
\]
is \(M\)-dependent iff \(X\vee e=X\vee f\), equivalently \(H_e=H_f\). If it is independent, then
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\bar p\bigl((X\vee e\vee f)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]
Thus the desired Plücker relation is exactly Lemma 5.2. Hence \(p^\dagger\) is a rank \(n\) valuated matroid.

---

## 6. \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\)

We prove the circuit-incidence relations for \(p^\dagger\).

We use two standard contraction facts.

- If \(X\) is a flat of \(M\), \(D\) is a circuit of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\), and one representative \(e_P\) is chosen from each parallel class \(P\in D\), then there is an \(M\)-circuit
  \[
  C\subseteq X\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
  \]
  such that
  \[
  C\setminus X=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
  \]

- If \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit not contained in \(X\), then every \(e\in C\setminus X\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\). Indeed,
  \[
  e\in \operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus\{e\})
  \subseteq
  \operatorname{cl}_M\bigl(X\cup(C\setminus X\setminus\{e\})\bigr),
  \]
  so \(e\) is dependent on \(C\setminus X\setminus\{e\}\) in \(M/X\), and a minimal dependent subset gives the desired circuit.

### Lemma 6.1: codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a rank \(n-1\) flat of \(M\). Let \(N=\operatorname{si}(M/X)\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]

Proof. By Lemma 1.1, it suffices to prove that every real superlevel set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

First we prove \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed. Let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line spanned by them. If \(P_3=P_1\) or \(P_3=P_2\), there is nothing to show.

Assume \(P_1,P_2,P_3\) are distinct. Put
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\qquad
H_i=P_i^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). Since \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\),
\[
H_3=P_3^\perp\supseteq P_1^\perp\wedge P_2^\perp=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Let
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(K)=k-1\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose
\[
e_i\in H_i\setminus K.
\]
In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three atoms \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit after simplification. By circuit lifting, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq K\cup\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}
\]
with
\[
D\setminus K=\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}.
\]
Apply the quotient-incidence condition for \(p\) to \(B\) and \(D\). Elements of \(D\cap K\) contribute \(\infty\), and
\[
p_{B\cup e_i}
=
\bar p(H_i)
=
u(P_i).
\]
Thus
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge \lambda\), this forces \(u(P_3)\ge \lambda\). Hence \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed.

Now in a finite simple modular matroid, every line-closed set of points is a flat. Indeed, let \(\mathcal S\) be line-closed, and let \(F=\operatorname{cl}(\mathcal S)\). Choose an independent set \(b_1,\dots,b_m\in\mathcal S\) spanning \(F\). We prove by induction on \(m\) that every point of \(F\) belongs to \(\mathcal S\). The cases \(m\le 2\) are precisely line-closedness. For \(m\ge3\), put
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}(b_1,\dots,b_{m-1}).
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(\mathcal S\). Let \(p\) be a point of \(F\). If \(p\le G\) or \(p=b_m\), we are done. Otherwise consider the line
\[
\ell=p\vee b_m.
\]
By modularity,
\[
\operatorname{rk}(\ell\wedge G)
=
\operatorname{rk}(\ell)+\operatorname{rk}(G)-\operatorname{rk}(F)
=
2+(m-1)-m
=
1.
\]
Thus \(\ell\wedge G\) is a point \(q\in G\), hence \(q\in\mathcal S\). Since \(q,b_m\in\mathcal S\), line-closedness implies every point of \(\ell=q\vee b_m\), including \(p\), lies in \(\mathcal S\).

Therefore each \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is a flat, and Lemma 1.1 gives \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). ∎

Now let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit and \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\). We show
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup\{c\}\) is dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent. Let
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-1\). For \(c\in X\), \(A\cup\{c\}\) is dependent, so
\[
D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)=\infty.
\]
For \(c\notin X\), the flat
\[
P=X\vee c
\]
is a point of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\), and
\[
D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)
=
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p(P^\perp)
=
u(P).
\]
By Lemma 6.1, the lifted vector on \(M/X\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\).

If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise, if the minimum on \(C\) had a unique finite minimizer \(e\), then by the contraction fact above, \(e\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\). On that contraction circuit, the lifted vector would again have a unique finite minimum, contradiction.

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the quotient-incidence relations, so
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]

---

## 7. Involutivity and flat quotients

Since \(p^\dagger\) is again a quotient of \(M\), the flat-constancy lemma applies to it.

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then
\[
p_I=\infty
\quad\text{and}\quad
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]
If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, let
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p\bigl((F^\perp)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Now let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) denote the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Let \(f=\operatorname{rk}(F)\). The finite bases of \(p^F\) are precisely the \(M\)-bases of rank \(r-f\) flats \(G\) complementary to \(F\), i.e.
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing,
\qquad
G\vee F=E.
\]
Indeed, \(G\vee F=E\) says that \(G\) projects to a spanning set in \(M/F\), and modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(G\wedge F)
=
\operatorname{rk}(G)+\operatorname{rk}(F)-\operatorname{rk}(G\vee F)
=
(r-f)+f-r
=
0.
\]

For a rank \(f\) flat \(H\), \((p^F)^\dagger\) is finite on the \(M\)-bases of \(H\) exactly when \(H^\perp\) is complementary to \(F\). By applying polarity, this is equivalent to \(H\) being complementary to \(F^\perp\). Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^F)
\longmapsto
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^{F^\perp})
\]
under the dagger operation.

---

## 8. The annihilator description and order reversal

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
b_i^*
=
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Since \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is a rank-one flat, hence a single element.

For \(S\subseteq [r]\), write \(B_S=\{b_i:i\in S\}\). Then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B_S)
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\}).
\]
Indeed, the inclusion \(\subseteq\) is clear. Conversely, if \(e\) lies in every hyperplane \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})\) for \(i\notin S\), then its fundamental circuit over \(B\) uses only elements \(b_i\) with \(i\in S\), so \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_M(B_S)\).

Applying polarity gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B_S)^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
In particular,
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on the set of \(M\)-bases.

For \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x
=
\left\{
y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M):
\forall M\text{-bases }B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},
\quad
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

### Theorem 8.1: point-annihilator theorem

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). For every \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\),
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\iff
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Proof. First assume \(0<k<r\). Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis, and define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
(\mu_B)_T=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
By Lemma 4.2, \(\mu_B\) is not identically \(\infty\).

For \(R\subseteq [r]\) with \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^\perp\right)
=
\bar p\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}\right)
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin R\}}.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

Suppose \(x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\) and \(y\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). Then for every \(M\)-basis \(B\),
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B).
\]
By affine annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(y\in\widetilde H_x\), so
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, assume
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\). We prove
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and put
\[
S=[r]\setminus T,
\qquad |S|=k-1.
\]
Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\), hence in \(\widetilde H_x\). Testing the defining condition of \(\widetilde H_x\) on \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. But
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T\setminus\{i\}}
=
(\mu_B)_{S\cup\{i\}}
=
p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
Therefore this is exactly the circuit equation for
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on the set of \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts correctly to every \(M\)-basis.

It remains to check the global circuit equations for \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
J\in \binom E{r-k+1}.
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(C\). Since \(x|_C\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger|_C)\), the circuit equation for \(J\) holds.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), then every \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has nullity \(1\), hence contains a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). For \(j\notin C\), \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is dependent. For \(j\in C\), \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is independent and has closure \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\), so the values
\[
p^\dagger_{J\setminus\{j\}}
\]
are independent of \(j\in C\). If this common value is \(\infty\), the equation is automatic. Otherwise it reduces to the ordinary \(M\)-circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in C}x_j
\]
for \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Hence
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]

The boundary cases are straightforward.

If \(k=0\), then
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\},
\qquad
p^\dagger=M
\]
up to scalar, so both sides hold for every \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar shift of \(M\), while \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Thus
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\). Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) and an index \(i\). Put
\[
A=B\setminus\{b_i\}.
\]
Then \(D_A^p\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Testing \(D_A^p\in\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\), the only possibly finite term is
\[
p_B+x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Therefore this term cannot be finite, so \(x_{b_i^*}=\infty\). Since the elements \(b_i^*\) range over all of \(E\) as \(B\) and \(i\) vary, \(x=\infty^E\). ∎

---

## 9. Construction of the involution in the simple modular case

For an affine tropical linear space
\[
X=\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M),
\]
define
\[
\widetilde\Phi(X)
=
\left\{
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M):
X\subseteq \widetilde H_x
\right\}.
\]
By Theorem 8.1,
\[
\widetilde\Phi\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\bigr)
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\widetilde\Phi(X)\) is again an affine tropical linear space contained in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

If \(X\subseteq Y\), then
\[
Y\subseteq \widetilde H_x
\implies
X\subseteq \widetilde H_x,
\]
so
\[
\widetilde\Phi(Y)\subseteq \widetilde\Phi(X).
\]
Hence \(\widetilde\Phi\) reverses inclusion. Since
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p,
\]
we also have
\[
\widetilde\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
Thus \(\widetilde\Phi\) is an inclusion-reversing involution on the affine relative Dressian.

For flat quotients, Section 7 gives
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde\Phi\!\left(
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
\right)
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

All constructions commute with tropical scalar translation. After deleting \(\infty^E\) and quotienting by \(\mathbb R\mathbf 1\), \(\widetilde\Phi\) descends to an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\to \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
in the simple loopless modular case, with the required behavior on the embedded flat quotients.

---

## 10. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

If \(\operatorname{rk}(M)=0\), then \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) is empty projectively, and \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) has a single object. The identity map is the desired involution.

Assume \(\operatorname{rk}(M)>0\). Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). By Section 2,
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
is a poset isomorphism compatible with the embedded flat quotients. The polarity on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends through
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
to a polarity on \(\mathcal L(S)\), and by Section 3 the lattice \(\mathcal L(S)\) is modular. Therefore Section 9 gives an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to \operatorname{Dr}(S).
\]
Define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Since \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism and \(\Phi_S\) is an inclusion-reversing involution, \(\Phi_M\) is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Finally, let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and set \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Compatibility of \(\Delta_*\) with flat quotients gives
\[
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]
The polarity on \(S\) sends \(\bar F\) to \(\psi(F^\perp)\). Hence
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Thus \(\Phi_M\) is the desired inclusion-reversing involution extending the given flat-lattice involution. ∎

**Major missing lemma needed.** Replace Fact 1 by the correct criterion:

> A function \(q:\binom E d\to\mathbb T\), not identically \(\infty\), is a valuated matroid iff its finite support \(\{I:q_I<\infty\}\) is the set of bases of an ordinary matroid and the finite values satisfy the valuated basis-exchange axiom; equivalently, one may verify the full tropical Plücker relations.

To repair Section 5, one must additionally prove for the constructed \(p^\dagger\) that
\[

=
\{J\in\binom E{r-k}: J\text{ is }M\text{-independent and }

\]
is the set of bases of a matroid, and that the values \(p^\dagger_J\) satisfy full valuated basis exchange. The current proof supplies only the local 3-term relations, which is insufficient. I do not see a complete local patch in the given argument; this requires a new nontrivial argument.

**Routine patch: contraction circuit lifting.** If \(X\) is a flat and \(D\) is a circuit of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\), choose representatives \(e_P\) in the corresponding parallel classes of \(M/X\). The representative set is dependent in \(M/X\), and no proper subset meeting fewer classes is dependent by minimality of \(D\). Hence it contains a circuit \(D'\) of \(M/X\) meeting exactly those classes. By the standard description of circuits of a contraction, there is an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq X\cup D'\) with \(C\setminus X=D'\). This is the lifting used in Lemmas 5.2 and 6.1.

**Routine patch: modularity under contraction/simplification.** For a flat \(X\), the lattice of flats of \(M/X\) is the interval \([X,E]\) in \(\mathcal L(M)\). If \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular, then every interval is modular. Simplification does not change the lattice of flats except for replacing parallel classes by single points. Therefore \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) is a finite simple modular matroid.

**Routine patch: \(B^*\)-involution.** From
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B_S)^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\},
\]
taking \(S=\{i\}\) gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B^*\setminus\{b_i^*\})
=
\operatorname{cl}_M(\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Applying \(\perp\) again gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B^*\setminus\{b_i^*\})^\perp=\operatorname{cl}_M(b_i),
\]
so, since \(M\) is simple, the star of \(b_i^*\) relative to \(B^*\) is \(b_i\). Thus \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution. Consequently, as \(B\) ranges over bases, \(B^*\) also ranges over bases, so the elements \(b_i^*\) range over all nonloops, i.e. all of \(E\) in the simple loopless case.

original_problem:
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?
claim_verified: The proof conditionally constructs the desired order-reversing map for finite matroids assuming the additional unproved assertion that the dagger construction always produces a genuine valuated matroid. What is actually established unconditionally is only the reduction to the simple modular case, the definition of \(p^\dagger\), and verification of certain local 3-term relations and incidence-type conditions. The finite theorem is not proved as written.
solution_verified:
## Claim proved

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid whose lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\). The following is proved.

1. There is a canonical reduction from \(M\) to its simplification \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\):  
   \[
   \operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong \operatorname{Dr}(M),
   \]
   compatibly with the embedded flat lattice. The involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends to one on \(\mathcal L(S)\), and \(\mathcal L(S)\) is modular.

2. In the finite simple loopless modular case, for every valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\), a function \(p^\dagger\) is defined. It is shown unconditionally that:
   - \(p^\dagger\) is nonzero and formally involutive;
   - \(p^\dagger\) sends flat quotients \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) to \(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}\);
   - \(p^\dagger\) satisfies all local short three-term tropical Plücker relations;
   - \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the \(M\)-circuit incidence relations which would imply
     \[
     \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
     \]
     once \(p^\dagger\) is known to be a genuine valuated matroid.

3. Consequently, under the additional hypothesis:

   **Dagger hypothesis.** For every finite simple loopless modular matroid \(N\) with a polarity and every valuated quotient \(p\) of \(N\), the function \(p^\dagger\) defined below is a valuated matroid,

   there is an inclusion-reversing involution
   \[
   \Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\to \operatorname{Dr}(M)
   \]
   extending \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

No unconditional assertion that the dagger hypothesis holds in general is part of this claim.

---

## 1. Preliminaries

Work over \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\) with the minimum convention.

For a rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(p\) on a finite set \(E\), write
\[
p:\binom Ed\to \mathbb T.
\]
The affine tropical linear space of \(p\) is
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in \mathbb T^E:
\forall J\in \binom E{d+1},\
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
For \(d=0\), this is \(\{\infty^E\}\). Projectivization deletes \(\infty^E\) and quotients by tropical scalar translation \(x\sim x+\lambda \mathbf 1\).

A function \(p:\binom Ed\to\mathbb T\), not identically \(\infty\), is a valuated matroid iff its finite support is the set of bases of an ordinary matroid and its finite values satisfy the valuated basis-exchange axiom. Equivalently, \(p\) satisfies the full tropical Plücker relations. The short three-term relations alone are not used here as a valuated-matroid criterion.

For an ordinary matroid \(M\), we regard \(M\) as the valuated matroid with value \(0\) on \(M\)-bases and \(\infty\) otherwise.

We use the following standard facts.

### Fact 1.1: Quotient-containment criterion

Let \(p\) be a rank \(d>0\) valuated matroid on \(E\). Then
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
\]
iff for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\), the minimum
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^p(c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer, where
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
For \(d=0\), containment is automatic.

When this condition holds, \(p\) is called a valuated quotient of \(M\).

### Fact 1.2: Coordinate cocircuits

If \(p\) has rank \(d>0\), then for every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\),
\[
D_A^p\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p).
\]

### Fact 1.3: Affine annihilator duality

If \(\mu\) is a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\), then for \(u\in\mathbb T^N\),
\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
\]
iff
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)
\subseteq
\left\{
v\in \mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

### Fact 1.4: Uniqueness

A valuated matroid is determined by its affine tropical linear space up to adding a global tropical scalar to all Plücker coordinates.

We also need the following elementary description of ordinary Bergman fans.

### Lemma 1.5: Superlevel-flat criterion

Let \(N\) be an ordinary matroid on a finite ground set \(E_N\). For \(w\in\mathbb T^{E_N}\),
\[
w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)
\]
iff, for every real \(\lambda\), the superlevel set
\[
S_\lambda(w)=\{e\in E_N:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

#### Proof

Suppose \(w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). If \(S_\lambda(w)\) is not flat, then there is \(e\notin S_\lambda(w)\) with
\[
e\in \operatorname{cl}_N(S_\lambda(w)).
\]
Hence some circuit \(C\subseteq S_\lambda(w)\cup\{e\}\) contains \(e\). Since \(w_e<\lambda\le w_c\) for \(c\in C\setminus\{e\}\), the minimum of \(w\) on \(C\) is uniquely attained at \(e\), contradiction.

Conversely, suppose all superlevel sets are flats. If some circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\), choose \(\lambda\) strictly between \(w_e\) and the other finite values on \(C\), and larger than \(w_e\) if all other values are \(\infty\). Then
\[
C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq S_\lambda(w),
\qquad
e\notin S_\lambda(w),
\]
but
\[
e\in \operatorname{cl}_N(C\setminus\{e\})
\subseteq
\operatorname{cl}_N(S_\lambda(w)),
\]
contradicting flatness. ∎

---

## 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops of \(M\). Let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes, and let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map. Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\), viewed as a simple loopless matroid on \(\bar E\).

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

If \(\bar q\) is a rank \(d\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), define its zero-parallel extension \(q\) to \(E\) by
\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing
\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
The full tropical Plücker relations for \(q\) are precisely those for \(\bar q\), together with tautological relations involving loops or parallel representatives. Hence \(q\) is again a valuated matroid, and
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]
In particular,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr).
\]

Conversely, let \(q\) be a valuated matroid on \(E\) such that
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
If \(q\) has rank \(0\), then it is the zero-parallel extension of the unique rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Assume \(\operatorname{rk}(q)=d>0\).

For every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\), Fact 1.2 gives
\[
D_A^q\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
Thus \(D_A^q\) satisfies every \(M\)-circuit equation.

First, no finite \(q\)-basis contains an \(M\)-circuit. Indeed, if \(I\) is a finite \(q\)-basis and \(C\subseteq I\) is an \(M\)-circuit, choose \(c\in C\) and set \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then
\[
D_A^q(c)=q_I<\infty,
\]
while \(D_A^q(e)=\infty\) for every \(e\in C\setminus\{c\}\), contradicting the circuit equation for \(C\).

In particular, no finite \(q\)-basis contains a loop of \(M\). If \(e,f\) are parallel non-loops of \(M\), then \(\{e,f\}\) is an \(M\)-circuit, so for every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\),
\[
D_A^q(e)=D_A^q(f).
\]
This implies both that no finite \(q\)-basis contains two elements from the same parallel class and that replacing one representative of a parallel class by another preserves the value of \(q\).

Therefore \(q\) descends to a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\): for \(\bar I\in\binom{\bar E}{d}\), choose any representative set \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\), and set
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]
This is independent of representatives, and the Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) follow from those of \(q\). Moreover \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\).

Since
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr)
\subseteq
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr),
\]
and \(\Delta\) is injective on \(\mathbb T^{\bar E}\), we get
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S).
\]

Hence \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

Now compare flats. A subset \(F\subseteq E\) is a flat of \(M\) iff
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F)
\]
for a unique flat \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\). Thus
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism.

For a flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). The matroid
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
has rank function
\[
r(A)=r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F).
\]
Since
\[
r_M(A)=r_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)),
\]
this matroid is precisely the zero-parallel extension of
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Therefore \(\Delta_*\) identifies the flat object indexed by \(\bar F\) in \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\) with the flat object indexed by \(F\) in \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

The order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends to one on \(\mathcal L(S)\) by
\[
\bar F^\perp
=
\psi\bigl(\psi^{-1}(\bar F)^\perp\bigr).
\]

---

## 3. A polarity forces modularity after simplification

Let \(L\) be a finite geometric lattice of rank \(r\), with rank function \(\rho\), and suppose \(L\) has an order-reversing involution \(X\mapsto X^\perp\).

Since \(X\mapsto X^\perp\) is an anti-automorphism, it identifies the interval \([\hat 0,X]\) with \([X^\perp,\hat 1]\). Hence
\[
\rho(X^\perp)=r-\rho(X).
\]

Geometric lattices are semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Applying this to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\), and using
\[
X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp=(X\vee Y)^\perp,
\qquad
X^\perp\vee Y^\perp=(X\wedge Y)^\perp,
\]
gives
\[
r-\rho(X)+r-\rho(Y)
\ge
r-\rho(X\vee Y)+r-\rho(X\wedge Y).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\le
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Together with semimodularity, equality holds for all \(X,Y\). Therefore \(L\) is modular.

Thus, after passing to the simplification, the relevant case is:

- \(M\) is finite, simple, and loopless;
- \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular;
- \(M\) has rank \(r\);
- \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is a fixed order-reversing involution of \(\mathcal L(M)\).

---

## 4. The dagger construction in the simple modular case

Assume now that \(M\) is finite, simple, loopless, modular of rank \(r\), and equipped with a polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\):
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]

### Lemma 4.1: Flat constancy

Assume \(k>0\). For every rank \(k\) flat \(F\) of \(M\), the value \(p_I\) is independent of the \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(F\). Denote this common value by
\[
\bar p(F).
\]

#### Proof

Every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent. Indeed, if a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\) contained an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), choose \(c\in C\) and put \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then
\[
D_A^p(c)=p_I<\infty,
\]
while \(D_A^p(e)=\infty\) for all \(e\in C\setminus\{c\}\), contradicting the quotient-containment criterion.

Now suppose
\[
I=A\cup\{a\},
\qquad
I'=A\cup\{b\}
\]
are \(M\)-bases of the same rank \(k\) flat. Then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying the quotient-containment criterion to \(A\) and \(C\), the only possibly finite terms are
\[
p_{A\cup a}
\quad\text{and}\quad
p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Therefore they are equal in \(\mathbb T\).

The basis graph of the restriction \(M|F\) is connected, so any two \(M\)-bases of \(F\) are connected by such exchanges. Hence \(p_I\) is constant over all \(M\)-bases of \(F\). ∎

### Lemma 4.2: Finite \(p\)-bases inside \(M\)-bases

Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis.

#### Proof

Choose one finite \(p\)-basis \(I\). By Lemma 4.1, \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, so it extends to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\).

Let \(B\) be any \(M\)-basis. Since the basis graph of \(M\) is connected, it suffices to pass across one exchange
\[
B''=B'\setminus\{b\}\cup\{e\}.
\]
Assume \(B'\) contains a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\). If \(b\notin I\), then \(I\subseteq B''\). If \(b\in I\), set
\[
A=I\setminus\{b\}.
\]
Let \(C\subseteq B'\cup\{e\}\) be the fundamental circuit containing \(b\). Because \(B''\) is a basis, this circuit also contains \(e\).

Since
\[
D_A^p(b)=p_I<\infty,
\]
the circuit equation for \(C\) forces another finite term
\[
D_A^p(c)=p_{A\cup c}<\infty
\]
with
\[
c\in C\setminus(A\cup\{b\}).
\]
Then \(A\cup\{c\}\subseteq B''\) is a finite \(p\)-basis. ∎

We now define \(p^\dagger\).

If \(k=0\), then \(p\) has a single coordinate \(p_\varnothing\). Define \(p^\dagger\) to be the ordinary valuated matroid \(M\), shifted by \(p_\varnothing\).

If \(k=r\), then Lemmas 4.1 and 4.2 imply that \(p\) is a scalar shift of \(M\). If its finite basis value is \(c\), define \(p^\dagger\) to be the rank-zero valuated matroid with coordinate \(c\).

Assume now
\[
0<k<r,
\qquad
n=r-k.
\]
For \(J\in\binom En\), define
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),
&J\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,
&J\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

At this stage \(p^\dagger\) is only a function \(\binom En\to\mathbb T\). Its valuated-matroid status is exactly the dagger hypothesis.

The function \(p^\dagger\) is nonzero. Indeed, choose a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), let
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I),
\]
and choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

Also, \(p^\dagger\) is formally involutive: applying the same formula again gives \(p\). Indeed, if \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, then both \(p_I\) and the formal \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I\) are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent and \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I,
\]
where \(J\) is any \(M\)-basis of \(F^\perp\).

---

## 5. Local short three-term relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the local short three-term tropical Plücker relations. This does not imply that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid; it is only one verified local property.

### Lemma 5.1: Rank-three edge lemma

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let
\[
w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]
For lines \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) of \(N\), define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),&L_i\neq L_j,\\
\infty,&L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\,
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\,
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If two lines coincide, say \(L_1=L_2\), then \(\langle12\rangle=\infty\), while
\[
\langle13\rangle=\langle23\rangle,
\qquad
\langle14\rangle=\langle24\rangle.
\]
Thus the second and third matching sums are equal.

Assume the \(L_i\) are distinct. If \(w=\infty^E\), all relevant quantities are \(\infty\), and there is nothing to prove. Otherwise subtract a scalar so that \(\min w=0\).

By Lemma 1.5, the positive superlevel sets of \(w\) are nested proper flats of the rank-three matroid \(N\). Hence they are contained in a point-line flag
\[
a\le B.
\]
Thus there exist \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\) such that
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}.
\]

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For \(i\neq j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
Thus an edge \(\{i,j\}\) has positive cost only when the two colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree. Among four colored objects, the minimum perfect matching cost is never unique: the color multiplicities are one of
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4,
\]
and in each case at least two perfect matchings attain the minimum.

Now suppose \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
Let
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,&y=a,\\
\beta,&y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are distinct, at least two of \(c(y_2),c(y_3),c(y_4)\) equal the minimum value. If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of that equal pair tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three matching costs are equal. ∎

### Lemma 5.2: Local four-hyperplane lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
[12]+[34],\,
[13]+[24],\,
[14]+[23]
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If two hyperplanes coincide, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Thus the second and third matching sums are equal.

Assume now that the \(H_i\) are distinct. Since \(M\) is modular, two distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\).

Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting a flat with a hyperplane in a modular lattice lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\rho(Z)\ge k-2.
\]
Also \(Z\le H_i\wedge H_j\), and each \(H_i\wedge H_j\) has rank \(k\), so
\[
\rho(Z)\le k.
\]

If \(\rho(Z)=k\), then every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(Z\), so all three matching sums are equal.

Suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
Then \(\rho(T_i)=k-1\). Indeed, \(\rho(T_i)\ge k-1\), and if \(\rho(T_i)=k\), then intersecting \(T_i\) with the remaining hyperplane \(H_i\) would force \(\rho(Z)\ge k-1\), contradiction.

Moreover
\[
T_i\wedge T_j=Z.
\]
If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then \(T_m,T_n\le H_i\wedge H_j\), and modularity gives
\[
\rho(T_m\vee T_n)
=
(k-1)+(k-1)-(k-2)
=
k.
\]
Since \(H_i\wedge H_j\) also has rank \(k\), we have
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]

Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose
\[
t_i\in T_i\setminus Z.
\]
Then \(A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}\) is an \(M\)-basis of \(H_i\wedge H_j\). The short three-term Plücker relation for the valuated matroid \(p\), applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4,
\]
is exactly
\[
\min\bigl(
[12]+[34],\,
[13]+[24],\,
[14]+[23]
\bigr),
\]
so the desired condition holds.

It remains to treat \(\rho(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\), after simplification, is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid \(N\). Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]

We claim that
\[
w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]
Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(N\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each point \(P\in D\), choose
\[
e_P\in P\setminus Z.
\]
By the standard circuit lifting property for contractions, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
such that
\[
C\setminus Z=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
\]
Applying the quotient-containment criterion to \(A\) and \(C\), the elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}
=
\bar p(P)
=
w(P).
\]
Thus the minimum of \(w\) on \(D\) has no unique finite minimizer. Hence
\[
w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]

Each \(H_i\) descends to a line \(L_i=H_i/Z\) of \(N\), and \(H_i\wedge H_j\) descends to \(L_i\wedge L_j\). Lemma 5.1 therefore gives the desired conclusion. ∎

### Proposition 5.3: Local short three-term relations for \(p^\dagger\)

Assume \(0<k<r\), and let \(n=r-k\). For every
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2}
\]
and every four distinct elements \(a,b,c,d\in E\setminus S\), the minimum
\[
\min\bigl(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},\,
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},\,
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then all three sums are \(\infty\). Assume \(S\) is independent and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every matching sum has an \(\infty\)-summand, so the relation is automatic.

Assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\). Set
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(\rho(X)=n-2\), we have
\[
\rho(Y)=k+2.
\]
For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\).

For distinct \(e,f\), the set \(S\cup\{e,f\}\) is \(M\)-dependent iff
\[
X\vee e=X\vee f,
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
H_e=H_f.
\]
If \(S\cup\{e,f\}\) is independent, then
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\bar p\bigl((X\vee e\vee f)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]
Therefore the desired three-term relation is precisely Lemma 5.2 applied to
\[
H_a,H_b,H_c,H_d.
\]
∎

---

## 6. Incidence relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We next prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the \(M\)-circuit incidence relations.

We use two elementary facts about contractions.

### Lemma 6.1: Circuit lifting for contractions

Let \(X\) be a flat of \(M\).

1. If \(D\) is a circuit of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\), and \(e_P\) is a representative in the corresponding parallel class for each \(P\in D\), then there exists an \(M\)-circuit
   \[
   C\subseteq X\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
   \]
   such that
   \[
   C\setminus X=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
   \]

2. If \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit not contained in \(X\), then every \(e\in C\setminus X\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\). Equivalently, the parallel class of \(e\) lies in a circuit of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) contained in the set of parallel classes met by \(C\setminus X\).

#### Proof

For (1), the chosen representative set is dependent in \(M/X\), and no proper subset meeting fewer simplification classes is dependent, by minimality of \(D\). Hence it is a circuit of \(M/X\). Circuits of a contraction are precisely minimal nonempty sets of the form \(C'\setminus X\), where \(C'\) is an \(M\)-circuit not contained in \(X\). This gives the desired \(M\)-circuit.

For (2), let \(e\in C\setminus X\). Since \(C\) is a circuit,
\[
e\in \operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus\{e\})
\subseteq
\operatorname{cl}_M\bigl(X\cup(C\setminus X\setminus\{e\})\bigr).
\]
Thus \(e\) is dependent on \(C\setminus X\setminus\{e\}\) in \(M/X\). A minimal dependent subset gives a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\) and containing \(e\). ∎

Intervals of a modular lattice are modular, and simplification preserves the lattice of flats. Thus, for every flat \(X\), the simplification \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) is again a finite simple modular matroid.

### Lemma 6.2: Codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a rank \(n-1\) flat of \(M\). Let
\[
N=\operatorname{si}(M/X).
\]
The points of \(N\) are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]

#### Proof

By Lemma 1.5, it suffices to show that every real superlevel set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

First we show \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed. Let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be another point on the line spanned by \(P_1,P_2\) in \(N\). Put
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\qquad
H_i=P_i^\perp.
\]
Then \(Y\) has rank \(k+1\), and the \(H_i\) are hyperplanes of \(Y\). Because \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\),
\[
H_3=P_3^\perp
\supseteq
P_1^\perp\wedge P_2^\perp
=
H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Let
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(\rho(K)=k-1\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose
\[
e_i\in H_i\setminus K.
\]
In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three atoms \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit after simplification. By Lemma 6.1, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq K\cup\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}
\]
with
\[
D\setminus K=\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}.
\]
Applying the quotient-containment criterion to \(B\) and \(D\), elements of \(D\cap K\) contribute \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{B\cup e_i}
=
\bar p(H_i)
=
u(P_i).
\]
Thus
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Therefore \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed.

Now, in a finite simple modular matroid, every line-closed set of points is a flat. Let \(\mathcal S\) be line-closed, and let
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}(\mathcal S).
\]
Choose an independent spanning set
\[
b_1,\dots,b_m\in\mathcal S
\]
for \(F\). We prove by induction on \(m\) that every point of \(F\) lies in \(\mathcal S\). The cases \(m\le 2\) are exactly line-closedness.

Assume \(m\ge 3\). Put
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}(b_1,\dots,b_{m-1}).
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(\mathcal S\). Let \(p\) be a point of \(F\). If \(p\le G\) or \(p=b_m\), then \(p\in\mathcal S\). Otherwise, consider the line
\[
\ell=p\vee b_m.
\]
Since \(G\vee \ell=F\), modularity gives
\[
\rho(\ell\wedge G)
=
\rho(\ell)+\rho(G)-\rho(F)
=
2+(m-1)-m
=
1.
\]
Thus \(\ell\wedge G\) is a point \(q\in G\). Since \(q,b_m\in\mathcal S\), line-closedness implies that every point of the line \(q\vee b_m=\ell\), including \(p\), lies in \(\mathcal S\).

Therefore every superlevel set \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is a flat, so Lemma 1.5 gives
\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]
∎

### Proposition 6.3: Incidence relations for \(p^\dagger\)

Assume \(0<k<r\), and let \(n=r-k\). For every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\),
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup\{c\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Assume \(A\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\rho(X)=n-1\). For \(c\in X\),
\[
D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)=\infty.
\]
For \(c\notin X\), the flat
\[
P=X\vee c
\]
is a point of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\), and
\[
D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)
=
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
By Lemma 6.2, the corresponding function on \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) lies in its Bergman fan.

If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise, suppose the minimum on \(C\) has a unique finite minimizer \(e\). Then \(e\notin X\). By Lemma 6.1, the parallel class of \(e\) lies in a circuit of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) contained in the classes met by \(C\setminus X\). On that circuit, the induced function has a unique finite minimum at the class of \(e\), contradicting Lemma 6.2.

Thus the incidence relation holds for every \(C,A\). ∎

Therefore, if the dagger hypothesis holds so that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid, Proposition 6.3 and Fact 1.1 imply
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]

---

## 7. Flat quotients

For a flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), let \(p^F\) denote the ordinary valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Thus \(p^F\) has rank \(r-\rho(F)\).

A finite basis of \(p^F\) is an \(M\)-basis of a rank \(r-\rho(F)\) flat \(G\) complementary to \(F\), meaning
\[
G\wedge F=\hat 0,
\qquad
G\vee F=E.
\]
Indeed, \(G\vee F=E\) says that \(G\) spans \(M/F\), and modularity gives
\[
\rho(G\wedge F)
=
\rho(G)+\rho(F)-\rho(G\vee F)
=
(r-\rho(F))+\rho(F)-r
=
0.
\]

Let \(f=\rho(F)\). The dagger \((p^F)^\dagger\) has rank \(f\). For a rank \(f\) flat \(H\),
\[
(p^F)^\dagger
\]
is finite on the \(M\)-bases of \(H\) iff \(H^\perp\) is complementary to \(F\). Applying the polarity, this is equivalent to \(H\) being complementary to \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

Thus the dagger construction sends the flat object indexed by \(F\) to the flat object indexed by \(F^\perp\).

---

## 8. Conditional construction of the order-reversing involution

Assume throughout this section that \(M\) is finite, simple, loopless, modular, and satisfies the dagger hypothesis.

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
b_i^*
=
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Since \(M\) is simple and \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is a single element of \(E\).

For \(S\subseteq [r]\), write
\[
B_S=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]

### Lemma 8.1: The basis-star involution

For every \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B_S)^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
In particular,
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis, and the operation \(B\mapsto B^*\) is involutive.

#### Proof

First,
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B_S)
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\}).
\]
The inclusion “\(\le\)” is clear. Conversely, if an element \(e\) is not in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B_S)\), then either \(e=b_j\) for some \(j\notin S\), in which case \(e\notin \operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_j\})\), or else the fundamental circuit of \(e\) with respect to \(B\) uses some \(b_j\) with \(j\notin S\), again implying
\[
e\notin \operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_j\}).
\]
Thus the equality holds.

Applying the polarity gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B_S)^\perp
=
\bigvee_{i\notin S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]

Taking \(S=\varnothing\), we get
\[
E=\operatorname{cl}_M(B^*),
\]
so \(B^*\) spans. Since \(B^*\) has \(r\) elements in a rank \(r\) simple matroid, it is an \(M\)-basis.

Taking \(S=\{i\}\), we get
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(b_i)^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M(B^*\setminus\{b_i^*\}).
\]
Applying \(\perp\) again gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B^*\setminus\{b_i^*\})^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M(b_i).
\]
Since \(M\) is simple, the star of \(b_i^*\) relative to \(B^*\) is \(b_i\). Hence \(B\mapsto B^*\) is involutive. ∎

For \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x
=
\left\{
y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M):
\forall M\text{-bases }B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},\
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

### Proposition 8.2: Point-annihilator description

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Under the dagger hypothesis,
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x
\]
for every \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

#### Proof

First assume \(0<k<r\), and set \(n=r-k\).

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
(\mu_B)_T=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Lemma 4.2 ensures that \(\mu_B\) is not identically \(\infty\).

For \(R\subseteq [r]\) with \(|R|=n\), Lemma 8.1 gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M(B_{[r]\setminus R})^\perp.
\]
Therefore
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(B_{[r]\setminus R})\bigr)
=
p_{B_{[r]\setminus R}}
=
(\mu_B)_{[r]\setminus R}.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

Suppose
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\]
and
\[
y\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p).
\]
Then
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B).
\]
By affine annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\),
\[
y\in \widetilde H_x.
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\). We prove
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(n+1\), and put
\[
S=[r]\setminus T,
\qquad |S|=k-1.
\]
Let
\[
A=B_S.
\]
By Fact 1.2,
\[
D_A^p\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p),
\]
so \(D_A^p\in\widetilde H_x\). Testing the defining condition of \(\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i\in T}
\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. But
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T\setminus\{i\}}
=
(\mu_B)_{S\cup\{i\}}
=
p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
Thus this is precisely the circuit equation for
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Because \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on the set of \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts correctly to every \(M\)-basis.

It remains to check the global circuit equations for \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
J\in\binom E{n+1}.
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(C\). Since \(x|_C\) lies in the tropical linear space of \(p^\dagger|_C\), the circuit equation for \(J\) holds.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\rho_M(J)<n\), then every \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Finally, suppose \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\rho_M(J)=n\). Then \(J\) has nullity \(1\), so it contains a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). For \(j\notin C\), the set \(J\setminus\{j\}\) remains dependent. For \(j\in C\), the set \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is independent and has closure \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\), so
\[
p^\dagger_{J\setminus\{j\}}
\]
is independent of \(j\in C\). If this common value is \(\infty\), the circuit equation is automatic. Otherwise the equation reduces to the ordinary \(M\)-circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in C}x_j
\]
for \(x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Hence
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]

The boundary cases are straightforward.

If \(k=0\), then
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\},
\]
and \(p^\dagger\) is \(M\), shifted by a scalar. For \(x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), both sides of the desired equivalence hold.

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar shift of \(M\), while \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Hence
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then clearly
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) and an index \(i\). Let
\[
A=B\setminus\{b_i\}.
\]
Then \(D_A^p\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Testing \(D_A^p\in \widetilde H_x\) on \(B\), the only possibly finite term is
\[
p_B+x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Therefore this term cannot be finite, so
\[
x_{b_i^*}=\infty.
\]
As \(B\) varies, the elements \(b_i^*\) range over all of \(E\), because \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on the set of bases. Thus \(x=\infty^E\). ∎

We can now construct the involution.

For an affine tropical linear space
\[
X=\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M),
\]
define
\[
\widetilde\Phi(X)
=
\{x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M):X\subseteq \widetilde H_x\}.
\]
By Proposition 8.2,
\[
\widetilde\Phi\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\bigr)
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\widetilde\Phi(X)\) is again an affine tropical linear space contained in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

If
\[
X\subseteq Y,
\]
then
\[
Y\subseteq \widetilde H_x
\implies
X\subseteq \widetilde H_x,
\]
so
\[
\widetilde\Phi(Y)\subseteq \widetilde\Phi(X).
\]
Thus \(\widetilde\Phi\) reverses inclusion.

By formal involutivity of the dagger construction,
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]

Finally, Section 7 shows that for every flat \(F\),
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde\Phi\!\left(
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
\right)
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

All constructions commute with tropical scalar translation, so \(\widetilde\Phi\) descends to an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\to\operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
extending the given polarity on flats.

---

## 9. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) be an arbitrary finite matroid with a polarity on \(\mathcal L(M)\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(M)=0\), then projectively \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) is empty and \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) has a single object; the identity is the required involution.

Assume \(\operatorname{rk}(M)>0\). Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M).
\]
By Section 2,
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
is a poset isomorphism compatible with the embedded flat quotients. The polarity on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends through
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
to a polarity on \(\mathcal L(S)\). By Section 3, \(\mathcal L(S)\) is modular.

If the dagger hypothesis holds for \(S\), Section 8 gives an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S)
\]
extending the descended polarity. Define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

For a flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), set
\[
\bar F=\psi(F).
\]
Compatibility of \(\Delta_*\) with flat quotients gives
\[
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]
The descended polarity sends \(\bar F\) to \(\psi(F^\perp)\). Therefore
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Thus, for finite \(M\), the desired order-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) is obtained conditionally from the dagger hypothesis, while the reduction to the simple modular case and the stated local and incidence properties of \(p^\dagger\) hold unconditionally.

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Patch for Facts 1.1–1.4.** Insert the standard covector theorem: for a rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(p\), the affine tropical linear space \(L(p)\) equals the tropical convex span/submodule generated by its coordinate cocircuits \(D_A^p\), \(|A|=d-1\). This is a standard Murota–Tamura/Dress–Wenzel valuated-matroid fact; modern references state that tropical linear spaces are spanned by valuated cocircuits and that valuated matroid quotients are equivalent to inclusions of projective tropical linear spaces. ([pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12031988/?utm_source=openai))

   Then Fact 1.1 follows as follows. For each ordinary \(M\)-circuit \(C\), let
   \[
   H_C=\{x:\min_{c\in C}x_c\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
   \]
   Each \(H_C\) is tropically convex: if a tropical linear combination of two vectors in \(H_C\) had a unique finite minimum at \(e\in C\), whichever summand attained that value at \(e\) would itself have a unique finite minimum on \(C\). Since
   \[
   L(M)=\bigcap_{C\in\mathcal C(M)}H_C,
   \]
   containment \(L(p)\subseteq L(M)\) is equivalent, by the cocircuit-span theorem, to every \(D_A^p\) lying in every \(H_C\), exactly the incidence condition in Fact 1.1. Fact 1.2 is the same covector theorem. Fact 1.3 follows because circuits of \(\mu^*\) are cocircuits of \(\mu\), so \(u\in L(\mu^*)\) iff \(u\) is tropically orthogonal to every cocircuit generator of \(L(\mu)\), equivalently to all of \(L(\mu)\). Fact 1.4 can either be cited as the standard uniqueness of a valuated matroid from its covector/tropical-linear-space module, or removed if unused.

2. **Patch for Section 2.** Add the explicit verification: the zero-parallel extension \(q\) has finite bases exactly the representative sets of finite \(\bar q\)-bases with no loops and no repeated parallel class. Any tropical Plücker relation for \(q\) either projects to the corresponding relation for \(\bar q\), or contains a loop/repeated parallel class and has all finite terms paired with equal values, hence is tautological. For tropical linear spaces, equations involving loops force loop coordinates to be \(\infty\), and equations involving two representatives of a nonloop parallel class force those coordinates to be equal; after imposing these conditions, the remaining equations are precisely those of \(\bar q\). Thus
   \[
   \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)=\Delta(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)).
   \]

3. **Patch for Proposition 8.2.** State explicitly that if \(B\) is an \(M\)-basis and \(B\) contains a finite \(p\)-basis, then
   \[
   \mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}}
   \]
   is the deletion/restriction of \(p\) to \(B\), hence is a rank \(k\) valuated matroid. If \(y\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\), then \(y|_B\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B)\) because all defining circuit equations for \(\mu_B\) are defining equations for \(p\) restricted to subsets of \(B\). The same applies to \(p^\dagger|_{B^*}\). Finally, note explicitly that adding a tropical scalar to \(x\), \(y\), \(p\), or \(p^\dagger\) does not affect any “no unique finite minimizer” condition, so the affine construction descends to projective tropical space.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
